## FAILURE MODE LIBRARY — FIELD-VERIFIED CAUSES AND FIXES

This section documents real failure patterns encountered in Phoenix Controls field service. Use this when textbook diagnostics don't match what you're seeing.

topic: Failure Mode Library — Field-Verified Causes and Fixes
content:
## FAILURE MODE LIBRARY — FIELD-VERIFIED CAUSES AND FIXES

This section documents real failure patterns encountered in Phoenix Controls field service. Use this when textbook diagnostics don't match what you're seeing.

### "New board commissioned correctly but flow alarm fires immediately"
**Cause 90% of the time**: Parameter 1 wrong mode. The board thinks it's a different application type. A VAV board (vAvv) installed on a CV application will alarm because it expects sash feedback it isn't getting. A CV board (cvn) on a VAV application will never track sash position.
**Fix**: Re-enter calibration. Set Parameter 1 first. Re-calibrate all parameters.

### "Valve tracks correctly at mid-range but wrong at extremes"
**Cause**: Parameters 10 and 11 (sash sensor calibration) captured at wrong sash positions, OR sash physically doesn't reach fully open/closed during calibration.
**Fix**: Verify sash physically reaches end stops. Re-capture Parameters 10 and 11 with sash held firmly at each extreme. The delta between P10 and P11 should span the full signal range — if it doesn't, the sensor or cable has a problem.

### "System commissioned and working, then fails 2 weeks later with Unlinked"
**Cause**: MS/TP termination resistor installed incorrectly (middle of trunk, not at physical end) — system works initially because impedance is borderline, then fails as temperature or humidity changes shift cable impedance.
**Fix**: Walk the entire MS/TP trunk physically. Remove any termination not at the physical ends. Verify 120Ω at each true end with cable disconnected from devices.

### "Face velocity correct at setpoint but drifts low over the day"
**Cause**: Duct static pressure dropping as AHU filters load up (increasing restriction). The valve cannot maintain flow below minimum duct static.
**Fix**: Check duct static at beginning and end of day. If it's dropping below 0.3" WC, AHU filters need service. Coordinate with facilities. This is not a valve or controller problem.

### "ACM just replaced, now two other ACMs are offline"
**Cause**: Replacement ACM installed with same MAC address as an existing ACM (factory default or tech error). Three-way MAC conflict on trunk.
**Fix**: Power down the replacement ACM. Verify its SW1 DIP address in the off state. Set to a unique address. Reconnect. Original ACMs should recover within 30 seconds.

### "FHM shows correct CFM but BMS shows 0"
**Cause**: BMS proxy point is bound to the wrong BACnet object, or proxy point Enabled = false in Niagara, or device instance mismatch after board replacement (new board has default device instance, not the original).
**Fix**: Verify BACnet device instance on replacement board matches original (set in Workbench). Verify Niagara proxy point Enabled = true. Force Read on proxy point to confirm communication.

### "Celeris valve hunts but only when sash is between 30–70% open"
**Cause**: Sash sensor has a dead zone or nonlinearity in mid-range — loose wiring, damaged potentiometer track, or partial connector corrosion causing signal dropout in the middle of travel.
**Fix**: Monitor sash sensor voltage or resistance while slowly moving sash through full range. Look for any discontinuity or jump. Replace sensor if non-linear.

### "Pneumatic valve works fine cold but hunts when building warms up"
**Cause**: Pneumatic tubing has a small kink that becomes more restrictive as it warms (tubing softens and kink tightens). Or: I/P transducer orifice partially clogged — restricts flow at low signal when thermal expansion changes orifice geometry.
**Fix**: Inspect all tubing for kinks, especially near support brackets where tubing can press against metal. Replace any kinked section. Test I/P transducer by bypassing it temporarily with a known-good unit.

### "N4 poll scheduler was fine for months, now suddenly at 85%"
**Cause**: An IT network change (VLAN segmentation, switch replacement, firewall rule addition) broke COV subscriptions. All previously COV-subscribed points reverted to polling, multiplying poll load 3–5x.
**Fix**: Restart BacnetNetwork in N4 (disable/enable). This re-establishes all COV subscriptions. If scheduler stays high, check BBMD configuration — VLAN change may have broken broadcast forwarding.

topic: PBC Datasheet — Overview, Applications, LED Status (MKT-0511 Rev.C 11/2024)
content:
PROGRAMMABLE BACNET CONTROLLER (PBC) — MKT-0511 Rev.C 11/2024

The PBC can be mounted on a Phoenix Controls Venturi Valve or in a control panel and serves as the zone controller for offset control, comfort control, emergency control, dynamic pressurization control, and additional programmability.

FUNCTIONS:
- GEN (Generic Application) — standalone programmable logic and I/O module
- ZBH (Zone Balance High-Speed) — zone controller for high-speed valves (<1 sec response); handles up to 20 ACMs and 10 FHDs
- ZBL (Zone Balance Standard) — zone controller for up to four standard speed valves (<40–90 sec response)

SERIES:
- PBC 500 = BACnet MS/TP with BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)
- PBC 501 = BACnet MS/TP without BLE
- PBC 504 = BACnet IP with BLE
- PBC 505 = BACnet IP without BLE

PLATFORM: Crossover processor NXP I.MRT Cortex M7; 16 MB SDRAM; 16 MB QSPI Flash; Real-time clock (24 hour backup after power failure)
OPERATING SYSTEM: Niagara N4.9u1 or later
POWER: 24 VAC, 50/60 Hz @ 50 VA max; 8 VA nominal
DIMENSIONS: 8.5" x 4.79" x 2.1" (216 x 121.6 x 53 mm); Weight: 1.256 lbs (570 g)
ENCLOSURE: IP20, NEMA 1
MOUNTING: Venturi valve control enclosure, DIN rail (DIN43880, 35mm rail), or surface mount with screws
INSTALLATION GUIDE: 651-320-001

COMMUNICATIONS:
- BACnet MS/TP: RS485, baud rates 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 76800 bps
- BACnet IP: Two RJ45 Ethernet ports; DHCP or static IP
- Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE): Connects to Phoenix Controls Flow Manager Mobile App (Android/iOS)

LED STATUS INDICATORS:
- Small LEDs (green): Communication signal transmission or reception
- Large LEDs: Device status — green (normal), yellow (warning), red (fault)

UNIVERSAL I/Os:
- 16-bit A/D resolution
- UI: 0–10V or 4–20 mA; temperature sensors: 10K NTC Type II/III, 20K NTC, PT100, PT1000, NI1000, PT3000, 100Ω–100kΩ resistive
- AO: Voltage output 0–11V or 4–20mA current
- DO: Dry contact binary output
- SSR: 24VAC/VDC max, 1.5A constant, 3.5A inrush
- Relays: 3 contacts (NO, NC, Common); 10A constant; up to 277VAC; 4 relays; total 12A if commons jumpered

ENVIRONMENTAL:
- Operating: 32–122°F (0–50°C); 5–95% RH non-condensing
- Storage: -40–150°F (-40–66°C)
- Altitude: to 6562 ft (2000 m)

REGULATORY: CE, FCC, ICES, UL/cUL, RoHS3, REACH, Prop 65

APPLICATIONS:
- Research labs: Zone balance, offset control, 1-second response for fume hood containment
- Life Sciences/Vivariums: Zone balance, comfort, humidity, air change diversity
- High-Purity Manufacturing: Precision pressure control, ISO class monitoring
- Healthcare (OR, ICU, pharmacy): Offset control, temperature, occupancy, pandemic modes

topic: PBC Complete Pin/Terminal Reference — All I/O Terminals (MKT-0511/MKT-0520)
content:
PBC TERMINAL/PINOUT REFERENCE — MKT-0520 Guide Specification Rev.A 02/2024

POWER TERMINALS:
- Pin 1: Building earth ground
- Pin 2 (VO): 24 VAC/VDC power input
- Pin 3: 24 VAC/VDC power input
- Pin 48: 24 VAC power input voltage
- Pin 49 (VO): 24 VAC power input voltage

DO RELAY TERMINALS (Pins 4–15):
- NC1/NO1/IN1 = Relay 1 (normally closed, normally open, input power)
- NC2/NO2/IN2 = Relay 2
- NC3/NO3/IN3 = Relay 3
- NC4/NO4/IN4 = Relay 4
Note: Up to 277VAC; 10A constant on NO contact; total 12A if commons jumpered

DO SSR TERMINALS (Pins 16–23):
- Pin 16 (AUX OUT): 24VAC/VDC auxiliary output
- Pin 17 (SRIN): 24VAC/VDC AUX input via factory jumper
- Pins 18, 20, 21, 23: SR1, SR2, SR3, SR4 SSR outputs
- Pins 19, 22: C (Common)

UIO TERMINALS — GROUP 1 (Pins 52–72):
- IO1 through IO12: Universal inputs/outputs 1–12
- C: Common between IO pairs
- 24VDC OUT: Auxiliary power output pins 65, 72

UIO TERMINALS — GROUP 2 (Pins 32–38):
- IO13 through IO16: Universal inputs/outputs 13–16
- C: Common
- 24VDC OUT: Auxiliary power output (pin 38)

BACNET MS/TP TERMINALS:
- Pin 42 (+): BACnet MS/TP Positive
- Pin 43 (-): BACnet MS/TP Negative
- Pin 44 (SHLD): BACnet MS/TP Shield

RS485 TERMINALS:
- Pin 45 (+): RS485 Modbus positive
- Pin 46 (-): RS485 Modbus negative
- Pin 47 (COM): Shield termination

SYLK BUS (Wall Module):
- Pin 50 (WM1): Sylk Bus
- Pin 51 (WM2): Sylk Bus
Note: Hardwired wall modules (space temp, setpoint, fan speed override, occupancy override, LED control) connect via SYLK bus

topic: PBC BACnet Integration Points — Complete Object Reference (MKT-0520)
content:
PBC BACNET INTEGRATION POINTS — MKT-0520 Rev.A 02/2024

KEY ANALOG INPUTS (AI) — Read Only:
AI 1: LabTotalExhRet — Total lab exhaust+return CFM
AI 2: LabTotalSupply — Total lab supply CFM
AI 3: LabTotalGexFlow — Total general exhaust CFM
AI 4: LabTotalMavFlow — Total make-up air valve CFM
AI 5: LabTotalHoodFlow — Total fume hood exhaust CFM
AI 6: LabTotalReturnFlow — Total return air CFM
AI 7: LabTotalBypassFlow — Total bypass CFM
AI 8: LabZoneFlowOffset — Current zone flow offset CFM
AI 9: LabZoneEffFlowOffsetSP — Effective flow offset setpoint CFM
AI 10: ACH — Air changes per hour
AI 30–35: LoSEA zone totals (exhaust, return, offset, supply, exhaust+return, effective offset)
AI 50–65: Individual valve data (ValveEffFlowCmd1–4, Valveflow1–4, ValveVpot1–4, ValveAlarmStatusBits1–4)
AI 101–132: UIO1–IO16 scaled and raw values
AI 200: Space_Temp (°F)
AI 201: EffCoolTempSP, AI 202: EffHeatTempSP
AI 203: CoolingDemand (%), AI 204: HeatingDemand (%)
AI 220: RoomHumidity (%), AI 221: HumidifyDemand, AI 222: DehumidifyDemand

KEY ANALOG OUTPUTS (AO) — Read/Write:
AO 1: LabZoneFlowOffsetCmd (CFM)
AO 2: LoSEAZoneFlowOffsetCmd (CFM) — primary offset setpoint for supervisory writes
AO 3: ACHSetpoint
AO 200: OccCoolSetpoint, AO 201: OccHeatSetpoint
AO 202: UnoccCoolSetpoint, AO 203: UnoccHeatSetpoint
AO 204: StndbyCoolSetpoint, AO 205: StndbyHeatSetpoint

KEY ANALOG VALUES (AV):
AV 50–53: ValveFlowCmd1–4 (CFM) — individual valve flow commands
AV 200–203: MinVentOccSP, MinVentStandbySP, MinVentUnoccSP, MinVentVacantSP

KEY BINARY INPUTS (BI):
BI 50: ValveJamAlarm1, BI 51: ValveFlowAlarm1
BI 52–57: Jam and flow alarms for valves 2–4

KEY BINARY VALUES (BV):
BV 50: ValveFlowOvridEnable, BV 52: UIOSignalOvridEnable

MULTI-STATE OUTPUTS (MSO):
MSO 1: EModeCmd — Emergency mode command
MSO 2: OccupancyCmd — Occupancy mode command

MULTI-STATE VALUES (MSV):
MSV 1: EffEmMode — Effective emergency mode
MSV 2: EffOccMode — Effective occupancy mode
MSV 3: LabZoneCtrlState, MSV 4: LoSEAZoneCtrlState
MSV 50–53: ValveState1–4 (Disable/Opening/Closing/AppOverride)
MSV 200: TempCtrlMode

topic: FHD500 Fume Hood Display — Complete Spec, Terminals, Displays, Ordering (MKT-0510 Rev.A 02/2024)
content:
FUME HOOD DISPLAY 500 SERIES (FHD500) — MKT-0510 Rev.A 02/2024

OVERVIEW:
Color touch-screen display for fume hoods, used with Phoenix Controls Venturi valves, sash sensors, and Zone Presence Sensors. Works with ACM as part of CSCP. Supports CV, two-position, drive, and VAV applications.

POWER: 24 VAC (±10%), 50/60Hz, Class 2 Transformer ONLY; 360mA max (8.68 VA) @ 24VAC

COMMUNICATIONS: BACnet MS/TP baud rates: 9,600 / 19,200 / 38,400 / 76,800 / 115,000 (Auto-Discovery)

DISPLAY: 24 BPP TFT CTP, 480x480 pixel, 4" diagonal, dimmable LCD; IP54 rated

MONITOR I/Os:
- UI1, UI2, UI01, UI02: Universal inputs — voltage 0-10V ±5%, or dry contact (<100Ω closed, >100kΩ open)
- DO Switch: Relay output, 1 Amp max @ 24VAC

TERMINAL LAYOUT:
- 24VAC Power: HOT and GND terminals
- UI01: Universal I/O 1, GND
- UI02: Universal I/O 2, GND
- UI1: Universal Input 3, GND
- UI2: Universal Input 4, GND
- DO Switch: Generic programmable digital output
- RS485+: BACnet MS/TP positive
- RS485-: BACnet MS/TP negative
Wire gauge: 18–22 AWG (0.5–0.75 mm); solid core recommended

WIRING INSTALLATION (651-000-014):
- Raco 8670, Raco 8650, Raco 8663 or equivalent electrical boxes
- US: 3-1/4" (82.75mm) mounting hole spacing; European: 2-3/8" (60.3mm)
- Mount base plate first, pull wires through hole, terminate with supplied release tool or paper clip
- Mount screen bottom-up at slight angle, align socket/clip holes, press upper corners to snap in, tighten bottom locking screw to 2 in-lbs (0.2 Nm) with 5/64" allen key

RETROFIT MOUNTING OPTIONS:
- RD1: Covers FHD1xx recess mounting holes
- RM1: Covers FHMx00 and FHMx10 recess mounting holes
- RM3: Covers FHMx30 recess mounting holes

SCREEN DISPLAYS:
Normal: Normal operation, setback, flow override, face velocity override, remote exhaust override
Hibernation: Reduced energy mode; shows "OPEN SASH TO RESUME OPERATION"
Alarm/Emergency: Exhaust override, shows emergency messages with exhaust cancel button
Unlinked: FHD500 not connected to ACM — no information displayed
Fail-Safe: Shows "HOOD IN FAIL-SAFE" message

DISPLAY MODES:
- Icon mode: Sash position icon (approximates <1%, 1-33%, 34-66%, 67-99%, >99% open)
- Text only mode: Hood status text in center
- Gauge mode: Face velocity gauge display
- Kiosk: Alternates between sash position and alarms at 2-second intervals

ENERGY FEATURES:
- Hood energy waste indicator: Alerts when sash open + room unoccupied
- Night energy waste alert: Room lights off, instructs user to lower sash
- Hood hibernation: Reduces flow to minimum when no safety concerns

ORDERING GUIDE MODEL STRUCTURE:
FHD 500 — [Network Protocol] — [Options]
- FHD = Fume Hood Display
- 500 = BACnet MS/TP with BLE
- DHV = DHV Interface card in enclosure (retrofit without HUB)
- HUB = Fume Hood HUB: enclosure + interconnect PCB for sash sensors, ZPS, FHD, ACM (retrofit, no DHV)
- HUV = Fume Hood HUB with DHV included

TROUBLESHOOTING:
- Screen black: Check 24VAC at power terminals, check power wire seating
- Communication not working: Check RS485 polarity, EOL 120Ω resistors at both trunk ends, baud rate match, no conflicting MAC/Device ID
- Audible alarm not working: Check volume >0%, check if alarm muted (bell with circle-slash shown)

ENVIRONMENTAL: 32–122°F (0–50°C) operating; -40–150°F storage; 10–90% RH non-condensing
REGULATORY: CE, FCC, ICES, UL/cUL, RoHS3, REACH, Prop 65

topic: CSCP Venturi Valves — Complete Specs, Flow Ranges, Ordering Guide (MKT-0525 Rev.B 11/2024)
content:
CSCP VENTURI VALVES — MKT-0525 Rev.B 11/2024

FEATURES:
- Mechanically pressure independent — no electronics needed to maintain flow setpoint
- ±5% accuracy at any flow and pressure within rated specification
- Factory characterization on 3rd party NVLAP accredited airstations (Lab Code 200992-0)
- Inlet and exit insensitive — elbows/transitions don't cause inaccuracies
- No scheduled maintenance required
- Turndown ratios up to 20:1
- High-speed linear actuators: <1 second position response

PERFORMANCE:
- Medium pressure: 0.3"–3.0" WC (75–750 Pa) — ±5% accurate; Note: 0.3"–0.6" WC accuracy not guaranteed
- Low pressure: 0.1"–3.0" WC (25–750 Pa) — ±5% accurate; Note: 0.1"–0.3" WC accuracy not guaranteed
- Volume control accurate to ±5% of command signal

SINGLE VALVE FLOW RANGES:
- 6" valve: 30–350 CFM (51–595 m³/hr) — Medium pressure only, Single body only, Class A only, H and U orientations only
- 8" valve: 35–700 CFM (60–1189 m³/hr)
- 10" valve: 50–1000 CFM (85–1700 m³/hr)
- 12" valve: 90–1500 CFM (153–2549 m³/hr)
- 14" valve: 200–2500 CFM (340–4248 m³/hr)

DUAL VALVE FLOW RANGES:
- Dual 10": 100–2000 CFM (170–3398 m³/hr)
- Dual 12": 180–3000 CFM (306–5097 m³/hr)
- Dual 14": 400–5000 CFM (680–8495 m³/hr)
Note: Max flows may be less on low pressure or shutoff valves

ACTUATION SPEEDS:
- Control Types M and T (high-speed linear): <1 second, <3 second full stroke
- Control Type L (linear standard-speed): <40 seconds, <50 second full stroke
- Control Types H, I (rotary standard-speed): <1 minute, <90 second full stroke (60Hz power)

CONSTRUCTION:
- Aluminum body: 16 ga. spun, continuous welded seam
- Class A: Uncoated aluminum
- Class B: Heresite coated (corrosion resistant)
- Class C: Heresite coated with coated hardware
- Class D: PVDF coated (chemical intense applications)
- 6" cone: Industrial grade composite (PPS) with integrated bearings
- Spring: Stainless steel; slider: polyester or PPS
- Supply valves insulated: 3/8" closed-cell foam, flame/smoke rating 25/50

ORDERING GUIDE STRUCTURE: [Type][Chem Resistance][Bodies][Size][Fail-Safe][Orientation]-[Controller][Actuator][Pressure]-[Options]-[Protocol]
- Type: PVE = Phoenix Valve Exhaust; PVS = Phoenix Valve Supply
- Bodies: 1 = Single, 2 = Dual, F = Flanged
- Fail-Safe Module: N = None (fail to last position); Y = Programmable fail-safe position
- Controller: B = ACM only; D = ACM + PBC; P = PBC only; N = None; T = Terminal strip
- Actuator: C = CV (no Vpot); F = Fixed; H = Rotary standard-speed NEMA1; I = Rotary standard-speed IP54; L = Linear standard-speed IP56; M = Linear high-speed IP56; T = SMART proportional
- Pressure: L = Low (0.1–3.0" WC); M = Medium (0.3–3.0" WC)
- Options: PSL = pressure switch low limit; PTR = pressure transducer; REI = remote electronics indoor; EVI = exhaust insulation; LCL = low casing leakage; SSB = stainless shaft brackets

ACM CONTROLLER I/Os:
- Motor (factory wired)
- DP sensor/switch (typically factory wired)
- 2 universal I/O (UIO) programmable for fume hood sensors, ZPS, or other input
- Vpot (factory wired)
- Fail-Safe Module via right touch-flake connectors

POWER: 24VAC ±15% @ 50/60Hz
- Standard-speed (H, I, L): 50VA max
- High-speed single (M): 50VA max; high-speed dual (M): 70VA max

CERTIFICATIONS: HCAI (formerly OSHPD) preapproval per 2013 CBC/2012 IBC/ASCE 7-10/IEC-ES-AC-156; OSP-0290; CE, FCC, ICES, UL/cUL, RoHS3, REACH, Prop 65

topic: FHD500 Installation Guide — Mounting, Wiring, Troubleshooting (651-000-014 Rev.A 04/2024)
content:
FHD500 INSTALLATION GUIDE — 651-000-014 Rev.A 04/2024

PACKAGE CONTENTS:
- FHD500 monitor
- Two (2) Phillips Pan Head Screws #8 x 3/4"
- Wiring pin installation tool

REQUIRED TOOLS:
- Two (2) Phillips screwdrivers
- Small flat-blade screwdriver for wiring connections
- Supplied wiring pin installation tool (or paper clip for wire insertion)
- 5/64" allen/hex key for bottom locking screw

ELECTRICAL BOX REQUIREMENTS:
- Standard 3-1/4" (82.75mm) US mounting hole spacing OR standard European 2-3/8" (60.3mm)
- Compatible US boxes: Raco 8670, Raco 8650, Raco 8663, or equivalent single gang

STANDARD MOUNTING PROCEDURE:
1. Mount base plate to single gang electrical box using supplied screws (US or European spacing)
2. Pull wires through hole in base plate, leave adequate slack
3. Install wires per wiring section using supplied release tool
4. Hold FHD500 screen tilted slightly away from base plate
5. Align screw hole, socket, and clip holes to base plate counterparts
6. Lift screen slightly to maintain alignment, move up and over base plate
7. Gently but firmly press upper corners of screen to snap into base plate
8. While applying light inward pressure to base of screen (to compress gasket), tighten bottom locking screw to 2 in-lbs (0.2 Nm) with 5/64" allen key

RETROFIT MOUNTING:
- Slide U-Nut (included) over existing mounting holes with spring side inward
- Slide assembly into cutout area
- Attach bracket to fume hood with mounting screws provided

WIRING SPECIFICATIONS:
- Wire gauge: 18–22 AWG (0.5–0.75 mm)
- Solid core wire recommended for ease of installation (stranded acceptable)
- All wiring MUST comply with local electrical codes

WIRING PROCEDURE:
1. Press release tool (or paper clip) into tool hole while inserting wire into terminal hole
2. Push excess wire back through wall plate hole
3. Check for loose or frayed wires
4. After wiring verified, power on transformer and verify FHD500 powers up correctly
5. See FHD500 User Guide (MKT-0512) for startup and commissioning procedures

TERMINAL LAYOUT:
- 24VAC Power: HOT, GND
- UI01: Universal I/O 1, GND
- UI02: Universal I/O 2, GND
- UI1: Universal Input 3, GND
- UI2: Universal Input 4, GND
- DO Switch: Generic programmable output
- RS485+: BACnet MS/TP positive
- RS485-: BACnet MS/TP negative

TROUBLESHOOTING:
Screen is black:
- Check power properly connected to FHD500
- Check 24VAC across power wires
- Check power wires firmly pushed into baseboard

Communication not working:
- Check communication wiring polarity (MS/TP polarity must be maintained throughout trunk)
- End Of Line (EOL) resistors: 120Ω, 1/4W at BOTH physical ends of MS/TP trunk only
- MS/TP cable must meet minimum requirements (shielded twisted pair, 100–130 ohm characteristic impedance)
- Check FHD500 menu Communication configuration
- Verify MS/TP communication enabled
- Verify MAC address and Device ID do not conflict with other devices
- Verify BAUD rate matches all other devices on trunk

Audible alarm not working:
- Check volume set above 0% in menu
- Check if alarm is muted (alarm bell with circle/slash displayed)

topic: CSCP Valve Upgrade Kit — Overview, Requirements, Legacy Removal (MKT-0540 Rev.A 11/2024)
content:
CSCP VALVE UPGRADE KIT INSTALLATION GUIDE — MKT-0540 Rev.A 11/2024

OVERVIEW:
The valve upgrade program enables CSCP functionality in legacy systems. Covers upgrade of: Base Upgradable (BxV), High-Speed Analog Electric, All Pneumatics (Celeris 1, Analog, Celeris 2), Low/High Speed Electric (Celeris 2, Traccel, Theris), SMART Actuators BXV.

CRITICAL RULE: The entire room must be converted. Cannot convert just one or two valves — they cannot communicate with the rest of the room-level system.

INTEGRATION: Converted spaces integrate to BMS through native BACnet MS/TP or BACnet IP PBC, or optional Phoenix Controls Integrator (PCI8000).

SOFTWARE/HARDWARE REQUIREMENTS (ALL SYSTEMS):
- One upgrade kit per valve converted (legacy triple/quad valve sets require TWO conversion kits)
- Optional MS/TP to IP router or PCI8000 for BMS integration
- Laptop running Phoenix Controls Workbench v5.2.1 or later (MANDATORY — earlier versions will not work)
- New wall-mounted sensors (if local occupancy override required)
- Appropriate number of transformers
- Additional communications cable for room-level network
- Miscellaneous cables for reconnecting peripherals

IMPORTANT CONSIDERATIONS (ALL PHOENIX SYSTEMS):
- Review expected sequence of operations before starting
- As you upgrade valves, enter original characterization data on upgrade valve controller label
- You will likely need to relocate temperature control wiring
- Some cabling can be reused; some must be moved or replaced
- Only existing valve body and base channel are reused — replace Vpot, electronics enclosure, valve controller, and varying cabling
- Triple/quad valve sets = two separate upgrade kits (represent two duals on CSCP network)
- New CSCP database required for spaces being upgraded
- Significant BMS vendor coordination required as spaces transition

VINTAGE IDENTIFICATION TABLE:
Vintage A/B: Hourglass (Accel I) valve shape; U-channel base; Telco 8-pin connector; 3/16" pivot arm hole; Two actuators per dual
Vintage C: Diffused (Accel II) shape; Flat base; Telco connector; 3/16" pivot arm hole; Two actuators per dual
Vintage D/E: Diffused (Accel II) shape; Flat base; Terminal block connector; 1/4" pivot arm hole; One actuator per dual

CELERIS 1 UPGRADE SEQUENCE:
1. Celeris 1 controllers removed
2. Power supplies added for fume hood components or valves as needed
3. Network upgraded to CSCP
4. Valve controllers upgraded
5. Communications upgraded to optional PCI8000 or router

CELERIS 2 / TRACCEL / THERIS UPGRADE SEQUENCE:
1. Celeris 2 controllers removed
2. Network upgraded to CSCP
3. Power supplies added for necessary valves/devices
4. Valve controllers upgraded
5. Communications: PCI8000 or optional router

POWER REQUIREMENTS AFTER UPGRADE:
- FHD500: 8.5 VA
- ZPS 310: 7 VA
- ACM: 50 VA
- PBC: 50 VA
Note: Existing fume hood monitors and ZPS previously powered from ADV or analog valve — must be powered independently after CSCP upgrade. CSCP does not provide power for FHD, ZPS, DHV card.

MS/TP CABLE SPECIFICATIONS:
- Shielded 3-conductor cable, characteristic impedance 100–130 ohms
- Distributed capacitance between conductors: <100 pF/meter (30 pF/ft)
- Capacitance conductor to shield: <200 pF/meter (60 pF/ft)
- Maximum segment length: 4000 ft (1200m) with AWG 18 cable
- Plenum rated: Connect Air W223-C2060YPC (Black/White/Red with shield+drain)
- Non-plenum: Belden 3016A 120 ohm (White+orange stripe / Orange+white stripe / Blue+white stripe)

CELERIS 1 REMOVAL PROCEDURE (critical steps):
1. Power down valve; disconnect air supply; seal pneumatic line
2. Disconnect all wiring from controller and PEM (note fume hood, thermostat connections)
3. Remove and discard pneumatic connection controller-to-actuator
4. Dismount controller from base channel
5. Note pressure switch and hose orientation — MUST REPLICATE when installing new switch
6. Remove and discard pressure switch, wiring, and hoses
7. Disable actuator mechanical clamps; pump actuator until shaft collar clear
8. Remove hair pins and clevis pins from linkage
9. Remove actuator from base channel
10. Remove Vpot cover screw (save screw and cover)
11. Loosen Vpot-pivot arm coupling; twist mounting tabs; pull Vpot assembly out through installation slot
12. Discard Vpot assembly — REUSE VOIDS WARRANTY

UPGRADE CONTROLLER BOARDS:
- CSCP Low-Speed PBC Upgrade Controller (for standard speed applications)
- CSCP High-Speed ACM Upgrade Controller (with optional Fail-Safe Module)

topic: CSCP Valve Upgrade Kit — Vpot Installation, Controller Mounting, Flow Curve Download (MKT-0540)
content:
CSCP VALVE UPGRADE KIT — INSTALLATION DETAILS (MKT-0540 Rev.A 11/2024)

UPGRADE TOOLS REQUIRED:
- Torque wrench (11–80 in-lb) with 1/4" nut driver and 3/32" hex bit (Vpot coupling)
- Additional bits: 9/64" hex (pneumatic), 3/16" hex (Vintage D/E), 5/16" hex (Vintage A/B)
- 10K potentiometer and 9V battery (high-speed electric)
- Two 9V batteries in series (low-speed electric)
- Wire strippers, volt meter, needle-nose pliers, squeeze bulb (pneumatic)
- Flat and Phillips screwdrivers, 7/16" and 3/8" open-end wrenches
- Diagonal wire cutters (Vintage A/B)

VPOT INSTALLATION BY VINTAGE:
Vintage A/B (Below Base Channel):
- Vpot assembly slides up through installation slot in base channel
- Align Vpot shaft with pivot arm hole (3/16" shaft for Vintage A/B/C)
- Slide coupling onto Vpot shaft; slide against pivot arm bushing
- Tighten coupling to Vpot shaft; verify smooth rotation

Vintage C/D/E (Above Base Channel):
- Vpot assembly mounts above base channel through top slot
- 1/4" pivot arm hole (Vintage D/E) requires 1/4" shaft extension kit
- Align and install per vintage-specific procedure

CONTROLLER ENCLOSURE INSTALLATION:
Vintage A/B Enclosure Installation:
1. Mount enclosure to base channel using hardware from upgrade kit
2. Route communications cable through enclosure base
3. Connect MS/TP communications to controller terminal block (A, B, GND/SHLD)
4. Connect 24VAC power (verify polarity)
5. Install controller board into enclosure

Vintage C/D/E Enclosure Installation:
1. Remove any legacy enclosure hardware from base channel
2. Mount new CSCP enclosure per upgrade kit instructions
3. Connect all wiring; verify termination

No Electronics Enclosure Option:
- Available for applications not requiring on-valve electronics
- Remote electronics option (REI) allows electronics in separate enclosure

High-Speed ACM Installation (all vintages):
1. Install ACM controller board into upgrade enclosure
2. Connect actuator motor leads (factory wired on most upgrades)
3. Connect Vpot signal (factory wired)
4. Connect RS485 MS/TP network (A+, B-, SHLD)
5. Connect 24VAC power
6. Configure MS/TP address per PBC trunk requirements

Low-Speed PBC Installation (all vintages):
1. Install PBC controller board into upgrade enclosure
2. Wire PBC per standard PBC installation (see MKT-0511)
3. Set DIP switches for zone assignment if applicable
4. Configure BACnet IP or MS/TP per site requirements

PRESSURE SENSOR INSTALLATION:
Single Valve (pressure sensor tubing):
- High-pressure port connects to upstream (supply/inlet) side of valve
- Low-pressure port connects to downstream (exhaust) side
- CRITICAL: Pressure switch hose orientation MUST replicate original — photograph before removing
- Mismatched hose connections = inverted pressure reading = alarm at normal operation

Dual Valve Vintage A and E (separate tubing routes):
- Each valve body has independent pressure sensing
- Follow vintage-specific tubing routing per MKT-0540 diagrams

Dual Vintage B, C, D (shared manifold or separate):
- Pressure sensor may be shared between dual valve bodies
- See MKT-0540 Section 3.6.5.3 for specific tubing routing

FLOW CURVE DOWNLOAD — MANDATORY AFTER INSTALLATION:
The CSCP upgrade requires downloading the factory flow characterization curve to the ACM/PBC.

Vintage A/B/C Procedure:
1. Connect laptop with Phoenix Controls Workbench v5.2.1+ to PBC via Ethernet or Bluetooth
2. Navigate to valve device in Workbench
3. Select "Use the Default Curve Import Wizard" (Vintage A/B/C use factory default curve)
4. Download flow curve to controller
5. Verify curve download successful in Workbench

Vintage D/E Procedure:
1. Locate original characterization data (from valve label or request .VPT files from Phoenix Controls Tech Forum)
2. Open Workbench v5.2.1+ and use the Curve Import Wizard
3. Import the .VPT characterization file for this specific valve
4. Download to controller
5. CRITICAL: Do not use default curve for Vintage D/E — each valve has a unique characterization

FLOW CURVE NOTES:
- Original characterization data MUST be recorded on upgrade valve label (tie tag through base channel hole)
- Both original label AND upgrade label must be retained on valve
- If original data unavailable: contact Phoenix Controls Tech Support (800) 340-0007 for .VPT file retrieval from serial number database

topic: PBC Guide Specification — Zone Balance, Comfort Control, Occupancy, Emergency Control (MKT-0520)
content:
PBC GUIDE SPECIFICATION — MKT-0520 Rev.A 02/2024

ZONE BALANCE CONTROL:
- Sums total flows from Phoenix Venturi Valves in a space
- Provides networked command for volumetric offset control
- Supports Progressive Offset Control (POC)
- Supports cooling and heating volumetric overrides
- High-speed zone balance: via RS485 network internal to room (ZBH mode)
- Standard-speed zone balance: via hardwired connection to up to 4 valve bodies (ZBL mode)

COMFORT CONTROL:
- Temperature and humidity control
- Uses combination space temperature/humidity sensors, space/duct/outside air temperature sensors, discharge temperature sensors
- Supports: hydronic reheat, electric reheat, PID control, steam valves
- Cooling and heating setpoints: Occupied, Unoccupied, Standby, Vacant modes

OCCUPANCY CONTROL:
- Multiple settings programmable for Occupied, Unoccupied, Standby modes
- Allows airflow and temperature settings to relax when space is unoccupied
- Commands via BACnet MSO 2 (OccupancyCmd)

EMERGENCY CONTROL:
- Valves commanded to pre-determined positions via network command or local override
- Emergency mode command via BACnet MSO 1 (EModeCmd)
- Multiple emergency mode configurations programmable in DDC firmware

DIVERSITY CONTROLS:
- Allows valves to be set to lower flows based on building fan loading diversity
- Energy savings without compromising safety

STANDALONE PROGRAMMABLE CONTROLLER (GEN mode):
- 16 Universal I/Os, 8 Solid State Relays, 4 Dry Contact Relays
- Full DDC programming via Phoenix Controls Workbench
- Native function block library and sample applications

PHYSICAL SPECIFICATIONS:
- Platform: NXP I.MRT Cortex M7 crossover processor; 16MB SDRAM; 16MB QSPI Flash
- Real-time clock: 24-hour backup after power failure
- Power: 24VAC ±15%, 50–60 Hz; 50VA max, 8VA nominal
- Dimensions: 8.5" x 4.79" x 2.1" (216 x 121.6 x 53mm); Weight: 1.256 lbs (570g)
- DIN rail mounting (DIN43880, 35mm rail) horizontal or vertical; also surface mount with screws

FIELD WIRING:
- System transformers must be safety isolating per IEC 61558-2-6
- In USA/Canada: NEC Class 2 transformers required

SERVICEABILITY:
- Controllers replaced as single unit in field
- Controller cover NOT intended for field removal
- PCB handling not recommended (ESD risk)

SECURITY:
- BACnet MS/TP controllers do not protect against all cybersecurity risks
- Recommend: BMS controls behind firewall, VPN connection for remote access
- Disconnect power before installing or dismantling

AGENCY APPROVALS:
- UL 60730-1, CAN/CSA-E60730-1:02
- Complementary listing UL916, CSA C22.2 No. 205
- BACnet BTL Listed: IP Unitary and MS/TP Unitary as BACnet Advanced Application Controller (B-AAC)
- CE, FCC part 15B Class B, RoHS

PROGRAMMING:
- Configured using Phoenix Controls Workbench 5.0 (or later)
- Bluetooth communication to Flow Manager App for field monitoring and setpoint changes
- Native functionality supports field software upgrade over BACnet MS/TP or WiFi

topic: Flow Manager Mobile App — Complete Guide (MKT-0519 Rev.A 04/2024)
content:
CSCP FLOW MANAGER MOBILE APP USER GUIDE — MKT-0519 Rev.A 04/2024

OVERVIEW:
The Flow Manager Mobile App allows authorized users to monitor, troubleshoot, and operate Phoenix Controls CSCP devices (PBC, FHD500, ACM) via Bluetooth. Provides room-level view for monitoring, diagnostics, limited configuration changes, and setpoint changes.

SUPPORTED DEVICES:
- Android: Android 11.x and later (SDK v24.0)
- iOS: 14.x and later
- Minimum 200 MB free space required
- Portrait mode and light theme only; iPads/tablets supported with limited screen sizing

PREREQUISITES:
- Bluetooth enabled on mobile device AND on target Phoenix Controls devices
- Target devices must be configured as discoverable in Phoenix Controls Workbench
- NO cloud or wireless network connection required — Bluetooth only
- Range: 30–40 feet maximum (walls, metal objects may reduce range)
- Must connect to live PBC device FIRST on initial use
- Latest device firmware (PBC, FHD500, ACM) required
- Phoenix Controls Workbench required to commission PBC, FHD500 jobs
- Phoenix Controls Device Manager required to make devices discoverable

BLUETOOTH COMPATIBILITY:
- PBC: 500 series only (with BLE) — PBC 500 and 504 models
- FHD500: All models
- ACM: All models
- PBC 501 and 505 (without BLE) NOT supported by Flow Manager App

INSTALLATION:
- iOS: Download from App Store; after installation, must trust Honeywell certificate (Settings > General > Device Management > Honeywell certificate > Trust)
- Android: Download APK from Google Play; enable unknown sources if needed (Settings > Apps > Special app access > Install unknown apps)

FIRST-TIME USE WORKFLOW:
1. Tap Flow Manager icon → Get Started
2. Enable Bluetooth scan → app scans for discoverable Phoenix Controls devices
3. Select target device from discovered list
4. Enter passcode (set in Phoenix Controls Workbench during commissioning)
5. Passcode can be saved for future use; expired passcode can be reset in Workbench

PBC DASHBOARD — LAB ZONE VIEW:
- Displays: Zone exhaust CFM, supply CFM, zone offset, temperature, humidity, ACH
- Shows device status: Normal (white/green), Warning (yellow), Alarm (red)
- Override mode: Available for valves and UIOs; global override screen; relinquish all option
- Alarm types: Jam alarm, flow alarm, communication fault, temperature alarm
- Setpoint editing: Tap tile to edit; temperature/humidity/ACH/flow setpoints adjustable

PBC DASHBOARD — LoSEA VIEW:
- Shows standard-speed valve data (Valve 1–4)
- Valve states: Opening, Closing, Disabled, App Override
- Zone flow totals: exhaust, supply, return, bypass, offset
- Banner status: indicates zone control state

ACM DASHBOARD:
- ACM lab view: Valve position, Vpot reading, flow actual vs command
- ACM status: Normal/Warning/Alarm with color coding
- ACM alerts: Jam alarm, flow alarm, FSM status
- Valve position override: Manual position command capability (for T&B and diagnostics)
- UIO override: Manual I/O point override capability with live polling values

FHD500 DASHBOARD:
- FHD500 types: VAV, CVV, Two-State, Drive
- Status: Normal, Setback, Alarm, Hibernation, Unlinked, Emergency, Fail-Safe
- Hibernation mode: Shows open sash prompt
- UIO override screens for FHD500 universal inputs/outputs

USING FOR T&B (Test and Balance):
- ACM valve position override: Force valve to specific position for T&B verification
- PBC override: Force zone flow commands
- Live data display: Real-time CFM, Vpot, and I/O values
- Alarm viewing: See current alarm status without connecting a laptop

DISCONNECTING/MANAGING DEVICES:
- Saved Devices list: Previously connected devices
- Other Devices list: Newly discovered devices
- Disconnect: Available from device screen
- Forget/Remove: Clears device from app's saved list

topic: Phoenix Controls Vision Software — Setup, BACnet Integration, Hood Certification (MKT-0467 Rev.A 02/2024)
content:
PHOENIX CONTROLS VISION SOFTWARE USER'S GUIDE — MKT-0467 Rev.A 02/2024

PREREQUISITES:
- Room Manager version 3.x must be fully licensed and installed BEFORE Vision installation
- Vision software must be installed on Room Manager BEFORE initiating project in Workbench
- Name conventions for folders and points must be uniform across both Vision and Workbench
- Back up Vision jobs (PA-Vision folder) before starting new job — new job overwrites previous

VISION PROJECT SETUP IN WORKBENCH:
Step 1: Create New Job
1. In Workbench navigation pane, create new job under Phoenix Controls
2. BACnet Type: choose BACNET/IP
3. Integration: choose "Has Room Manager"
4. Enter PC login credentials, then job authentication (username/password)

Step 2: Add Remote PCI8000
1. Right-click RoomView → Add Remote PCI8000
2. Enter PCI IP address and login credentials
3. Upload existing station (right-click PCI → Upload → Yes to overwrite)
4. Connect local (right-click PCI → Connect local → Save)
5. Connect remote (right-click PCI → Connect Remote with credentials)

Step 3: Create Directory Structure
1. Expand: RoomManagerView > NetworkView > Station > Config > Drivers
2. Double-click Niagara Network → Station Manager
3. Click New Folder (DO NOT use right-click) → enter campus/job site name
4. Add building folders: Double-click campus → New Folder → enter building name
5. Add floor folders: Double-click building → New Folder → enter floor name

Step 4: Discover PCI
- If PCI_101 in Niagara Network: right-click → Cut → paste into Floor folder
- If not in Niagara Network: Double-click Floor1 → Discover → Add PCI_101 → Verify station name
- Set: Foxs = true, Port = 4911
- Credentials: Username: admin, Password: Admin12345
- Approve certificate: Services > Platform Services > certmanagerservice > Allowed Hosts

Step 5: Create Reciprocal Connection (PCI to Room Manager)
1. Remote connect to PCI → Expand Network > Station > Config > Drivers
2. Double-click NiagaraNetwork → locate Vision station → double-click
3. Enter IP address, Foxs = true, Port = 4911, username/password, Enabled = TRUE
4. Ping Vision station → approve certificate in certmanagerservice → ping again (should be OK)
5. Save PCI and Room Manager

Step 6: Duplicate Workbench Structure in Room Manager
- IMPORTANT: Valve application types (MAV, ATC) must be uniquely named or point import fails
- Open VisionRoomPoints_Completed file from Palette
- Drag VisionRoomPoints folder onto Points Object → rename → repeat for each room
- For each valve in each room: Discover → navigate to valve → select all points → Add

Step 7: Map/Link Points
1. Double-click room → change View to Wiresheet
2. Right-click room name → Actions → Set → enter room name
3. Expand valve → right-click point → Link Mark → on wiresheet right-click target → Link from → select source → OK
4. Delete unused points from wiresheet

ADDING ALARM SERVICE:
1. Drag Alarm Service from Alarm palette onto Services object
2. Open AlarmService Wiresheet → drag ConsoleRecipient from Recipients folder
3. Connect Default Alarm class to ConsoleRecipient (Shift+click → Route Alarm)
4. Double-click Alarm Db Config → adjust capacity (up to 250,000 records) → Save
5. Add BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt to JAM_ALARM, FLOW_ALARM, etc.
6. Set Source Name: %parent.parent.displayName%_%parent.displayName%

EDIT HISTORIES:
1. Config Services → programservice → Find object → bql query builder → Search icon
2. Find in: config/drivers/NiagaraNetwork; Type: Control Point → OK
3. Edit Slot → property: NumericInterval
4. Change History name to: %parent.parent.displayName%_%parent.displayName%
5. Set time interval and max records → OK

ADD VISION TO PCI8000:
1. Upload existing station from PCI to localhost
2. Platform > Distribution File Installer → choose Vision dist file → Install → wait 10 minutes for reboot
3. Download local station copy to PCI
4. Add DGLux5Service: Palette → dglogik → drag DGLux5Service to Services
5. Transfer vision.nav file via File Transfer Client to: home/niagara/stations/[PCI]/shared/navFiles
6. Install kitControl module via Software Manager → Commit → Restart

VISION USER ACCOUNT:
1. Navigate to Station > Config > Services > UserService → AX User Manager
2. Right-click Admin → Duplicate → rename to dgSuper
3. Set password → set Nav File to vision.nav from navFiles folder
4. Login via browser at PCI IP with dgSuper account
5. Complete Vision licensing if prompted (form → VisionOrders@viconequip.com)

REPORTS IN VISION:
1. Vision Dashboard → Reports section
2. Create Summary Report: select date range, rooms, and points
3. Report includes: hood face velocity data, alarm history, energy usage

SCHEDULES IN VISION:
In Workbench: Standard Niagara WeeklySchedule approach
In Vision: Dashboard schedule widget allows non-technical users to view/edit occupancy schedules

BACNET INTEGRATION FROM VISION:
1. Enable BACnet Networking: Station > Config > Drivers → right-click → Actions → Enable
2. Set BACnet/IP settings: Local Device ID, Network Number, IP address binding
3. Using BACnet Points: right-click device → Views → Point Manager → Discover → add proxy points

HOOD CERTIFICATION:
- Vision provides Hood Certification feature for compliance documentation
- Navigate to room in Vision dashboard → Hood Certification section
- Records face velocity measurements, date/time, technician name
- Generates certification report for ASHRAE 110 or local code compliance

topic: ACM Actuator Control Module — Specifications and Features (MKT-0513 Rev.B 04/2024)
content:
ACTUATOR CONTROL MODULE (ACM) — MKT-0513 Rev.B 04/2024

OVERVIEW:
The ACM is installed on all high-speed Phoenix Controls valves in the CSCP. It houses the factory valve characterization curve, Vpot data, motor control, RS485 communication, and two universal inputs/outputs (UIO). The ACM can be used as a standalone valve controller or linked with up to 20 ACMs in one zone for high-speed zone control.

PROGRAMMING SPEED:
- Default: High-speed control (<1 second response)
- Can also be programmed for standard speed control if application requires

OPTIONAL FAIL-SAFE MODULE (FSM):
- Allows valve to be moved to pre-programmed position on power failure (Full Open, Full Closed, or any position)
- Configurable fail-safe state for high-speed operation: fail-open, fail-closed, or fail-to-any position
- Factory installed; simple plug-and-play touch-flake connection to ACM
- Can be ordered as option on valves or added later as add-on
- When ordered: comes pre-configured, factory installed

I/O INTERFACES:
- 24VDC Actuator Output (motor control — factory wired)
- 24VAC main input (power)
- External DP (differential pressure) sensor interface — factory wired, optional
- Two UIO (Universal Input/Output) interfaces — programmable for fume hood sensors, ZPS, or other
- Vpot interface (valve position transducer — factory wired)
- RS485 communication interface (BACnet MS/TP at up to 76.8 kbps)

OPTIONAL DP SENSING:
- Factory-mounted 0–5 in WC (0–1244 Pa) pressure transducer, OR
- Low pressure differential pressure switch
- Both available as factory-installed options

MOUNTING:
- Suitable for DIN43880 fuse boxes with slot height max 1.77" (45mm)
- 1.38" (35mm) standard panel rail mounting (horizontal or vertical)
- FSM mounts to ACM via touch-flake connection (plug-and-play)

INTEGRATION POINTS:
- For complete BACnet integration point list, see ACM and FSM Guide Specification (MKT-0527)
- Zone-level integration via PBC: Up to 20 ACMs per PBC in ZBH (Zone Balance High-Speed) mode

PURCHASING NOTE:
- ACM is purchased as part of complete Phoenix Venturi Exhaust (PVE) or Supply (PVS) valve
- Not available as standalone purchase — ordered as part of valve configuration
- Controller Type: B = ACM only; D = ACM + PBC

topic: Phoenix Controls Workbench — PBC-CT Commissioning Tool, Device Manager, Job Setup
content:
PHOENIX CONTROLS WORKBENCH — COMMISSIONING TOOL REFERENCE

VERSION REQUIREMENTS:
- Workbench 3.0+: Required for CSCP (PBC/ACM/FHD500) commissioning
- Workbench 5.0+: Required for PBC programming (DDC function block)
- Workbench 5.2.1+: Required for CSCP Valve Upgrade Kit flow curve import

WORKBENCH STRUCTURE:
The Phoenix Controls Workbench is a Niagara 4-based tool. It is NOT the same as standard Niagara Workbench — it includes Phoenix Controls-specific palettes, commissioning tools (CTs), and device management.

PBC COMMISSIONING TOOL (PBC-CT):
1. Create hierarchy of rooms and devices using palette and Device Manager
2. Discover, add, and match devices (PBC, ACM, FHD500) in Device Manager
3. Configure each device using built-in Commissioning Tools (-CTs)
4. Adjust flow curves as needed (Vpot characterization)
5. Download configurations to devices
6. For PBCs: make DDC programming changes if required, then Teach to controllers

DEVICE MANAGER:
- Required to make PBC, FHD500, and ACM devices discoverable by Flow Manager App
- Once device appears in "Discovered" section: double-click or click Add button
- Verify selected device type matches physical device before adding
- Note: ACM commissioning feature planned for future Workbench release

CONNECTING TO PBC PLATFORM:
1. Open Phoenix Controls Workbench
2. File → Open → Platform (or New Platform Connection)
3. Enter PBC IP address (find via arp -a after boot)
4. Port: 4911 (Fox protocol)
5. Credentials: admin/admin (change immediately — standard practice)
6. After connection: Platform tools appear in nav panel

DISCOVERING DEVICES ON NETWORK:
1. Connect to PBC platform in Workbench
2. Navigate to station → Config → Drivers → BacnetNetwork
3. Right-click BacnetNetwork → Discover (or Views → Device Manager → Discover)
4. Wait 30–60 seconds for devices to respond
5. Select discovered devices → Add to database
6. For each device: configure device instance, baud rate, network settings

FLOW CURVE ASSIGNMENT:
- Each ACM stores the factory characterization curve for its specific valve body
- Curve contains 48+ calibration points mapping position to flow
- During upgrade: import .VPT file or use Default Curve Wizard
- Curve download: right-click device → Actions → Download Flow Curve
- Verify: check valve position vs actual CFM after download

BLUETOOTH CONFIGURATION:
- PBC 500/504 series and FHD500 support Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)
- Configure Bluetooth discoverability in Workbench before using Flow Manager App
- See Workbench Help for Bluetooth configuration details
- Set passcode in Workbench (required for Flow Manager App first-time connection)

STANDARD WORKBENCH CONCEPTS (applies to Phoenix Controls Workbench):
- Platform: The hardware controller (PBC, PCI8000) — connect to this first
- Station: The software running on the Platform — contains all programming and configuration
- Fox protocol: Port 4911 — used for Workbench-to-Platform communication
- Download: Push configuration from Workbench to physical device
- Upload: Pull current configuration from physical device to Workbench
- Teach: Apply DDC programming changes to controller firmware

topic: PBC LED Status Diagnostics and Communication Indicators
content:
PBC LED STATUS DIAGNOSTICS — MKT-0511/MKT-0520

LED LAYOUT:
- Small LEDs (green, 2 total): Communication signal transmission/reception indicators
- Large LEDs (1–3 depending on model): Device status indicators

SMALL LEDS — COMMUNICATION:
- Blinking green (TX): Data being transmitted from PBC on MS/TP trunk
- Blinking green (RX): Data being received by PBC from MS/TP trunk
- Both blinking normally: MS/TP trunk is active and communicating
- No blinking: No communication — check trunk wiring, baud rate, termination, power

LARGE LEDS — DEVICE STATUS:
Green (solid): Normal operation — PBC powered, programmed, communicating normally
Yellow (solid or blinking): Warning condition — device online but something needs attention:
  - Could indicate a setpoint limit being reached
  - Temperature or humidity out of range
  - Diagnostic mode active
Red (solid): Fault condition — device has a critical error:
  - Power issues
  - Communication failure
  - Hardware fault
  - Uncommissioned state (new device, no program downloaded)

SERVICE PIN LED:
- Press service pin button on PBC to send service pin message on BACnet network
- LED pulses when service pin pressed
- Used during device discovery in Workbench or BMS
- Allows manual identification of specific PBC on network

MS/TP COMMUNICATION STATUS LED PATTERNS:
- Rapid blinking: Active token passing — normal for busy network
- Slow intermittent blink: Low traffic — normal for lightly loaded network
- No activity: Check: (1) power, (2) baud rate match, (3) 120Ω termination, (4) A/B polarity, (5) cable continuity
- Constant solid (not blinking): Could indicate lockup — cycle power

GENERAL PBC STARTUP SEQUENCE:
1. Apply 24VAC power
2. Large LED: Red briefly (boot sequence)
3. Large LED: Yellow (loading program/database)
4. Large LED: Green (normal operation — program loaded, communicating)
5. Small LEDs begin blinking (MS/TP communication active)
6. If large LED stays red after 60 seconds: firmware issue or corrupt database — contact Phoenix Controls Support

Certain things must be done prior to working on other items. For example, a monitor must be in- stalled, wired and powered prior to calibration. If the installation or wiring is wrong, field calibration becomes difficult, time consuming and sometimes use- less. The point here is to be certain each step of the start-up process is managed thor- oughly. We can break this process down into a few simple steps that will help you, as a Field Service Engineer, manage the details in a structured and orderly fashion. Use Table 1 on the next page as a guide to the start-up methodology. The remaining pages of this section will provide some detail on each item from Table 1. Think of the table as the start-up process summary and the pages that follow as the complete explanation. 1-2 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 System Start-up Process Table 1-1. Summary of the Start-up Process Issue Required Action Comments Reference 1. Installation support A. Review basic installation. 1. Valves 2. Monitors 3. Sash sensors 4. Zone Presence Sensors 5. Drives 6. Panels 7. Power supplies B. Review wiring. 1. Wiring diagrams 2. Terminators C. Review pneumatics. Record contractors’ names and numbers in your file. As contractors complete the installations, have them complete the start-up checklists found in Section Use Installation Guide found in Section 3. Use Submittal. 2. Pre-calibra- tion checkout A. Field check equipment. 1. Verify field equipment vs. room schedule sheet (RSS) 2. General installation 3. Wiring and tubing B. Field check overall installation. Should have received completed start-up check- lists. Work with each contractor. Use Factory RSS. Use Installation Guide and Submittals. 3. Field calibra- tion (elec- tronic) A. Calibrate equipment. 1. Combination sash sensor 2. Monitors 3. Drives 4. Some scaling function modules B. Functional checks H/V boards only All monitors PCD series Depends on model code; may include valves, MAC/ MIX/MAS boards Use field calibration procedures found in Section 4. System checkout Verify volumes electronically. Balancer should verify electronically. Use procedure found in Section 5. 5. Balancing support A. Review equipment and system. 1. Valve systems

System Start-up Process 1-3 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Rev. 5/04 Field Service Manual Installation Support Field Service Engineers (FSEs) quite often take on the role of Project Manager. We must provide support to the installing contractors—especially to first-time installers and unique installations. Be sure to write down the names and phone numbers of these contacts; you’ll need these down the road. Section 3 is our Product Installation Guide, which you should use as a refer- ence. It can be copied and distributed to the field installers, but please do not let it be a substitute for your field support! For tubing and wiring requirements, use the project submittal as a reference. Start-up Checklists We depend on the installing contractors to complete the installation, wiring and tubing of our equipment. We cannot provide start-up until this work is complete; therefore, we need them to communicate to us when they truly are done. (How many times have you been told equipment is ready for start-up only to find the power has not been connected?) The use of Start-up Checklists will help. After reviewing the product installation with each contractor, provide them with the appropriate Start-up Checklist (blank forms are in Section 8). Please note that you must take a few minutes to prepare each checklist–do not simply give a contractor a blank form. As contractors complete each item on the Start-up Checklist, they should initial the appropriate step. Upon completion of the entire installation, have them send the form to you; this indicates that they truly have taken the time to com- plete the installation. NOTE: For projects being completed in phases, the checklists should be completed and submitted for each phase. Contractors Typical Product Installation Mechanical (sheet metal) Valves ATC Contractor (pneumatic) Pneumatic tubing Control Electrician Monitors, sash sensors, panels, etc.; low voltage wiring Power Electrician Line voltage wiring to power supplies 1-4 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 System Start-up Process Pre-Calibration Checkout In general, a field service engineer will need to put a bit of time into overall sys- tem checkout prior to starting field calibration procedures. The intent of this section is to review issues faced by a field service engineer at the time when equipment is ready for calibration. In other words, the initial installation meet- ing with the various contractors and engineers has already taken place and the equipment is “ready” for start-up. The FSE should do a “spot check” walk- through in an effort to: Verify field installed equipment vs. the scheduled equipment (room sched- ule sheets). Verify proper product installation. Verify wiring and tubing installation. Field check overall HVAC system installation. Once the system has been installed correctly, the actual field calibration can be- gin. Larger projects may be completed in phases so that the field calibration in one area can be completed prior to the installation of another. Verify field installed equipment vs. the scheduled equipment. You should have the Start-up Checklists signed by each of four contrac- tors: Power electrician, controls electrician, pneumatic controls and the sheet metal contractor. b. As you enter each room, verify the installed equipment matches the equipment on the room schedule sheet. Verify proper product installation. As you progress through each room, observe the general installation of all Phoenix Controls equipment. Check valves, sash sensors, ZPSs®, monitors, panels, etc. Meet with the installation contractor to rectify any problems. Verify wiring and tubing. Verify that the wiring and the pneumatic connections appear complete. b. Check that each lab has

For example, valves may require electrical and pneumatic connections, some may need minor assembly, and others may require insulation. See each prod- uct’s installation instructions for specific details. Table 2-1. Product Installation Requirements Product Installing Contractor Requires system wiring? Requires tubing? Requires power wiring. 2-2 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 System Overview Product Packaging and Shipping Phoenix Controls ships all products in specially designed packages for protec- tion from damage during transit and handling. Phoenix typically ships all pack- ages via UPS or a common carrier to each job site. Each package is labelled and shipped in accordance with Table 2-2. One of the boxes in each shipment will have a pink packing list in a sealed, plas- tic envelope. This packing list will detail all the equipment contained in a par- ticular shipment. Be sure to verify that the contents of every package are in accordance with the packing list. If you notice that a product is

Several other contractors are also in- volved (see Table 2-3). The following sections will help to clarify the typical responsibilities for each contractor. It in no way relieves contractors from their specified duties; this should only be used as an aid in understanding the general installation. Mechanical Contractor’s Primary Duties Receive all products. Often the equipment is shipped to the job site to the attention of the me- chanical contractor. Take care to distribute the various equipment to the proper contractor and/or location. Each box includes a label to identify the product, the model number, and, in many cases, the room number and tag information. Refer to Section 1 of this manual for more information. Install all valves. Each Phoenix Controls venturi air valve is designed to be installed in a spe- cific location on the project with a specific orientation. Therefore, all valves have two labels: Valve Identification—Lists the following information: Job name Phoenix job number Tag number Room number Phoenix part number The valve tag number matches the room schedule sheet and may also identify the duct location for valve. See 1 in Figure 2-1. Table 2-3. Contractor’s Primary Responsibilities for Installing Phoenix Controls Products Contractor Primary Responsibilities Mechanical (sheetmetal) Receive all products, install valves, connect valve linkages Controls electrician Install products (excluding valves, MIX cards and drives; provide low-voltage wiring) FMS contractor Install MIX integrated make-up air controllers Power electrician Power wiring, usually one location per room; install drives ATC (pneumatic) Supply main pneumatic interconnections Balancing Verify airflows Insulation Insulate supply air valves 2-4 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 System Overview b. Valve Orientation—Indicates the valve’s proper orientation with re- spect to airflow. See 2 in Figure 2-1. Valves are available in 3 orienta- tions: Horizontal (indicated by -AXXHX in the Phoenix part number) Vertical down (indicated by -AXXDX in the Phoenix part num- ber) Vertical up (indicated by -AXXUX in the Phoenix part number) The valves must be installed in the specified orientation (see 3 in Figure 2-1). Linkage Connection Many Phoenix Controls valves are shipped partially assembled. Linkages must be connected for valves to work properly. Figure 2-1. Valve labeling Controls Electrician’s Primary Duties Install products. With the exception of valves, MIX cards, and the variable speed drive, the controls electrician typically installs all Phoenix Controls products. Section 4 of this manual contains all installation instructions. Make low-voltage terminations. Every Phoenix Controls project includes specific point-to-point wiring/ piping diagrams for field connections. These wiring diagrams include low and hig- voltage connections, cable requirements, and pneumatic connec- tions for all systems on the project. Rev. 1/04 MKT-0071 MPC-0375 SLIM LINE FUME HOOD MONITOR—1 OF 5 Phoenix Controls FHM420 Fume Hood Monitor is sized to fit any fume hood and includes icons for simple and universal operation. The FHM420 slim line monitor works in conjunc- tion with a separate hood interface unit (mounted on top of the fume hood) to provide flow control logic and alarming functions. Control: Provides an airflow control signal to a Phoenix Con- trols valve. For VAV applications, constant face velocity is main- tained by adjusting the exhaust airflow as the measured sash position changes. The face velocity may be reset to a lower set- ting when an operator is not present at the fume hood. Alarm: Continuous display of normal and alarm hood condi- tions by use of green and red LEDs. Alarms are also audible. Specifications subject to change without notice. Rev. 1/04 APPLICATIONS VAV Fume Hoods: Fume hood containment is accom- plished by maintaining proper face velocity through the variable sash opening. Phoenix Controls FHM420 fume hood monitors can be used on manifolded exhaust systems (with Phoenix Controls valves) and on individual exhaust systems (with a Phoenix Controls drive). Display Unit/Hood Interface Unit functions: • Constant face velocity control: The goal is to maintain a con- stant face velocity (FV) as the sash opening varies. Since the FV setpoint is known, a change in sash area causes a linear change in exhaust flow (FV x Area = Flow command). Example: 5 ft2 x 100 ft/min = 500 ft3/min (or 0.5 m2 x 0.5 m/s x 368 s/hr = 900 m3/hr) • Setback of face velocity: Under many conditions, the face veloc- ity can be set back to provide safe containment when the hood area is vacated. Setback face velocity is adjustable to field condi- tions—typically between 60-100 fpm (or 0.3- 0.5m/s). • Alarms: The monitor provides indication of normal operation and alarm conditions. To bet- ter understand what is meant by board configuration, we need to review the general concept of our analog electronics board design. With our analog electronics, one board is designed to provide many different control strategies by incorporating several circuits. These circuits are enabled by placing “jumpers” in specific locations, essentially allowing one board to take on many different control strategies. (By comparison, a digital electronics board changes control strategies by entering software parameters.) We differentiate the many control strategies by assigning each one a model code. What is a Model Code? As mentioned, one product can be used for several purposes or applications. Many of the Phoenix Controls circuit boards can change function by simply changing jumper configurations. Model codes are used to differentiate the con- figuration of a product. In other words, one product has several applications and, therefore, several model codes. The Purpose of Model Codes As a manufacturer, we must know which configuration is required to meet a given application. Model codes inform the manufacturing personnel exactly how to configure the product. Each configuration of a product takes significant detail and explanation. By coding each configuration, the process is greatly simplified. The following Phoenix Controls products

The jumpers for each monitor must be set in the field–these are not factory set. 4-2 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Electronic Board Configurations How to Use This Section Section 4 includes the following subsections that provide an understanding of how the product configuration process works: Model Code Descriptions—A quick reference of model codes and descrip- tions listed by product. Board (Jumper) Configuration Charts—Each electronic board is broken down into tables with details on jumper requirements and board adjust- ments. Use this section to verify the board is configured properly for your application and/or model code. Board Test Points and Component Layout—A diagram of each electronic board is shown. Included are terminal points for wiring, test points for troubleshooting and calibration and components which have significance to field work. Use this section for a point by point or component by com- ponent understanding and checkout. content: 5-2 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Field Calibration and Verification Procedures positions. These are 22 turn “pots” that will click when fully tuned. Set the jumper configuration for the monitor by using the information in the following table: * Denotes factory installed jumpers. Change as needed. Determine the design face velocity for the hood. From the room schedule sheet, determine the minimum and maximum cfm values for the fume hood. For valve systems, list the scale factor for the hood valve. Face Velocity________ fpm Scale Factor ________ cfm/volt (hood valve) Minimum cfm________ Maximum cfm________ Pot Function Initial Setting Pot Function Initial Setting VR1 Sash calibration ccw VR6 Setback max clamp VR2 Min command ccw VR7 (FHM610) CFM adjustment mid-position VR3 Max command ccw VR8 (FHM610) FV adjustment mid-position VR4 Fault threshold (drive) ccw VR8 (FHM310) Meter adjustment mid-position VR5 Setback % VR9 (FHM310 and 610 Energy waste adjustment factory set Jumper Function Jumper Function S1* In for VSS 1xx, CSS 1xx, CSS 2xx, HSS 2xx In for FHM310/610 units where the energy waste alarm trigger point is 5” above the constant volume height In for HSS 1xx S8* In for FHM310 and 610 units where the energy waste alarm trigger point equals the constant volume height In if setback is used and minimum cfm is not setback Out (required for setback maximum clamp only) S4* S10* S5* In for valve systems S11 Out (located on the top board) In for drive systems Field Calibration and Verification Procedures 5-3 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Rev. 5/04 Field Service Manual Calculate the minimum and maximum vertical sash heights, based on the minimum and maximum cfm values. (For horizontal sashes only, use height in lieu of width in the following formula.) NOTE: This formula allows for a leakage rate of 3 cfm/ft2 of maximum sash opening. Leakage may change based on fume hood design. Fume hood must be VAV type. Following is an example of a 10ft2 sash opening, minimum cfm=200, max- imum cfm=1030: Formula: Example: min hgt = [144] x [min cfm - (leakage)] min hgt = 144 x [200 - 30] [width (inches)] x [setpoint face velocity] 51.5 x 100 min hgt = ________ inches min hgt = 4.75 inches Formula: Example: max hgt = [144] x [max cfm - (leakage)] max hgt = 144 x [1030 - 30] [width (inches)] x [setpoint face velocity] 51.5 x 100 max hgt = ________ inches* max hgt = 28 inches * If this exceeds the maximum physical sash opening, use the maximum sash opening and calculate the proper airflow using: cfm = {[(max hgt x width)/144] x setpoint FV} x 1.05. Be sure to notify the project management of this discrepancy and to adjust the room schedule sheet. Place the sash to the minimum position calculated in step 5. Adjust VR1 clockwise to the point where the small red light emitting diode (LED) un- der VR4 just turns off (the on/off threshold). Set the digital voltmeter to d.c. volts and monitor the cmd (command) test point with respect to ground (see test points on the lower card). For valve systems, adjust VR2 clockwise for the minimum voltage, which will be: Formula: min volt = min cfm ÷ scale min volt = _______ For drive systems, measure the airflow (face velocity) and adjust VR2 clock- wise until the desired setpoint is reached. Verify the system is responding properly by measuring the fbk (feedback) test point and comparing it to the command–they should be approximately equal. Raise the sash to the maximum position calculated in step 5. Monitor the cmd test point. For valve systems, adjust VR3 for the maximum voltage, which will be: Formula: max volt = max cfm ÷ scale max volt = _______ For drive systems, measure the airflow (face velocity) and adjust VR3 clock- wise until the desired setpoint is reached. 5-4 Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Field Calibration and Verification Procedures NOTE: Do not adjust beyond the maximum feedback signal; this will cause an alarm. Verify the system is responding properly by measuring the fbk (feedback) test point and comparing it to the command–they should approximately equal. Product Support Line—Contact Information . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 7-5
Field Service
Manual
7-1
MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Rev. 5/04
Section 7
Troubleshooting Guide
Introduction
This section will be helpful when you need to troubleshoot a specific Phoenix
device or the entire system.  We’ll look at troubleshooting in two ways:
The field service engineer who runs into problems during the calibration
process
You receive a “trouble call” during or after the warranty period
The majority of our problems are a result of an HVAC system problem, such as
loss of airflow or low static pressure in the ductwork. Keep in mind that our
equipment may be the device which alerts the people to a problem (e.g. fume
hood monitor in alarm), so human nature is to call us for the solution. With
this fact in mind, the single best way to troubleshoot a system is to have an ed-
ucated maintenance and user group on site. Before we get into the details of
troubleshooting, please understand that providing proper system training to the
owner’s personnel is the best way to avoid the need for troubleshooting. When
the building personnel truly understand the entire HVAC system and how our
system relates, most problems will be resolved without our involvement.
Troubleshooting
During Start-up
Sometimes you’ll progress through a start-up doing all the right procedures and
you’ll find the equipment does not respond as designed. To troubleshoot the
system:
Review the Troubleshooting Summary on pages 7-3 and 7-4. This sum-
mary lists the most common problems you’ll encounter, along with possi-
ble causes and solutions.
You can contact the Phoenix Controls Product Support Line for additional
help at 800-4-PHX-TEC (800-474-9832) from 8 a.m.-5 p.m. Eastern
time, Monday-Friday.
When you do find problem equipment, complete the Manufacturing
Field Report (see Section 8), contact the Product Support Line for a
return authorization and send the product and report to Phoenix Con-
trols.
7-2
Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431
Answering a Trouble
Call
When you go out on a call concerning Phoenix equipment, please remember to
record your findings. To troubleshoot the system:
Familiarize yourself with the system and the equipment in question.
Review the HVAC plans, the wiring diagrams and the sequence of opera-
tions from the submittals or as-builts.
Review the Troubleshooting Summary on pages 7-3 and 7-4. This sum-
mary lists the most common problems you’ll encounter, along with possi-
ble causes and solutions.
You can contact the Phoenix Controls Product Support Line for additional
help at 800-4-PHX-TEC (800-474-9832) from 8 a.m.-5 p.m. Eastern
time, Monday-Friday.
Record your findings, provide a copy to the owner’s personnel, keep a copy
for your files and, when appropriate, send a copy to your office for filing
and/or billing.
Troubleshooting Guide
7-3
MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Rev. 5/04
Field Service
Manual
Troubleshooting Summary
Fume Hood Monitor
Tools required
Digital voltmeter with clip leads
Magnehelic gauge and tubing
Small screwdriver
* Voltages may vary.
Problem
Voltage at TB1-6
on Monitors
(310/410/610)
Possible Cause/Solution
1. Monitor in flow alarm
> 10 V
A. Low static pressure scross valve
Loss of airflow. Check fan operation and duct blockage.
Too many sashes open at one time. Close sashes.
Valve failed open. Check pneumatic and mechanical con-
nections; check for 20 psi.
5 V
B. Incorrect valve position
Valve failed open.
Sash open beyond the maximum allowable position.
Lower sash.
A broken sash cable. Check all sash sensor connections.
C. Equipment/connection problems
Blocked or kinked pressure switch tubing. Correct tubing.
Wiring terminations between monitor and control device.
Correct terminations.
Malfunctioning alarm circuits. Verify proper static.
Contact Phoenix Controls Service Group for assistance.
Miscalibration of monitor. See Recalibration procedures.
2. Monitor indicates normal opera-
tion, but actual face velocity has
been measured
0 V
A. Low static pressure
Differential pressure between the pressure switch setpoint
and the low end of the static pressure operating range
(i.e., 0.3” and 0.6” wc [75-150 Pa] for medium pressure
valves) will not trip the alarm circuit.
Measure differential pressure. If low, see Possible Cause/
Solution 1A above.
B. Monitor miscalibration
Contact Phoenix Controls Service Group for assistance.
See Recalibration procedure.
3. Monitor malfunction:
No display
Cannot mute alarm
A. Loss of power
Check wiring connections at monitor, valve, and power
supply.
Verify power supply has input voltage (120 Vac).
Check power at monitor TB1-1 (+15 Vdc) and TB1-3 (-15
Vdc).
B. Defective monitor. Contact Phoenix Controls Service Group
for assistance.
7-4
Rev. 5/04 MKT-0001 MPC-0431
Troubleshooting Guide
Isolation Room
Monitors
General Operational
Problems
Problem
Possible Cause/Solution
1. Door alarm is engaged.
A. Door is ajar. Close the door completely.
B. Switch assembly broken or wiring problem.
2. Isolation alarm is engaged.
A. Loss of duct airflow. Check for LED D2.
B. Offset is incorrect. Check for LED D5 and D8.
C. Low air changes. Check for LED3.
D. Auxiliary measuring device in error. Check for LED1.
Problem
Possible Cause/Solution
1. Room is too hot or too cold.
A. Reheat coil is wide open or closed.
Check discharge air temperature into the room.
Check reheat operation.
B. Thermostat is malfunctioning. Check setpoint and calibration.
C. Air handler is malfunctioning.
Air handler is shut down. Check operation.
Verify air handler unit discharge temperature.
D. Incorrect valve position. Perform Lab Room Airflow Verification procedure.
2. Odors outside the lab/room pressurization
problem.
Airflow tracking is malfunctioning.
Perform Lab Room Airflow Verification procedure.
Contact Phoenix Controls Service Group for assistance.
3. Hood does not go into standby operation
(only for hoods with Zone Presence Sen-
sors).
A. Zone Presence Sensor setback has been miscalibrated. Verify setback cal-
ibration using procedure outlined in Monitor Recalibration.
B. Hood has sash handles. Contact Phoenix Controls Service Group for assis-
tance.
Troubleshooting Guide
7-5
MKT-0001 MPC-0431 Rev. 5/04
Field Service
Manual
Troubleshooting
Quick Chart
This is the standard approach to troubleshooting Phoenix Controls products.
(Always have your factory room schedule sheet, wiring diagrams and Field Ser-
vice Manual handy.)
Installation/component location/obvious damage
Connections (pneumatic and electric, valve linkages)
Power (+ and -15 vdc and 20 psi main air)
Field calibration (sash sensor boards, fume hood monitor, variable speed
drive)
Differential static pressure requirements
DDC connections (that may pull down or affect signals)
Basic signal flow (command vs. feedback, override input, + and -12 and
10-volt regulator voltages on controller cards)
Factory settings (jumpers, clamps, sfms, potentiometer settings, valve
internal calibration)
Product Support
Line—Contact
Information
For assistance in equipment start-up and troubleshooting efforts, please contact
the Phoenix Controls Product Support Line at:
1-800-4-PHX-TEC
(1-800-474-9832)
Hours:
8 a.m.-5 p.m. Eastern time
Monday-Friday
7-6

content: P, PRV, RTU, SAT, SEP, ST, TCP, TXP ADU, CHB, CUH, EAV, ECH, EH, EUH, FCU, FD, FFU, FPVAV, FSD, GUH, HAV, PTAC, RHC, SAV, SD, UH, UV, VAV, WHP VFD ABBREVIATION TYPE DUCT CONNECTION SIZE.
