QUANTITY CFM ABBREVIATION TYPE ABBREVIATION TYPE LENGTH ABBREVIATION MARK ABBREVIATION FLOOR MARK SERVICING EQUIPMENT TAG SPECIFIC COMPONENT DESIGNATION XX-XX XXX(X) XXX-X XXX-X-X'X" XXX-X XXX-X-X VFD-XX-XXXX TEMPERATURE/HUMIDITY DESIGN PARAMETERS SUMMER: 95°F DB, 75°F WB WINTER: -5°F OUTSIDE LAB AREAS: 72°F, 60% RH MAXIMUM INSIDE (SUMMER): LAB AREAS: 70°F, 30% MINIMUM % RH INSIDE (WINTER): A. THE HVAC SYSTEM SHALL BE INSTALLED IN ACCORDANCE WITH APPLICABLE CODES AND STANDARDS. B. DRAWINGS ARE DIAGRAMMATIC. INDICATED POSITIONS SHALL BE FOLLOWED AS CLOSELY AS POSSIBLE. EXACT EQUIPMENT LOCATIONS AND DUCTWORK AND PIPING ROUTING SHALL BE SUBJECT TO BUILDING CONSTRUCTION AND INTERFERENCES WITH OTHER TRADES. WHERE THE LOCATION OF A SPECIFIC PIPE, DUCT, OR DEVICE IS DIMENSIONED, ITS INSTALLED LOCATION SHALL BE AS DIMENSIONED UNLESS COORDINATED OTHERWISE WITH THE ARCHITECT OR ENGINEER. C. ALTHOUGH ATTEMPTS HAVE BEEN MADE TO IDENTIFY EXISTING EQUIPMENT LOCATIONS, PIPE AND DUCTWORK ROUTING, AND SIZES WITH THE USE OF EXISTING DRAWINGS AND FIELD OBSERVATIONS, MC SHALL FIELD VERIFY EXISTING INFORMATION AND REPORT ANY DISCREPANCIES TO THE ARCHITECT OR ENGINEER. CONTRACTOR SHALL NOTE DISCREPANCIES ON THE RECORD DRAWINGS. D. UNLESS NOTED OTHERWISE, SERVICES INDICATED AS BEING REMOVED SHALL BE REMOVED TO THE POINT INDICATED FOR RECONNECTION, OR BACK TO THE MAIN, CAPPED, AND IDENTIFIED. REMOVE PREVIOUSLY ABANDONED PIPING, DUCTWORK, SUPPORTS, ETC ENCOUNTERED ABOVE CEILINGS. E. THE OWNER SHALL HAVE THE OPTION OF RETAINING ANY OR ALL REMOVED EQUIPMENT FOR SALVAGE. MC SHALL DISPOSE OF EQUIPMENT NOT RETAINED BY THE OWNER. F. WHERE EXISTING THERMOSTATS, THERMOMETERS, OR OTHER EQUIPMENT CONTAINS MERCURY OR MERCURY BASED PRODUCTS, FOLLOW EPA UNIVERSAL WASTE RULES FOR REMOVAL, TRANSPORTATION, AND RECYCLING. G. WHERE WORK OCCURS OUTSIDE THE PROJECT SCOPE BOUNDARY, THE CONTRACTOR PERFORMING THE WORK SHALL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR THE REMOVAL AND REINSTALLATION OF CEILINGS, GRIDS, AND LIGHTS AS REQUIRED TO PERFORM THE WORK. H. COORDINATE WORK WITH THE PHASING OF THE PROJECT. SOME SERVICES SHALL REMAIN ACTIVE TO SERVE OCCUPIED SPACES DURING CONSTRUCTION. SCHEDULE SERVICE SHUTDOWNS WITH OWNER AND/OR CONSTRUCTION MANAGER. CUTTING AND PATCHING OF WALLS AND FLOORS SHALL BE THE RESPONSIBILITY OF THE CONTRACTOR PERFORMING THE WORK REQUIRING THE PENETRATION. IF THE MC DEFACES OR DAMAGES WALLS, CEILINGS, FLOORS, OR FINISHES, THE MC SHALL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR PATCHING, REPAIRING, AND REFINISHING. PATCHING MATERIALS SHALL MATCH THE EXISTING CONDITIONS. FINISH PAINTING SHALL BE BY THE GENERAL CONTRACTOR. PROVIDE FIRESTOPPING AT PENETRATIONS OF FIRE-RATED ASSEMBLIES. REFER TO THE ARCHITECTURAL LIFE SAFETY PLANS FOR THE LOCATION OF FIRE-RATED ASSEMBLIES. FIRESTOPPING SHALL BE PERFORMED BY THE CONTRACTOR PERFORMING THE WORK REQUIRING THE PENETRATION. K. EXCEPT FOR SLAB ON GRADE, PROVIDE FIRESTOPPING AT PENETRATIONS OF NON-FIRE-RATED FLOORS. FIRESTOPPING SHALL BE PERFORMED BY THE CONTRACTOR PERFORMING THE WORK REQUIRING THE PENETRATION. L. WHERE DUCTS, CONDUITS, OR PIPES ARE REMOVED THROUGH FIRE-RATED FLOORS OR WALLS, THE CONTRACTOR REMOVING THE DEVICE/MATERIAL SHALL SEAL THE REMAINING OPENING TO MAINTAIN FIRE RATING. M. PROVIDE FILLING AND SEALING OF THE AREA AROUND PENETRATIONS OF SMOKE-RATED ASSEMBLIES. REFER TO THE ARCHITECTURAL LIFE SAFETY PLANS FOR THE LOCATION OF SMOKE-RATED ASSEMBLIES. MATERIALS USED SHALL BE COMPATIBLE WITH THE ASSEMBLY BEING PENETRATED. N. INSTALL EQUIPMENT REQUIRING AN ELECTRICAL CONNECTION IN SUCH A MANNER SO THAT PROPER CLEARANCE IS PROVIDED FOR SERVICING PER THE NATIONAL ELECTRIC CODE. O. EQUIPMENT LAYOUT IS BASED ON SCHEDULED EQUIPMENT. ACTUAL INSTALLED EQUIPMENT SIZE, CONFIGURATION, AND DUCTWORK/PIPING CONNECTIONS SHALL BE COORDINATED WITH THE BUILDING AND DUCTWORK/PIPING LAYOUT. P. EQUIPMENT SCHEDULES CONTAIN BOTH NOTES AND REMARKS. NOTES APPLY TO ALL EQUIPMENT SCHEDULED. REMARKS APPLY ONLY TO SPECIFIC EQUIPMENT AS INDICATED. Q. HVAC SYSTEM PIPING AND DUCTWORK SHALL NOT BE INSTALLED IN ELEVATOR SHAFTS, ELEVATOR MACHINE ROOMS, ELECTRICAL ROOMS, OR INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY (LOW VOLTAGE) ROOMS. EXCEPTION – PIPING AND DUCTWORK DIRECTLY RELATED TO HVAC EQUIPMENT CONDITIONING THE ROOM. ROUTE PIPING AND DUCTWORK INTO THE ROOM OVER THE DOOR AND NOT OVER PANELS OR EQUIPMENT. CONTACT ARCHITECT/ENGINEER PRIOR TO INSTALLATION OF ANY OTHER PIPING OR DUCTWORK. R. DO NOT SUPPORT DUCTWORK OR PIPING FROM ANOTHER DUCT, PIPE, OR CONDUIT. DO NOT SUPPORT ANY ITEM FROM METAL ROOF

topic: LACS Laboratory Airflow Control System — Specification with Addendum No. 1 (Battelle) (Part 3 of 3)
content: DECK. S. NO EXPOSED PIPING OR DUCTWORK SHALL BE INSTALLED BELOW 7’-6” IN AN EGRESS CORRIDOR. T. ATTENTION IS CALLED TO THE LIMITED CEILING SPACE. COORDINATE ALL WORK WITH CEILING HEIGHTS, SOFFITS, STRUCTURE, AND LIGHTS. HOLD DUCTWORK AS HIGH AS POSSIBLE, TO DECK BETWEEN BEAMS IF REQUIRED, AND TIGHT TO STRUCTURE. PROVIDE ANY AND ALL OFFSETS AND EFFORT REQUIRED TO FACILITATE THE INSTALLATION OF OTHER EQUIPMENT AND SYSTEMS. COORDINATE CLOSELY WITH OTHER TRADES. U. UNLESS NOTED OTHERWISE, STANDARD ROOM SENSOR/THERMOSTAT MOUNTING HEIGHT SHALL BE 44” ABOVE THE FINISHED FLOOR TO THE CENTERLINE OF THE DEVICE. V. DO NOT LOCATE THERMOSTATS OR SENSORS ON AN EXTERIOR WALL, BEHIND OPEN DOORS, OR IN CONFLICT WITH FURNITURE OR WALL HUNG DEVICES. COORDINATE EXACT LOCATION WITH OWNER/ARCHITECT. W. CONTROL WIRING SHOWN ON PLAN DOES NOT INDICATE ROUTING OR SCOPE OF WORK BETWEEN EQUIPMENT AND SENSOR/STAT. CONTROL WIRING SHOWN INDICATES EQUIPMENT ZONING. X. DUCTWORK DIMENSIONS SHOWN ARE GROSS (OUTSIDE SHEETMETAL SIZE). NET FREE AREA OF LINED OR DOUBLE WALL DUCTWORK IS REDUCED BY THE DUCT LINING OR INSULATION THICKNESS. Y. DURING CONSTRUCTION, COVER SUPPLY, RETURN, AND EXHAUST DUCTWORK OPENINGS TO PREVENT CONSTRUCTION DUST FROM ENTERING THE DUCTWORK. IF DUST COLLECTS INSIDE DUCTWORK, DUCTWORK SHALL BE CLEANED PRIOR TO PROJECT TURNOVER. Z. DUCTWORK SHALL BE PRESSURE TESTED AS INDICATED IN THE SPECIFICATIONS. IF A SECTION OF DUCTWORK FAILS THE TEST, THE SECTION SHALL BE SEALED AND RETESTED UNTIL IT PASSES. AA. RADIUS ELBOWS SHALL BE UTILIZED THROUGHOUT THE DUCTWORK SYSTEM. UNLESS SPECIFICALLY SHOWN, DO NOT USE 90° ELBOWS WITH TURNING VANES WITHOUT PRIOR APPROVAL OF THE ENGINEER. BB. INSTALL AND PROPERLY SUPPORT FLEXIBLE DUCTWORK TO AVOID AIRFLOW RESTRICTIONS. NO KINKS OR CRIMPS ARE PERMITTED. CC. FLEXIBLE DUCTWORK SHALL NOT PENETRATE THRU WALLS. RIGID SHEETMETAL DUCTWORK IS REQUIRED AT WALL PENETRATIONS. DD. FLEXIBLE DUCTWORK SHALL NOT BE USED IN EXPOSED LOCATIONS. EE. PROVIDE FIRE DAMPERS AND FIRE/SMOKE DAMPERS WHERE INDICATED AND WHERE REQUIRED BY CODE. REFER TO THE ARCHITECTURAL LIFE SAFETY PLANS FOR THE LOCATION OF FIRE AND SMOKE-RATED ASSEMBLIES. WHERE THE LOCATION OF A DAMPER (INDICATING A RATED ASSEMBLY), CONFLICTS WITH THE LIFE SAFETY PLAN, NOTIFY THE ARCHITECT OR ENGINEER. FF. PROVIDE DUCTWORK ACCESS DOORS FOR MOTOR OPERATED DAMPERS, FIRE DAMPERS, SMOKE DAMPERS, FIRE/SMOKE DAMPERS, SMOKE DETECTORS, HUMIDIFIERS, AND REHEAT COIL INLETS AND OUTLETS. WHERE REHEAT COIL IS INTEGRAL WITH A VAV TERMINAL UNIT, TERMINAL UNIT SHALL INCLUDE A BOTTOM ACCESS DOOR. IMPORTANT THAT DUCTWORK ACCESS DOORS ARE ACCESSIBLE. IF LOCATED ABOVE DRYWALL CEILING OR BEHIND FINISHED WALL, PROVIDE ACCESS DOOR. COORDINATE ACCESS DOOR LOCATIONS WITH ARCHITECT. topic: Niagara 4 BACnet Driver Setup — Complete Step-by-Step for Phoenix Controls PBCs
content:
NIAGARA 4 BACNET DRIVER SETUP — COMPLETE PROCEDURE

To set up BACnet communication with Phoenix Controls PBCs in Niagara 4:

1. Open the station in Workbench. Navigate to Config > Drivers.
2. If no BacnetNetwork exists: from the palette, drag a BacnetNetwork onto Drivers. If one exists, open it.
3. In the BacnetNetwork properties, confirm Enabled = true.
4. Confirm BACnet/IP is configured: set UDP port to 47808. Verify the local BACnet network number matches site design.
5. If PBCs are on a different subnet or VLAN: configure BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device) entries so broadcast discovery crosses VLAN boundaries. Without BBMD, PBCs on remote subnets will not appear.
6. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30-60 seconds for devices to respond.
7. Identify the target PBC by its Device Instance number (configured during PBC commissioning). Add the discovered PBC device to the station.
8. Under the PBC device, open the point manager or object explorer. Select the BACnet objects needed (AI for CFM flows, AO for commands, BV for alarms, MSO for occupancy/emergency mode).
9. Create proxy points by dragging or importing BACnet objects. Each proxy point maps a BACnet object to a Niagara slot.
10. Assign tuning policies to each proxy point:
    - Fast (1 second): life-safety, pressurization, critical CFM actual values, alarm inputs
    - Normal: general monitoring, setpoints, non-critical flows
    - Slow: energy reporting, noncritical trends, values that don't drive safety response
11. For pressurization and safety points, decide between COV (Change of Value subscription) and polling. COV reduces network load but requires the device to support it. Polling guarantees regular updates.
12. Verify proxy points are receiving live data: check status, units, facets, and that values match local PBC/FHD readings.

IF PBC DOES NOT APPEAR IN DISCOVERY — diagnose in this order:
- Network connectivity: Can the JACE ping the PBC IP?
- Wrong IP/subnet: PBC on different subnet with no BBMD configured
- VLAN segmentation: IT blocked BACnet/IP UDP 47808 or no BBMD router between VLANs
- Incorrect or duplicate Device Instance: Two devices with same instance — only one responds
- BACnet service disabled on PBC: verify BACnet is enabled in PBC configuration
- Firewall blocking UDP port 47808
- PBC power or boot: PBC may still be booting or in fault state
- MS/TP trunk issues: if PBC has offline ACMs, PBC may be partially functional
- Baud rate mismatch on MS/TP trunk
- RS485 polarity or termination/end-of-line resistor problem on trunk
- Duplicate MAC address on the MS/TP network
- Firmware compatibility issue between PBC and Niagara driver version

topic: Niagara 4 Polling, COV, Tuning Policies, Stale Points, Force Read, Poll Scheduler
content:
NIAGARA 4 POLLING, COV, AND STALE POINT DIAGNOSTICS

TUNING POLICY GUIDE:
- Fast (~1 second): Life-safety points, pressurization alarms, critical CFM actual values. Use only when safety or sequence response requires it. Overuse overloads the poll scheduler.
- Normal: General monitoring, setpoints, status points, non-critical flows.
- Slow: Energy reporting, noncritical trends, values that don't drive safety response.

WHEN LOCAL PBC READS CORRECT BUT NIAGARA READS WRONG:
Compare local PBC/FHD value against the Niagara proxy point. If local is correct and Niagara is wrong, the problem is on the Niagara side:
1. Right-click proxy point > Actions > Force Read — if value updates, polling is working but slow
2. Check point status: stale, null, fault, disabled, or overridden?
3. Check COV subscription: if COV-based, the subscription may have dropped. Restart the driver or switch to polling.
4. Verify Device Instance: if wrong, proxy point is reading from a different device
5. Verify BACnet object mapping: correct object type and instance number?
6. Check units and facets/scaling: value may be correct but displayed in wrong units
7. Check BBMD/network path: if network changed, COV subscription may be broken
8. StatusNumeric block: use to inspect the quality/status of a numeric input, not just the value

POLL SCHEDULER OVERLOAD:
- Above 75%: treat as overloaded, investigate before problems occur
- At 92%: critically overloaded — take action immediately
  Causes: failed network connectivity triggering constant retries, broken COV subscriptions forcing polling fallback, too many Fast bucket points, MS/TP trunk delays, stale/offline devices retrying, VLAN/BBMD changes breaking paths, JACE 9000 resource limits
  Remediation sequence:
  1. Restore network connectivity / BBMD / routing first
  2. Move noncritical points from Fast to Normal or Slow
  3. Fix stale/offline devices (remove or correct)
  4. Repair broken COV subscriptions
  5. Remove unnecessary high-frequency polling
  6. Verify JACE 9000 is not resource-limited (CPU/memory)
  7. Keep scheduler under 75% after all changes

NULL PROPAGATION IN KITCONTROL MATH:
A kitControl Add, Subtract, or Math block outputs null when either input has bad status — even if the displayed value looks valid. Use StatusNumeric to inspect input quality. Force Read each input proxy point. Verify tuning policy, polling/COV. Confirm neither input is stale, fault, disabled, or null before assuming the math block is broken.

topic: Niagara 4 Folder Structure, HistoryService, AlarmService, ORD Organization
content:
NIAGARA 4 FOLDER, HISTORY, ALARM, AND ORD ORGANIZATION

FOLDER HIERARCHY (multi-building Phoenix Controls projects):
Building > Floor > Room > [Equipment]
  - Room/FumeHood_01/ExhaustCFM_Actual
  - Room/FumeHood_01/FaceVelocity_Actual
  - Room/SupplyValve_01/SupplyCFM_Actual
  - Room/RoomPressure_Actual
  - Room/Alarms/FlowAlarm, PressureAlarm
  - Room/Setpoints/OffsetSP, FaceVelocitySP

NAMING CONVENTION:
Include building abbreviation, floor, room number, equipment type, and point function in every point name. Example: B7-L2-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM. Consistent naming ensures ORD paths are predictable and operators can navigate without custom graphics.

HISTORYSERVICE:
- Add HistoryExt to each proxy point that needs trending
- History name should match room and point purpose: B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM
- In HistoryService, organize histories by building/floor/room to match station folder structure
- Set appropriate interval: 1 minute for flow/pressure, 5-15 minutes for temperature/energy
- Critical trends: actual CFM, face velocity, room pressure differential, offset actual vs design

ALARMSERVICE:
- Add AlarmExt to critical proxy points: flow alarms, pressure alarms, face velocity low, communication faults
- In AlarmService, use source names that include room and point for fast operator identification
- Set alarm classes by severity: life-safety (highest priority), pressurization, flow, communication
- Route alarms to appropriate recipients: email, console, BMS notification

ORD (OBJECT RESOLUTION DESCRIPTOR):
- ORD is the full path from the station root to any object: slot:/Buildings/Bldg7/Floor2/RM214/FH1/ExhCFM
- Consistent ORD paths allow graphics, histories, and alarms to link reliably
- When copying or templating rooms, verify all ORDs update correctly
- Use ORD in alarm source names so operators can trace from alarm back to source device

topic: Niagara 4 Wire Sheet — Volumetric Offset Monitoring, Alarm, and Trending
content:
NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET — VOLUMETRIC OFFSET MONITORING

COMPLETE IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Bring in Supply CFM as a proxy point (from PBC BACnet AI for total supply)
2. Bring in Exhaust CFM as a proxy point (from PBC BACnet AI for total exhaust)
3. Add a Subtract block: Exhaust CFM minus Supply CFM = Actual Offset (positive = negative pressure room)
4. Add a NumericWritable named DesignOffset — operator sets the required design offset CFM here
5. Add a second Subtract block: Actual Offset minus Design Offset = Offset Error
6. Add Abs block if needed to get absolute error value
7. Add a GreaterThan block: compare Offset Error against allowable threshold (e.g., 50 CFM)
8. GreaterThan output drives a BooleanWritable (true = offset out of tolerance)
9. Add AlarmExt to the BooleanWritable: alarm activates when offset drifts beyond threshold
10. Add history extensions to:
    - Actual Offset (trend to verify pressurization is maintained)
    - Design Offset (trend to verify setpoint has not changed)
    - Offset Error (trend to see how far system drifts)
    - Room pressure differential if transducer available

TUNING POLICY: Exhaust and Supply CFM proxy points should use Fast tuning for pressurization safety applications.

COMMON MISTAKES:
- Using raw BACnet values without checking units/scaling
- Not adding history to offset error (operators can't see historical drift)
- Setting threshold too tight (nuisance alarms) or too loose (misses real events)
- Forgetting AlarmExt — the alarm exists but never routes to operators

topic: Niagara 4 Wire Sheet — High-Signal-Select, LoopPoint PID, NumericSwitch, Emergency Latch
content:
NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET — KEY CONTROL PATTERNS

HIGH-SIGNAL-SELECT (MIJ REPLICATION IN NIAGARA):
The high-signal-select strategy picks the greater of two demands — typically thermal demand vs pressurization demand — and sends the higher value as the supply command. In MIJ systems, this was hardware. In Niagara 4, replicate it with kitControl:
1. Thermal demand: comes from a LoopPoint PID output controlling to space temperature setpoint
2. Pressurization demand: comes from offset control logic or a NumericWritable setpoint
3. Max block (or Math/high-signal-select): compares both values and passes the higher to the supply valve command
4. NumericWritable: use for operator-adjustable minimum supply, maximum supply, or setpoint values
5. Output drives supply valve proxy point at appropriate BACnet priority

FACE VELOCITY SETBACK:
1. WeeklySchedule or occupancy sensor produces occupancy Boolean
2. Boolean feeds a NumericSwitch block with two inputs: 100 fpm (occupied) and 60 fpm (unoccupied)
3. NumericSwitch output is the active face velocity setpoint
4. Include override logic: BooleanWritable for occupied override, BACnet priority write behavior
5. For 24/7 labs: tie the occupied input to a constant true or use a separate exception schedule
6. Add history to setpoint and actual face velocity to verify setback is working

EMERGENCY EXHAUST LATCH:
1. Fire alarm or emergency input (BooleanWritable or BI proxy) connects to Set input of a Latch block
2. Manual reset button (operator action or BooleanWritable) connects to Reset input of Latch
3. Latch output drives emergency exhaust command BooleanWritable at BACnet priority (typically 8 or 3)
4. BACnet priority write forces exhaust to maximum
5. Add AlarmExt to emergency command point — routes alarm when emergency mode activates
6. Add history extension to latch output — shows when emergency activated and when manually reset
7. Provide operator indication in graphics: show latch state, time activated, reset status

LOOPPOINT PID SETUP:
- Controlled variable: space temperature (or other feedback)
- Setpoint: target temperature (from NumericWritable or schedule)
- Output: drives reheat valve, cooling valve, or fan speed
- Kp (proportional gain): start conservative (0.1-0.5), trend and tune
- Ti (integral time): start at 300-600 seconds, reduce if slow to recover
- Td: usually 0 for HVAC applications
- Action: direct (output increases as error increases) or reverse — set based on physical device
  - Heating: reverse action (output increases when temperature is below setpoint)
  - Cooling: direct action (output increases when temperature is above setpoint)
- Output limits: set Min/Max to match valve range (0-100%)
- Bias: initial output value at zero error — set if valve needs pre-positioning
- Trend: controlled variable, setpoint, and output together to diagnose hunting or drift

CASCADING ROOM PRESSURIZATION (THERIS/HEALTHCARE):
1. Room pressure proxy point (from differential pressure transducer)
2. Subtract block: actual pressure minus setpoint = pressure error
3. LoopPoint PID: takes pressure error, outputs exhaust offset command
4. Supply tracks exhaust offset to maintain pressurization differential
5. For cascaded suites: outer room LoopPoint output feeds inner room setpoint
6. Trend each LoopPoint's controlled variable, setpoint, and output

topic: CSCP Complete 20-Room Commissioning Sequence Including Niagara 4 Integration
content:
CSCP 20-ROOM COMMISSIONING SEQUENCE — COMPLETE

PHASE 1 — MECHANICAL READINESS (do not begin controls commissioning without this):
- Ductwork complete and sealed
- Air available: fans running, dampers open, filters installed
- Design airflow paths physically capable
- Valves installed in correct orientation
- All tubing and wiring pulled and terminated
- TAB/air balancing readiness confirmed — mechanical system must be stable first
- Controls cannot fix missing duct, no air, fan problems, or unbalanced mechanical systems

PHASE 2 — CSCP DEVICE SETUP (per room, documented):
1. Verify 24VAC power at ACM, PBC, FHD500
2. Assign unique MAC addresses to each ACM (DIP switches or software, document the address schedule)
3. Verify baud rate matches across all devices on the MS/TP trunk
4. Verify PBC-to-ACM MS/TP communication — all ACMs online in PBC status
5. Link FHD500 to ACM/PBC as required — run FHD500 Setup Wizard
6. Verify sash sensor input and face velocity calculation
7. Verify valve response: command exhaust valve open/closed, observe Vpot and FHD feedback
8. Verify exhaust CFM and supply CFM at design setpoints
9. Verify room pressurization/offset: exhaust minus supply equals design offset
10. Verify ZPS/zone presence sensor operation if installed

PHASE 3 — NIAGARA 4 INTEGRATION (per room/zone):
1. Add PBC device under BacnetNetwork in Niagara Drivers
2. Discover and confirm Device Instance
3. Create proxy points for: total exhaust CFM, total supply CFM, face velocity, room offset, valve states, alarms, occupancy mode, emergency mode
4. Assign tuning policies: Fast for safety/pressurization, Normal for general, Slow for trends
5. Add HistoryExt to: CFM actual, face velocity actual, room pressure/offset, setpoints
6. Add AlarmExt to: flow alarms, face velocity low, communication fault, pressurization deviation
7. Test each alarm: verify it routes to AlarmService and recipient
8. Verify trend data is collecting in HistoryService

PHASE 4 — ACCEPTANCE AND DOCUMENTATION (per room):
1. Verify face velocity or flow at setpoint — must be within ±10% or specified tolerance
2. Verify sash response: raise sash, confirm flow increases, face velocity maintained
3. Verify alarm response: force condition, confirm alarm appears in Niagara and at recipient
4. Verify offset/pressurization: exhaust exceeds supply by design offset CFM
5. Verify emergency mode: trigger, confirm valve goes to configured position, latch behavior
6. Sign off each room on commissioning checklist
7. Build punch list for any incomplete or non-conforming items
8. Produce turnover documentation: address schedule, calibration records, as-built sequences, alarm list, trend configuration

topic: Fume Hood CFM Reads Zero in BMS — CSCP Network Architecture Diagnostic
content:
FUME HOOD CFM READS 0 IN BMS — COMPLETE DIAGNOSTIC

This is a two-fork problem: actual airflow problem vs communication/data path problem. Distinguish them first.

FORK 1 — VERIFY LOCAL READING:
- Check the FHD500 display: does it show a valid CFM? If yes, the valve and ACM are working.
- Check ACM status in Flow Manager App or PBC: is the ACM online and showing flow?
- If local reading is correct → go to Fork 2 (communication/data path problem)
- If local reading also shows 0 → go to airflow/valve investigation

FORK 2 — COMMUNICATION AND DATA PATH (if local is correct):
1. PBC status: Is the PBC online? Is it communicating with Niagara?
2. MS/TP trunk: Is the ACM communicating with PBC? Check PBC ACM list — is this ACM online?
3. Niagara proxy point status: Is it showing stale, null, fault, or overridden?
4. Force Read: right-click proxy point in Niagara > Actions > Force Read. Does value update?
5. BACnet object mapping: Is Niagara reading the correct AI object for this room's exhaust CFM?
6. Device Instance: Is Niagara connected to the correct PBC?
7. Tuning policy: Is the proxy point on Slow or disabled? Switch to Normal and recheck.
8. COV subscription: If COV-based, subscription may have dropped. Restart driver or switch to polling.
9. BMS scaling/units: Some BMS integrations scale values — verify the integration layer is not zeroing the value.

AIRFLOW/VALVE INVESTIGATION (if local also shows 0):
- Sash sensor: Is sash position reading correctly? Closed sash = low/zero flow is normal.
- ACM valve command: Is ACM commanding the valve open?
- Vpot reading: Does Vpot show valve is moving?
- Duct static pressure: Adequate static at the valve inlet?
- Valve fail-safe: Is valve in fail-closed position?
- Power to ACM: 24VAC present?

topic: Pneumatic Phoenix Controls — I/P Signal Chain, Slow Response, Leak Test, Fire Alarm Sequence
content:
PNEUMATIC PHOENIX CONTROLS — COMPLETE REFERENCE

I/P TRANSDUCER SIGNAL CONVERSION:
The I/P (current-to-pressure) transducer converts a 4-20 mA electrical signal to a 3-15 PSI pneumatic output signal. This is the standard unless project documentation specifies a legacy or non-standard configuration. The 4 mA input produces approximately 3 PSI output (minimum/fail state). The 20 mA input produces approximately 15 PSI output (maximum stroke).

MIJ SIGNAL CHAIN — COMPLETE:
PBC/controller command > MIJ terminal block (TB) > 4-20 mA signal to I/P transducer > I/P converts to 3-15 PSI pneumatic output > pneumatic signal moves actuator > actuator moves valve cone to position > valve position creates flow > flow feedback to FHM/controller/BMS > feedback closes the control loop.

SLOW PNEUMATIC RESPONSE (45 seconds when should be 8):
Check in this order:
1. Tubing length — excessively long pneumatic runs slow response
2. Tubing restriction — kinked tubing, pinched fitting, undersized tubing
3. Orifice — plugged or undersized orifice in actuator or I/P limits flow rate
4. I/P transducer lag — I/P output slow to change, verify I/P is responding to mA signal
5. Supply pressure low — verify instrument air pressure at regulator (typically 15-20 PSI supply)
6. Regulator — regulator failing, fluctuating, or set too low
7. Air leak — leak in tubing or fittings bleeds off pressure, slows actuator
8. Contaminated pneumatic lines — moisture, oil, debris in lines restricts flow
9. Actuator diaphragm leak or damage — actuator cannot hold or build pressure
10. Mechanical binding — valve linkage or cone mechanically binding, slowing stroke

PNEUMATIC LEAK TEST PROCEDURE:
1. Isolate the pneumatic circuit to be tested
2. Apply test pressure through a calibrated regulator
3. Monitor pressure decay using a calibrated gauge — hold for specified time (typically 5 minutes)
4. Inspect all fittings, tubing joints, I/P connections, and actuator for leaks
5. Acceptance: per site or manufacturer specification — typically no measurable decay over test period
6. Document: initial pressure, decay amount, test duration, repair location, retest result

PNEUMATIC SUPPLY PRESSURE REQUIREMENTS:
Instrument air supply typically 15-20 PSI to I/P input. I/P requires clean, dry, oil-free air. Low supply pressure: actuator won't reach full stroke, valve may not open fully. High supply pressure: risk of I/P damage, overdriving actuator, diaphragm failure. Always check regulator setting and verify supply pressure at the I/P input, not just at the air compressor.

FIRE ALARM / EMERGENCY PNEUMATIC SEQUENCE:
On fire alarm or emergency signal: verify what fail-safe position the valve is configured for. Pneumatic valves may use spring-return to fail to a defined position when air is removed. Normally open (N.O.) valves open on loss of air — exhaust valves are typically normally open so they fail to maximum exhaust. Normally closed (N.C.) valves close on loss of air — supply valves may be normally closed for smoke containment. Confirm configuration before assuming fail direction. Include: fail-safe actuator position, spring-return behavior, N.O. or N.C. configuration, exhaust/supply response, emergency mode intent, and containment/smoke-control design.

topic: Commissioning Prerequisites, Acceptance Criteria, BSL-3, ACM Address Assignment
content:
COMMISSIONING PREREQUISITES AND ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

FIRST STEP — MECHANICAL READINESS (always verify before controls commissioning):
- Ductwork complete and sealed — not just roughed in
- Air available: supply fans running, return fans running
- Dampers: open and functional
- Filters: installed at correct locations
- Valves: physically installed in correct orientation with correct valve type
- Tubing and wiring: complete and terminated
- TAB/air balancing readiness: design CFMs achievable with mechanical system
- Do NOT start controls commissioning if fans aren't running, ductwork has open sections, or airflow is unavailable. Controls cannot compensate for mechanical deficiencies.

ACM ADDRESS ASSIGNMENT AND VERIFICATION:
1. Plan and document the address schedule before starting: assign unique MAC addresses to every ACM (typically 1-20 per PBC trunk)
2. Set MAC address via DIP switches on each ACM board — photograph each setting
3. Verify baud rate is consistent across all ACMs and the PBC (typically 76800 baud for CSCP)
4. Power up ACMs and PBC
5. In Phoenix Controls Workbench or Flow Manager App, verify each ACM appears in PBC device list
6. Confirm each ACM's address matches the address schedule
7. Power cycle individual ACMs if they don't appear — allow full boot time before assuming failure
8. Link each FHD500 to its assigned ACM/PBC using the Setup Wizard
9. Document: ACM serial number, assigned MAC, valve tag, room number, PBC assignment

ACCEPTANCE TEST CRITERIA:
For face velocity: measure actual face velocity at the specified sash opening. Must be within ±10% of setpoint (or per project specification). Document measured value, setpoint, and deviation.
For flow: measure actual CFM at design conditions. Must be within ±10% (or per spec).
For alarms: force each alarm condition, verify alarm appears at Niagara, BMS, and recipient.
For pressurization/offset: verify exhaust exceeds supply by design offset CFM.
For valve response: command open and close, verify valve strokes within specified time.
For sash response: raise/lower sash, verify face velocity remains within setpoint.
All results must be documented in commissioning report with sign-off.

BSL-3 AND HIGH-CONTAINMENT COMMISSIONING:
BSL-3 is not a standard lab — treat it with additional rigor:
- Monitored negative pressure: continuous monitoring with alarm on any positive pressure excursion
- Verified airflow offset: exhaust must exceed supply by verified margin at all times
- Backup/redundant exhaust: verify backup system activates on primary failure
- Fail-safe behavior: verify every valve fails to the correct position on power loss
- Emergency modes: test all emergency scenarios and document response times
- Alarms: every critical point has AlarmExt, routes to life-safety class, notifies designated personnel
- Trending: continuous trends of room pressure, exhaust/supply CFM, offset, and valve positions
- Manual override: document behavior, auto-return, and any overrides that require manual reset
- Documented verification: every test witnessed, measured, signed, and dated — BSL-3 commissioning records are regulatory documents

topic: Legacy Systems — FHM631 Er_c, MIJ Terminal Block, Celeris Hunting, FHM631 Replacement
content:
LEGACY PHOENIX CONTROLS — FIELD REFERENCE

FHM631 Er_c DISPLAY:
Er_c means the FHM631 is uncommissioned — it is in factory/uncommissioned condition. The board does not have its commissioning data loaded. The controller needs Parameter 1 and all required calibration/commissioning parameters restored or entered. This is not a generic calibration error — it is specifically an uncommissioned state. Causes: new replacement board installed, factory reset occurred, EEPROM loss. Fix: connect commissioning tool, enter Parameter 1 to set operating mode, enter all 23 calibration parameters per the Room Schedule Sheet for this hood.

FHM631 REPLACEMENT MID-PROJECT:
When replacing a failed FHM631 board without disrupting the network:
1. Document all current parameters before pulling the old board (photograph display, print parameter list)
2. Remove old board — network will alarm on communication loss from this board only
3. Install new board — verify jumper and DIP switch settings match the failed board exactly
4. Restore parameters from backup or documentation: Parameter 1 (operating mode), MAC/network address, baud rate, all 23 calibration values (face velocity setpoint, flow setpoints, sash sensor calibration, alarm setpoints, etc.)
5. Verify communication restored: board appears on network, no duplicate MAC conflicts
6. Verify face velocity and flow readings match expected values
7. Verify alarms function
8. Confirm network stability before returning hood to service
9. Update the documentation to reflect replacement date and board serial number

MIJ WIRING AND TERMINAL BLOCK (TB):
Flow Command wiring: red wire at terminal block (TB). Use TB terminology when describing MIJ wiring. The complete signal chain: PBC/controller command > MIJ terminal block (TB) > 4-20 mA current loop to I/P transducer > I/P converts to 3-15 PSI pneumatic > actuator strokes > valve positions > flow changes > flow feedback returns to controller/BMS. Feedback is a required part of the loop — not optional.

CELERIS HUNTING (80-120 CFM OSCILLATION):
Hunting indicates the control loop is unstable. Diagnose in this order:
1. Sash sensor: is sash position signal stable? Noise in sash input causes hunting.
2. Flow feedback: is flow feedback signal stable? Check Vpot signal quality.
3. PID tuning: gain too high causes oscillation. Reduce Kp first.
4. Deadband: increase deadband so minor flow deviations don't drive the loop.
5. Calibration: verify zero-flow calibration. Mis-calibrated zero causes constant hunting.
6. Actuator: pneumatic actuator slow or inconsistent? Check I/P, supply pressure, tubing.
7. Mechanical: valve linkage loose? Pivot arm worn? Mechanical play amplifies control noise.
8. Trending: trend command signal, feedback signal, sash position, and actual CFM together to identify which is oscillating first — this shows whether it's a command, feedback, or mechanical issue.
After each tuning change: wait 5-10 minutes and observe before making another change.

topic: Controls Logic — DCV, Occupancy Scheduling, Demand-Controlled Ventilation, Exhaust Offset Calculation
content:
CONTROLS LOGIC — DCV, SCHEDULING, AND PRESSURIZATION

DEMAND-CONTROLLED VENTILATION (DCV) FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS LABS:
DCV allows airflow to reduce when CO2 or occupancy indicates low occupancy, reducing energy. Key rule: DCV must never violate minimum pressurization offset or containment requirements.

DCV implementation:
1. CO2 sensor input (proxy point) or occupancy sensor feeds a reset calculation
2. CO2 level drives a CFM reset: higher CO2 = more ventilation, lower CO2 = allow reduction
3. Minimum ventilation floor: CFM cannot drop below the minimum required for pressurization
4. Override: sash opening or hood demand can override DCV and force higher flow
5. Pressurization lockout: if DCV would reduce exhaust to a level that violates the design offset, the offset setpoint takes priority — DCV is overridden by pressurization requirement
6. Mode: include an occupied/unoccupied/DCV override mode that operators can force

In Niagara wire sheet:
- CO2 proxy point feeds a LoopPoint or setpoint reset calculation
- NumericWritable holds minimum ventilation setpoint
- Max block: takes higher of CO2-driven demand and minimum ventilation floor
- Override BooleanWritable: forces to occupied level when sash open or hood active

OCCUPANCY SCHEDULING IN NIAGARA 4:
1. WeeklySchedule: define occupied hours for each day of week
2. Schedule output: Boolean (true = occupied, false = unoccupied)
3. BooleanWritable: receives schedule output with ability to be overridden
4. kitControl logic: if occupied, use occupied setpoints; if unoccupied, use setback setpoints
5. BACnet priority: write occupancy command at appropriate priority (typically 14 for schedule, 8 for local override)
6. Override handling: local occupancy sensor or pushbutton can override schedule at higher priority
7. 24/7 labs: constant occupied input, or WeeklySchedule with all hours occupied, or override BooleanWritable forced true

EXHAUST OFFSET AND PRESSURIZATION:
Volumetric offset = Exhaust CFM minus Supply CFM. This CFM difference is what drives negative pressure differential in the room. More offset = more negative pressure (more negative relative to corridor). Less offset = less negative pressure. The offset must be maintained at all times to keep the room negative. If DCV or energy savings reduces flows, both exhaust and supply must reduce proportionally to maintain the offset — reducing only supply while exhaust stays high would create excessive negative pressure; reducing only exhaust would eliminate negative pressure. The design offset CFM creates the room pressure differential: typically 0.01-0.05 inches w.c. for research labs, higher for BSL-3.

FOUR HOODS TO MAX EXHAUST AT 2AM — INVESTIGATION:
Common-cause simultaneous triggers to investigate:
- Schedule error: overnight schedule set to occupied or emergency mode
- Occupancy override active: a BACnet priority write forcing occupied mode
- Fire/smoke alarm input: wired to emergency mode command — alarm at 2am triggered
- ZPS/zone presence sensor: false detection or wiring fault sending occupied signal
- BACnet priority write from BMS: supervisor writing emergency command
- Wire sheet logic: shared command point or logic error driving all hoods simultaneously
- PBC-level override: operator-entered override not cleared from previous shift
- Emergency mode latch: emergency mode latched from a previous event, not manually reset
Investigate: check Niagara alarm history for 2am, check BACnet priority array on emergency command points, check wire sheet for common trigger points

topic: Troubleshooting Field Reference — Pressure Spec, Face Velocity, Power Restoration, Renovation Recommissioning
content:
TROUBLESHOOTING FIELD REFERENCE — KEY SPECIFICATIONS AND PROCEDURES

MINIMUM DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE FOR ±5% ACCURACY:
Low-pressure Phoenix Controls Venturi valves: minimum 0.1 inches w.c. (in. w.c.) across the valve, though accuracy is not guaranteed below 0.3 in. w.c. Medium-pressure valves: minimum 0.3 inches w.c. for guaranteed ±5% accuracy. When expressing pressure specifications always use inches w.c. or in. w.c. — not just WC without units.

FACE VELOCITY LOW ALARM — TWO DIAGNOSTIC FORKS:
A fume hood showing face velocity low alarm at 87 fpm with sash 8 inches open:

Fork 1 — Actual airflow/CFM problem:
- Is exhaust valve actually delivering design CFM?
- Check ACM Vpot reading and actual flow
- Check supply-side airflow (insufficient supply causing lack of exhaust)
- Check duct static pressure at valve inlet
- Is the face velocity low because the valve physically cannot deliver enough CFM?

Fork 2 — Face velocity calculation/input problem:
- Is the sash dimension (height x width) correctly entered in commissioning parameters?
- Sash area calculation: face velocity = CFM ÷ sash area. Wrong sash dimension = wrong face velocity calculation.
- Is the sash sensor reading the correct position? Sensor mis-calibrated = wrong area calculation.
- Has sash been modified (new sash, different width) without updating parameters?

POWER RESTORATION — HALF ACMs OFFLINE:
After power restoration, half the ACMs on a PBC trunk show offline:
1. Wait: do not immediately start resetting devices. Allow PBC and ACMs to complete full boot sequence (up to 60-90 seconds for PBC, 30-60 seconds for ACMs).
2. Check power: verify 24VAC at the offline ACM locations.
3. Check MS/TP trunk: is the trunk physical wiring intact? A loose connector on the daisy chain can isolate half the trunk.
4. Check baud rate: if baud was changed on some devices, mismatched baud isolates those devices.
5. Check duplicate MACs: if two ACMs have the same MAC address, one wins and one goes offline.
6. Check RS485 termination: end-of-line resistors — if the midpoint of the daisy chain has an end-of-line resistor, it isolates the second half.
7. Force rediscovery/relink if needed after resolving physical issues.

30-ROOM RENOVATION RECOMMISSIONING STRATEGY:
1. Documentation first: gather as-built drawings, original commissioning records, Room Schedule Sheets, parameter backups.
2. Priority order: life-safety spaces (BSL labs, AII/PE rooms) first, then high-occupancy labs, then support spaces.
3. Phase: run parallel teams — one team commissions CSCP devices while another integrates Niagara.
4. Room-by-room checklist: each room signed off before moving to next.
5. Issue log: track every deficiency found — open items must be resolved before final acceptance.
6. Phased acceptance: owner/engineer can accept completed rooms while others are in progress.
7. Final turnover package: address schedules, calibration records, Niagara point list, alarm list, trend configuration, acceptance test results, as-built sequences.
8. Documentation is not optional — it is a deliverable for BSL spaces and typically required by the owner.

topic: Niagara 4 Platform Fundamentals — Architecture, Fox Protocol, Platform vs Station, Workbench Basics
content:
NIAGARA 4 PLATFORM FUNDAMENTALS

ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW:
Niagara 4 (N4) is a Java-based middleware framework for building automation. It runs on physical controllers (JACE 8000, JACE 9000) or servers (Supervisor, Enterprise Server). Every N4 device has two layers:
- Platform: the operating system layer — handles licensing, modules, file system, network config, time sync, backup/restore. Connect to Platform for administrative tasks.
- Station: the application layer — contains all programming, databases, schedules, alarms, histories, drivers, and logic. Connect to Station for engineering and operations.

WORKBENCH CONNECTION:
- To Platform: File > Open > Platform. Enter IP address, port 3011 (platform daemon port). Credentials: separate platform admin credentials.
- To Station: File > Open > Station. Enter IP address, port 4911 (Fox protocol). Default credentials: admin/admin — change immediately on new installations.
- Fox protocol (port 4911): the proprietary Niagara protocol used for Workbench-to-station communication. If port 4911 is blocked by a firewall or VPN, Workbench cannot connect to the station.
- Foxs (secure Fox): Fox over TLS, port 4911 with SSL. Required when connecting over the internet or untrusted networks.

PLATFORM TASKS (connect to Platform first):
- Install/update N4 modules (.jar files) — required when adding new driver or functionality
- Update Niagara firmware
- Configure network settings (IP address, hostname, DNS)
- Backup and restore station
- Manage licenses (Software Maintenance Agreement / SMA)
- Set time zone and NTP sync
- Install distribution files (.dist) for vendor modules (like Phoenix Controls Workbench modules)

STATION TASKS (connect to Station):
- Add/configure drivers (BACnet, LonWorks, Modbus, etc.)
- Create and edit programming (wire sheets, Px graphics, schedules)
- Manage histories, alarms, users
- Commission and test devices
- View live data and override points

NIAGARAAX vs NIAGARA 4:
NiagaraAX (AX): older version, reached End of Life April 30, 2020. No new licenses available. Cannot be upgraded to N4 directly — requires migration. AX and N4 are not compatible at the station level. AX used .bog files; N4 uses different file formats. If you encounter AX in the field: treat it as legacy, document carefully, plan migration to N4.
Niagara 4: current version. Actively developed. Requires SMA (Software Maintenance Agreement) for updates. Versions 4.1 through 4.15 and beyond. JACE 9000 requires minimum N4.13.

LICENSING:
- Each JACE requires a license for the number of BACnet points, drivers, and features in use
- SMA (Software Maintenance Agreement): annual maintenance contract for updates and tech support. Without valid SMA, firmware updates are blocked.
- License file (.jar): stored on Platform. View in Platform > Licensing.
- Point count exceeded: Niagara will warn or limit when point count exceeds license
- Check license before adding large point databases to ensure compliance

topic: Niagara 4 Drivers — BACnet, Modbus, LonWorks, Driver Architecture, Device Discovery
content:
NIAGARA 4 DRIVER ARCHITECTURE AND DEVICE DISCOVERY

DRIVER ARCHITECTURE:
Every third-party protocol in Niagara uses a Driver. Drivers live under Config > Drivers in the station nav tree. Each Driver contains a network object, under which discovered/added devices appear, under which proxy points map to device data points.
Structure: Drivers > [ProtocolNetwork] > [Device] > [ProxyPoints]

BACNET/IP DRIVER SETUP:
1. Add BacnetNetwork to Drivers (from BACnet palette) if not present
2. Set Enabled = true
3. Under BacnetNetwork > Local Device: set Local Device Instance (must be unique on the BACnet network), set network number (matches site BACnet IP network design), set UDP port (47808 default — must match all devices)
4. Under Network Adapters: bind to the correct network interface (if JACE has multiple NICs)
5. Discovery: right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover — discovers devices broadcasting on BACnet/IP
6. For MS/TP devices (like some Phoenix Controls ACMs or legacy devices): add BacnetMstpPort under BacnetNetwork. Set baud rate, port (RS485 COM port on JACE). Discover on the MS/TP port separately.
7. After discovery: drag found devices into station, or right-click > Add. Set device instance if not auto-populated.

BACNET DEVICE INSTANCE:
- Every BACnet device must have a unique Device Instance on the entire BACnet internetwork (not just subnet)
- Duplicate Device Instances: only one device responds to requests — other appears offline or intermittent
- For Phoenix Controls PBC: Device Instance configured during commissioning in Phoenix Controls Workbench
- In Niagara: verify the Device Instance in the device properties matches the actual PBC configuration

MODBUS DRIVER:
- ModbusAsyncNetwork (RTU over serial) or ModbusTcpNetwork (TCP/IP)
- Serial: set COM port, baud rate, data bits, stop bits, parity — must match slave device settings exactly
- TCP: set slave device IP and port (502 default)
- Each register mapped as a proxy point with register address, data type (Int16, Float32, etc.), and read/write attribute
- Holding registers: 4xxxxx address space, read/write
- Input registers: 3xxxxx, read only
- Coils: 0xxxxx, read/write Boolean
- Discrete inputs: 1xxxxx, read only Boolean

LONWORKS DRIVER (legacy, Celeris 1/2 integration):
- Requires LON network interface card or PCI8000 gateway
- LonWorks uses Neuron IDs for device identification
- Phoenix Controls Celeris 1: use Accel-Works (not LonMaker) for direct commissioning
- Phoenix Controls Celeris 2: use LonMaker for Windows
- In Niagara: PCI8000 bridges LonWorks to BACnet, allowing Niagara to see Celeris devices as BACnet objects
- Never use LonMaker to commission Celeris 1 — corrupts Celeris 1 database

PROXY POINT ATTRIBUTES:
- Read: proxy point polls the device and displays value (read-only)
- Read/Write: proxy point can also write commands to device
- COV: device sends updates on change rather than Niagara polling
- For BACnet writable objects (like PBC AO or BV points): proxy point must be writable type
- BACnet priority: when writing, specify priority array slot (1-16, lower number = higher priority)

topic: Niagara 4 Histories and Alarms — HistoryService, AlarmService, Extensions, Email, Routing Complete Reference
content:
NIAGARA 4 HISTORIES AND ALARMS — COMPLETE REFERENCE

HISTORY SERVICE SETUP:
1. HistoryService lives under Config > Services in the station
2. Each proxy point needing trending gets a HistoryExt added to it:
   - Right-click proxy point > New > Extension > select history extension type
   - NumericIntervalHistoryExt: logs at fixed time interval (most common for continuous values like CFM, temperature)
   - NumericCOVHistoryExt: logs when value changes by a specified amount (good for slow-changing setpoints)
   - BooleanCOVHistoryExt: logs on Boolean state change (good for alarms, occupancy)
3. Set extension properties:
   - Enabled: true
   - History Name: meaningful name — suggest format Building-Room-Point (e.g., B7-RM214-ExhCFM)
   - Interval: time between logs (1 min for critical flows, 5 min for general, 15 min for energy)
   - Capacity: how many records to store locally (larger = more disk space)
4. HistoryService > Database: view all histories, configure capacity limits, export data
5. To export history data: right-click history > Export > CSV or use Workbench history chart view

ALARM SERVICE SETUP:
1. AlarmService lives under Config > Services
2. Add AlarmService from palette if not present
3. Under AlarmService: add alarm recipients (who receives alarms) and alarm classes (severity/routing)
4. Add AlarmExt to proxy points needing alarming:
   - BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt: for Boolean points (flow alarm, comm fault, emergency active)
   - NumericChangeAlarmExt or OutOfRangeAlarmExt: for numeric points (CFM out of range, face velocity low)
5. Set extension properties:
   - Alarm Class: assign to appropriate class (LifeSafety, Pressurization, Flow, Communication)
   - Source Name: use meaningful name — same Building-Room-Point convention
   - Enabled: true
   - For OutOfRange: set high limit, low limit, and deadband
   - For ChangeOfState: set alarmed state (true or false)
6. Alarm Classes: create classes under AlarmService with priority levels. Lower priority number = higher urgency (1 = critical, higher numbers = lower priority).
7. Route alarm class to recipient: drag ConsoleRecipient, EmailRecipient, or custom recipient under AlarmService

EMAIL ALARM RECIPIENT SETUP:
1. Configure SMTP under AlarmService or via Services > SmtpService
2. Set: SMTP host (your mail server), port (25, 465, 587 depending on mail server), authentication if required
3. Add EmailRecipient under AlarmService
4. Set: To addresses (comma-separated), From address, Subject template
5. Route alarm class to EmailRecipient: alarm class > Route Alarm > select EmailRecipient
6. Test: force an alarm condition, verify email arrives
7. Common failures: SMTP port blocked by IT firewall, incorrect authentication, wrong server address

ALARM ROUTING WORKFLOW:
AlarmExt on point > triggers Alarm > assigned Alarm Class > routed to Recipient(s) (Console, Email, etc.)
- An alarm with no class assigned = goes nowhere
- A class with no recipient = goes nowhere
- Both must be configured and linked for alarms to reach operators

ACKNOWLEDGING ALARMS:
- In Niagara HMI or Workbench: Alarm Console shows active alarms
- Click alarm > Acknowledge (or Ack All)
- Some alarms require acknowledgement before they clear (latching alarms)
- Unacknowledged alarms persist in console even after condition clears
- Configure acknowledgement requirement per alarm class

topic: Niagara 4 Schedules, Px Graphics, UserService, Security, Backup/Restore
content:
NIAGARA 4 SCHEDULES, GRAPHICS, USERS, AND BACKUP

SCHEDULE SERVICE:
WeeklySchedule: defines recurring occupancy or setpoint changes by day of week and time.
1. Add WeeklySchedule from kitControl or schedule palette
2. Set output type: Boolean (occupied/unoccupied) or Numeric (setpoint value)
3. Configure each day: add time blocks with occupancy state (true/false) or setpoint value
4. Default output: what outputs when no schedule block is active (typically false/unoccupied)
5. Connect schedule output to BooleanWritable or NumericWritable in wire sheet

CalendarSchedule / SpecialEvent: overrides weekly schedule for holidays or special days.
1. Add CalendarSchedule, define dates (specific date, date range, or recurring holiday pattern)
2. Link CalendarSchedule to WeeklySchedule as a SpecialEvent override
3. On special event days, the special event output overrides the weekly schedule

PX GRAPHICS (Workbench Graphics Editor):
Px graphics are the operator interface screens in Niagara 4. They display live data and allow operators to adjust setpoints.
1. Create new Px file: right-click station > New > PxView
2. Use the Px editor to draw graphics: drag controls (labels, meters, buttons, images) onto canvas
3. Bind controls to points: drag proxy point onto label or widget to link live data
4. Navigate between Px screens: use NavFile or direct ORD links
5. HTML5 Hx UI: modern N4 interface using web browser — more performant than legacy Px for operators
6. For Phoenix Controls: create room graphics showing CFM actual, face velocity, offset, alarm status, mode
7. Good practice: one Px file per room or per floor, linked from a building overview graphic

USER SERVICE AND SECURITY:
1. UserService lives under Config > Services
2. Add users: right-click UserService > New > User
3. User properties: username, password, operator profile (set of permissions), roles
4. Operator profiles: define which categories of features a user can access (read-only, read/write, admin)
5. Nav file: assign each user a default nav file so they see the appropriate graphics on login
6. Admin user: the default admin account has full access — change default password immediately
7. Role-based access: create profiles that restrict operators from seeing or modifying engineering parameters

HTTPS AND CERTIFICATE MANAGEMENT:
1. Niagara uses TLS for web access and Foxs connections
2. Platform > Certificate Management: view, import, and manage certificates
3. When connecting Workbench to a new JACE: certificate prompt appears — approve only if you trust the device
4. Self-signed certificates: default for new JACEs. Browsers show security warning — acceptable for internal use.
5. For production systems: install site CA certificate for clean browser connections

BACKUP AND RESTORE:
Platform backup (recommended regularly — monthly minimum, before any major change):
1. Connect Workbench to Platform
2. Platform > Backup > Create Backup — downloads .dist file containing station and platform config
3. Store backup off-device (network share, laptop, cloud)
4. JACE 9000: auto-daily backup to internal storage — verify this is enabled
5. Restore: Platform > Backup > Restore — upload .dist file, confirm overwrite

Station-only backup:
1. Connect to Station
2. File > Export > Station (exports .bog or .backup file)
3. Restore: File > Import > Station — overwrites current station
4. Always backup before commissioning, before making large changes, before firmware updates

topic: Niagara 4 kitControl — All Key Blocks, Wire Sheet Patterns, Common Mistakes
content:
NIAGARA 4 KITCONTROL — COMPLETE BLOCK REFERENCE AND WIRE SHEET PATTERNS

MATH BLOCKS:
- Add: sums two or more numeric inputs. Outputs null if any input has bad status.
- Subtract: A minus B. Use for: offset calculation (exhaust minus supply), error calculation (setpoint minus actual).
- Multiply: A times B. Use for: scaling, unit conversion, efficiency calculations.
- Divide: A divided by B. Guard against divide-by-zero (B=0 outputs null/fault).
- Abs: absolute value. Use after Subtract to get magnitude of error regardless of direction.
- Math: multi-operation math block with expression editor. Use for complex calculations.
- Max: outputs the higher of two inputs. Use for: high-signal-select, maximum demand selection.
- Min: outputs the lower of two inputs. Use for: minimum floor, limiting maximum setback.
- Clamp: limits output between min and max bounds. Use on PID outputs or setpoints.
- Ramp: slows rate of change of an output. Use to prevent sudden valve position jumps.

COMPARISON BLOCKS:
- GreaterThan: outputs true if A > B. Use for: threshold alarms, mode switching.
- LessThan: outputs true if A < B.
- GreaterThanOrEqual, LessThanOrEqual: inclusive comparisons.
- Between: outputs true if value is between min and max.

BOOLEAN LOGIC BLOCKS:
- And: true if all inputs true.
- Or: true if any input true.
- Not: inverts Boolean input.
- Latch: Set/Reset latch. Set input = true latches output true. Reset = true clears output. Output stays until explicitly reset. Use for: emergency modes, latching alarms, manual reset required conditions.
- Delay: delays a Boolean signal by a specified time. Use for: startup delays, anti-short-cycle.
- EdgeTrigger: detects rising or falling edge of Boolean signal.

CONVERSION BLOCKS:
- BooleanToNumeric: converts Boolean to numeric value (configurable 0/1 or custom values). Use to drive a NumericWritable from a Boolean output.
- NumericToBoolean: converts numeric to Boolean based on threshold.
- NumericSwitch: selects between two numeric inputs based on a Boolean. Use for: occupied/unoccupied setpoint selection, face velocity setback.

WRITABLE BLOCKS (operator-adjustable values):
- NumericWritable: a numeric value with 16-level priority array. Operators or BACnet writes can override. Fallback value activates when no priority is written. Use for: setpoints, limits, offsets.
- BooleanWritable: same concept for Boolean values. Use for: occupancy commands, emergency commands, override flags.
- Priority array: level 1 (highest) to 16 (lowest, fallback). A write at level 8 overrides a write at level 14 but not level 5. Always check which priority level BACnet integrations write to.

COMMON WIRE SHEET MISTAKES:
1. Null propagation: any Add/Subtract/Math block input with bad status outputs null — check each input's status before troubleshooting the math block itself.
2. Wrong priority level: BACnet write at level 14 doesn't override a local override at level 8.
3. Latch not resetting: emergency latch left set from a previous test — operators must manually reset.
4. Units mismatch: mixing CFM and L/s, or Fahrenheit and Celsius in the same math — verify units/facets.
5. Feedback loop: accidentally connecting output back to input — causes unstable runaway behavior.
6. COV subscription dropped: after network interruption COV-based inputs show last-known value forever — add Force Read periodic action or switch to polling.
7. Output not writing: NumericWritable may have a local override at a higher priority blocking the wire sheet output — check all priority levels.
8. Scale factor wrong: BACnet AI for CFM may return raw value needing unit conversion — verify facets/scaling on proxy point.

topic: Niagara 4 JACE Hardware — JACE 8000, JACE 9000, Network Config, Default IPs, Commissioning
content:
NIAGARA 4 JACE HARDWARE REFERENCE

JACE 9000:
- Default IP: 192.168.1.140 (factory default on primary LAN port)
- Minimum Niagara version: 4.13 (will not run earlier versions)
- Processor: Quad-core ARM Cortex-A53 (significantly more powerful than JACE 8000)
- Supports Niagara 5 (future-proof for upgrade path)
- Auto daily backup: enabled by default, stores to internal eMMC — verify it is enabled on commissioning
- Two Ethernet ports: can be used for separate IT and controls networks (IT isolation)
- USB port: for backup files, module installation, factory restore
- Expansion module: optional I/O expansion via DIN rail module
- Commissioning first connection: connect laptop directly to JACE 9000 Ethernet port, set laptop to 192.168.1.x subnet, browse to 192.168.1.140:8443 or open Workbench > Platform at 192.168.1.140
- Platform port: 3011 (or HTTPS 443 for web platform)
- Station/Fox port: 4911

JACE 8000:
- No fixed default IP — set during initial commissioning via USB or direct Ethernet connection
- Niagara version support: 4.1 through 4.15
- Does NOT support Niagara 5
- Processor: single-core ARM (slower than JACE 9000 — more susceptible to poll scheduler overload)
- EOL Americas: December 2026 — plan migration to JACE 9000 for new projects
- USB backup: not supported on units converted from AX via migration tool
- Two Ethernet ports: same IT/controls isolation capability as JACE 9000
- RS485 ports: available for MS/TP or Modbus RTU directly from JACE (no external USB-RS485 needed)

NETWORK CONFIGURATION:
1. Connect to Platform (port 3011 or web interface)
2. Platform > Network > configure primary LAN IP (static recommended for controllers)
3. Set subnet mask, gateway, DNS
4. If two NICs: configure each separately — one for IT/BMS network, one for controls devices network
5. Hostname: set to meaningful name (site-building-jace-01)
6. NTP time sync: configure under Platform > Time — critical for historian timestamps and alarm times
7. Firewall/IT requirements to open for Niagara operation:
   - Port 4911 (Fox): Workbench to station, station-to-station communication
   - Port 443 or 8443 (HTTPS): web HMI access
   - Port 3011 (Platform): Workbench platform administration
   - Port 47808 (UDP): BACnet/IP device communication
   - Port 25/465/587: SMTP for email alarms (outbound only)

FACTORY RESET:
- Hold reset button on JACE for 10-30 seconds (varies by model) to wipe station and return to factory defaults
- Use only as last resort — destroys all programming
- Alternatively: Platform > Factory Reset (requires platform admin access)
- After factory reset: reconfigure network, reinstall modules, restore station from backup

FINDING A JACE IP WHEN UNKNOWN:
1. Check network switch for MAC address and DHCP lease table
2. Use arp -a on a laptop on the same subnet after connecting
3. Use Niagara Workbench > File > Open > Platform > Discover to scan subnet for Niagara platforms
4. Connect directly with laptop set to 192.168.1.x subnet (for JACE 9000 at factory default)
5. Check as-built documentation or previous commissioning records

topic: Niagara 4 General Commissioning Checklist — Platform Admin, Station Setup, Driver Verification, Turnover
content:
NIAGARA 4 GENERAL COMMISSIONING CHECKLIST

PLATFORM ADMINISTRATION (do these first, before any station work):
☐ Connect to Platform — verify firmware version matches project requirements
☐ Change default platform admin password — never leave admin/admin in production
☐ Set static IP address, subnet mask, gateway — document in as-built
☐ Set hostname — meaningful, consistent naming convention
☐ Configure NTP time server — critical for historian and alarm timestamps
☐ Verify SMA (Software Maintenance Agreement) is current — check license expiry
☐ Install required modules (.jar files) for all drivers to be used
☐ Enable auto-backup (JACE 9000) — verify backup schedule
☐ Take initial Platform backup — store off-device

STATION SETUP:
☐ Change default station admin password — admin/admin is not acceptable
☐ Create user accounts with appropriate permissions — no shared passwords
☐ Assign nav files to user accounts — operators see correct screens
☐ Configure SMTP (email) service if alarm email required
☐ Set station properties: site name, location, contact
☐ Configure AlarmService: alarm classes, recipients, routing
☐ Configure HistoryService: capacity limits, database settings
☐ Add ScheduleService if scheduling required

DRIVER AND DEVICE VERIFICATION:
☐ BacnetNetwork Enabled = true
☐ Local Device Instance unique on entire BACnet internetwork
☐ UDP port 47808 — verify not blocked by IT firewall
☐ BBMD configured if devices on different subnet/VLAN
☐ All target devices discovered and added to station
☐ Each device: correct Device Instance verified, Enabled = true
☐ Proxy points created for all required points
☐ Proxy point status: all showing live data (not stale, null, fault)
☐ Tuning policies assigned: Fast for safety, Normal for general, Slow for trends
☐ Write points tested: BACnet AO/BV/MSO proxy points can be written and device responds

HISTORY VERIFICATION:
☐ HistoryExt added to all points requiring trending
☐ History names follow naming convention
☐ Trends collecting — verify data in HistoryService after 15 minutes
☐ History intervals appropriate for point type
☐ Export test: verify history data can be exported

ALARM VERIFICATION:
☐ AlarmExt added to all critical points
☐ Alarm classes configured and linked to recipients
☐ Each alarm tested: force condition, verify appears in Alarm Console
☐ Email alarm test: verify email received at correct addresses
☐ Alarm acknowledge workflow tested

GRAPHICS AND SCHEDULE VERIFICATION:
☐ Px or HMI graphics display live data — no null or stale values
☐ Operator-writable setpoints can be changed from graphics
☐ Schedules tested: advance time, verify occupancy mode changes correctly
☐ Schedule overrides work as designed

TURNOVER DOCUMENTATION:
☐ As-built point list: every proxy point with BACnet object, device, address
☐ Alarm list: every AlarmExt with description, class, recipient
☐ History list: every HistoryExt with interval, location
☐ User accounts list: username, role, nav file
☐ Network documentation: JACE IP, subnet, BBMD settings
☐ Password list (stored securely): platform admin, station admin
☐ Backup: final station backup taken and stored off-device
☐ Platform backup: taken and stored off-device

topic: Niagara 4 General Troubleshooting — Connection Failures, Point Issues, Performance, Common Errors
content:
NIAGARA 4 GENERAL TROUBLESHOOTING REFERENCE

WORKBENCH CANNOT CONNECT TO STATION (Fox port 4911):
- Firewall blocking port 4911 — check IT firewall rules between Workbench laptop and JACE
- Wrong IP address — verify JACE IP hasn't changed (DHCP lease expired, IP conflict)
- Station not running — connect to Platform first, verify station is started
- Certificate rejection — Workbench shows certificate warning, must approve to continue
- N4 version mismatch — very old Workbench connecting to newer N4 may fail
- Authentication failure — wrong username/password, account locked
- Fox vs Foxs — if station requires Foxs (secure), plain Fox connection will fail
- Network unreachable — verify laptop and JACE are on same subnet or routing is configured

PROXY POINT SHOWING STALE/NULL/FAULT:
Run through this sequence:
1. Is the device online? Check device status under BacnetNetwork.
2. Is the BacnetNetwork itself enabled and connected?
3. Is the proxy point enabled? (Not manually disabled)
4. Is there a BACnet path from JACE to device? (Network/VLAN/BBMD)
5. Force Read: right-click > Actions > Force Read — does value update?
6. Check the BACnet object type and instance — correct for this point?
7. Check Device Instance — correct device?
8. Check tuning policy — Slow? Disabled? Switch to Normal, recheck.
9. Verify COV subscription active — if COV-based, may need to restart driver
10. Network-level check: can JACE ping the device IP?

PROXY POINT STUCK ON OLD VALUE:
- COV subscription dropped — device stopped sending updates, Niagara shows last received value
- Solution: Force Read to get current value. Restart BacnetNetwork driver to re-subscribe COV.
- Alternative: switch from COV to polling so Niagara actively requests value on schedule

POINT SHOWS VALUE BUT OVERRIDDEN/NOT RESPONDING TO WRITES:
- Check priority array: something at higher priority is overriding the write
- Right-click proxy point > View > BACnet Priority Array — see all 16 priority levels
- Find the override: which priority slot has a non-null value blocking your write?
- Release the override: write null to that priority slot, or use emergency relinquish
- Common cause: emergency mode activated at priority 3, normal operations write at priority 14 — priority 3 wins

PERFORMANCE ISSUES (slow Workbench, high CPU):
- Check poll scheduler: above 75% = overloaded. Reduce Fast bucket points.
- Too many histories collecting: reduce interval or disable non-critical histories
- Too many COV subscriptions: some devices have limits on concurrent COV subscriptions
- JACE 8000 limitation: slower processor, will show high CPU before JACE 9000 does
- Station too large: consider splitting large stations across multiple JACEs
- Module conflict: a misbehaving module can consume excessive resources

STATION FAILS TO START:
- Check Platform log for error messages (Platform > Logs)
- Missing module: station references a module not installed on Platform — install the module
- License exceeded: too many points or features for current license
- Corrupt station file: restore from last known good backup
- Java heap error: station too large for available memory — optimize or upgrade hardware

COMMON N4 ERROR MESSAGES:
- "Component not found at ORD": the ORD path references a component that was deleted or renamed — update the ORD reference
- "UnresolvedException": wire sheet connection target was removed — find and fix broken wire
- "Failed to authenticate": wrong password or account locked
- "Certificate not trusted": approve certificate in Allowed Hosts (certmanagerservice)
- "Could not resolve slot": usually a renamed or deleted slot — fix in wire sheet or nav file

topic: Niagara 4 BACnet Driver — BacnetNetwork, Drivers, Discover, Enabled, UDP 47808, Proxy Point, Tuning Policy (Keyword Dense)
content:
NIAGARA 4 BACNET DRIVER — COMPLETE KEYWORD REFERENCE

EXACT STEPS — BACNET DRIVER SETUP IN NIAGARA 4:
1. Open Workbench. Connect to Station. Navigate to Config > Drivers.
2. From the BACnet palette, drag BacnetNetwork onto Drivers if not present.
3. In BacnetNetwork properties: set Enabled = true. This is required — driver does nothing if disabled.
4. Set Local Device Instance (unique on entire BACnet internetwork), UDP port 47808 (default BACnet/IP port — must match all devices and not be blocked by firewall).
5. If PBCs are on different subnet/VLAN: add BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device) table entries in BacnetNetwork so discovery crosses VLAN boundaries. Without BBMD, remote-subnet PBCs will not appear in Discover.
6. Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30-60 seconds.
7. Discovered PBCs appear in the discovery list. Add each device. Verify Device Instance matches PBC configuration.
8. Under each PBC device: create proxy points for each BACnet object (AI, AO, BI, BV, MSO, MSV) needed.
9. Assign tuning policy to each proxy point:
   - Fast (≈1 second): life safety points — pressurization alarms, flow alarms, emergency mode. Life safety points must use Fast or 1-second poll so Niagara detects critical conditions immediately.
   - Normal: general monitoring — CFM actual, face velocity, setpoints, status points.
   - Slow: energy reporting, noncritical trends, points that don't affect safety response.
10. Poll scheduler health: keep below 75%. Above 75% = overloaded. At 92% = critically overloaded.

POLL SCHEDULER AT 92% — CAUSES AND REMEDIATION:
Causes: network change (VLAN segmentation) breaking BBMD path → devices go offline → Niagara retries constantly → overloads Fast bucket. Also: too many points in Fast bucket, broken COV subscriptions polling as fallback, offline MS/TP devices retrying, JACE 9000 CPU or memory pressure.
Remediation: (1) restore network/BBMD first, (2) move noncritical points from Fast to Normal or Slow, (3) fix offline devices, (4) repair COV subscriptions, (5) remove unnecessary high-frequency polling. JACE 9000 has more resources than JACE 8000 but can still be overloaded by poor poll configuration.

PBC NOT IN DISCOVERY — ALL SIX CAUSES:
1. BBMD not configured — PBC on different subnet/VLAN, broadcasts not reaching Niagara
2. Device Instance conflict — duplicate Device Instance, one device shadows another
3. BacnetNetwork Enabled = false — driver not running, no discovery possible
4. Baud rate mismatch on MS/TP trunk — PBC cannot communicate on trunk
5. RS485 termination/end-of-line missing or wrong — MS/TP trunk signal integrity failure
6. Duplicate MAC address on MS/TP — two devices collide, neither responds reliably
Also: IP unreachable (wrong subnet, firewall blocking UDP 47808), PBC still booting, PBC in fault state.

topic: Niagara 4 Tuning Policies — Fast/Normal/Slow, Life Safety 1 Second, Poll Scheduler 75%, Point Classification
content:
NIAGARA 4 TUNING POLICIES — COMPLETE CLASSIFICATION

RULE: Life safety points must use Fast (1-second) tuning policy.

FAST TUNING POLICY (~1 second):
- Life safety: pressurization alarms, flow alarms, face velocity alarms, emergency mode active/inactive
- Room pressure differential (when driving safety response)
- Emergency exhaust command — must respond within 1 second to fire alarm or safety event
- Communication fault alarms — detect loss of PBC communication immediately
Reason: 1-second poll means Niagara detects a life-safety condition within 1 second. Using Normal (30-60 seconds) on a pressurization alarm means the BMS is blind to a dangerous condition for up to a minute.

NORMAL TUNING POLICY (~30-60 seconds default):
- CFM actual values (for monitoring and trending — not driving safety response)
- Face velocity actual (for operator display and trending)
- Setpoints (OccupancyCmd, OffsetSP — operator changes, not real-time control)
- Mode status (occupancy mode, valve state)
- General diagnostic points

SLOW TUNING POLICY (minutes):
- Energy reporting points — total CFM for energy calculations
- Noncritical trends — temperature, humidity trending
- Points that don't affect operator response time or safety sequences
- Archive/compliance reporting data

POLL SCHEDULER THRESHOLDS:
- Under 75%: healthy — acceptable load
- 75-90%: warning — investigate before problems occur, identify heavy poll sources
- 92%+: critically overloaded — take immediate action
  At 92%: points start being polled late or skipped. Alarms may be delayed. Stale values accumulate.
  JACE 9000 handles heavier load than JACE 8000 due to quad-core processor, but is not immune.

COV vs POLLING:
COV (Change of Value): device sends update when value changes. Reduces poll scheduler load. Best for slowly changing setpoints and binary status. Risk: COV subscription can drop after network interruption — point shows last-known value indefinitely (appears current but is stale).
Polling: Niagara requests value on schedule. Reliable, predictable. Higher poll scheduler load.
Recommendation for Phoenix Controls: use COV for setpoints and binary status, polling for CFM and face velocity actual values. Always set a fallback poll rate on COV subscriptions.

DIAGNOSING LOCAL CORRECT / NIAGARA WRONG:
1. Right-click proxy point > Actions > Force Read — if value updates, poll is working but infrequent
2. Check tuning policy — Slow policy means Niagara may not update for minutes
3. Check Device Instance — proxy point may be reading from wrong PBC
4. Check COV subscription — may have dropped, showing stale cached value
5. Check point status (stale, null, fault, disabled, overridden)
6. Verify BACnet object type and instance number mapping is correct

topic: CSCP PBC Firmware — Version Identification, Upgrade Procedure, Compatibility
content:
CSCP PBC FIRMWARE — VERSION IDENTIFICATION, UPGRADE, AND COMPATIBILITY

HOW TO DETERMINE FIRMWARE VERSION IN THE FIELD:
Method 1 — Phoenix Controls Workbench:
1. Connect Workbench to PBC platform or station (IP address, port 4911)
2. Navigate to platform > About or check the station firmware version in platform properties
3. Workbench displays current firmware version number

Method 2 — PBC label:
- Physical label on PBC enclosure or board shows firmware version installed at factory
- Label is not updated when firmware is upgraded — use Workbench for current version

Method 3 — Flow Manager App (Bluetooth):
- Connect Flow Manager App to PBC via Bluetooth
- App displays connected device firmware version in device info screen

KEY VERSION DIFFERENCES AND COMPATIBILITY:
- Workbench 3.0+ required: to commission any CSCP device (PBC, ACM, FHD500). Earlier Workbench versions cannot communicate with CSCP.
- Workbench 5.0+ required: for PBC DDC programming (wire sheets, function blocks, advanced logic)
- Workbench 5.2.1+ required: for CSCP Valve Upgrade Kit flow curve import (.VPT files). Earlier versions will not complete the upgrade procedure.
- Firmware compatibility: PBC firmware must be compatible with Workbench version in use. Check Phoenix Controls release notes for firmware/Workbench compatibility matrix.
- ACM and FHD500 firmware: each has independent firmware. All should be updated to current versions before commissioning. Mismatched firmware between ACM and PBC can cause communication issues.
- Upgrade procedure: connect Workbench to PBC platform > Software Manager > install firmware .jar file > commit > reboot. Always backup station before firmware upgrade.
- After upgrade: verify all ACMs reconnect, all FHD500s relink, all Niagara proxy points resume normal data.
- Compatibility testing: test all critical functions after upgrade in a controlled window before returning system to service.

topic: FHM631 vs FHM632 Board Differences, Er_c Factory Reset, Parameter 1, Input/Output Compatibility
content:
FHM631 vs FHM632 — COMPLETE DIFFERENCES AND ER_C DIAGNOSIS

ER_C ON FHM631 — COMPLETE EXPLANATION:
Er_c means the FHM631 board is in its factory/uncommissioned state. It has never been commissioned OR it has been factory reset. This is not a generic calibration error — it specifically means no commissioning data is present on the board.
Keywords: factory, uncommissioned, Parameter 1, 23 parameters, calibration required.

What Er_c requires:
1. Connect commissioning tool (HVAC PRO or equivalent for X30 series)
2. Set Parameter 1: defines the operating mode (VAV, CV, two-state, drive). This is the first and most critical parameter — all other parameters depend on it.
3. Enter all 23 calibration parameters per the Room Schedule Sheet: face velocity setpoint, flow setpoints (min/max), sash sensor calibration points, alarm setpoints, energy waste settings, beeper settings, ZPS configuration if applicable.
4. Without Parameter 1 set, the board will not operate — it remains in Er_c state.

FHM631 vs FHM632 — KEY DIFFERENCES:
FHM631:
- Primary board + secondary board (two-board assembly)
- Analog outputs: supports analog exhaust and supply valve control
- Compatible with: standard X30 VAV, CV, and two-state applications with analog valve control
- Inputs: analog sash sensor, ZPS, temperature, alarm
- Most common X30 replacement board

FHM632:
- Single-board design (replacement for FHM631 in newer production)
- Digital/network outputs: designed for network-based control, not analog valve control
- Compatibility: check project documentation carefully — FHM632 is not a drop-in replacement for all FHM631 applications
- Use FHM632 when: project specifies digital control, analog valve control not required, or FHM631 is specified as the replacement part
- Input compatibility: verify sensor types and wiring match FHM632 specifications

WHEN TO USE EACH:
Use FHM631: when replacing an existing FHM631 with analog valve control, when project documentation specifies FHM631, when analog output to valve is required.
Use FHM632: when project specifically calls for FHM632, when upgrading to digital/network control, when verified compatible with all existing sensors and actuators.
Critical: verify input type compatibility (sash sensor, temperature sensor types) and output type (analog vs digital) before substituting FHM632 for FHM631.

topic: Celeris Hunting — PID Gain, Deadband, Calibration, Complete Tuning Procedure
content:
CELERIS HUNTING — COMPLETE PID DIAGNOSIS AND TUNING

HUNTING DEFINITION: oscillation between two CFM values (e.g., 80-120 CFM) continuously. The control loop is chasing its own correction. Classic unstable PID behavior.

COMPLETE DIAGNOSTIC SEQUENCE (in order):
1. Sash sensor stability: is sash position signal stable and noise-free? Noise in sash input constantly changes face velocity setpoint → loop never stabilizes. Check sash sensor wiring and calibration.
2. Feedback signal quality: trend the Vpot signal. Is it smooth or noisy? Noisy Vpot = noisy feedback = hunting. Check Vpot wiring, coupling tightness, shaft alignment.
3. Calibration accuracy: verify zero-flow calibration. If the zero-flow calibration is off, the controller has an offset error that the integrator keeps trying to correct → hunting. Recalibrate if in doubt.
4. PID gain too high (Kp): the proportional gain is amplifying small errors into large corrections. Reduce Kp first — this is the most common cause of hunting after mechanical issues are ruled out.
