5. Deadband too small: if deadband is narrow, small flow variations trigger corrections. Increase deadband to allow minor fluctuations without response.
6. Integral windup: Ti (integral time) setting may be aggressive. Increase Ti (longer integration time) to slow the integral response.
7. Actuator: is the pneumatic actuator responding sluggishly or inconsistently? Variable actuator response can destabilize the loop. Check I/P, supply pressure, tubing, orifice.
8. Mechanical play: loose pivot arm, worn linkage, or excessive backlash amplifies control noise into position error. Inspect and tighten all mechanical connections.

TUNING PROCEDURE:
1. Set deadband to a moderately wide value first to stop active hunting
2. Reduce Kp (gain) in steps — after each step, wait 2-3 minutes and observe
3. Increase Ti (integral time) if oscillation continues after reducing Kp
4. Narrow deadband back gradually once gain is stable
5. Trend: command signal, feedback (Vpot), sash position, and actual CFM simultaneously
6. Look for: which signal oscillates first? That identifies the root cause signal.
7. After tuning: run through full sash range (fully closed to fully open) and verify no hunting at any position
8. Document: final Kp, Ti, deadband values on commissioning record

topic: MIJ Signal Chain — 4-20mA, Actuator, TB Terminal Block, Complete Flow from PBC to Valve
content:
MIJ SIGNAL CHAIN — COMPLETE REFERENCE

COMPLETE SIGNAL CHAIN (PBC command to valve position):
PBC outputs flow command signal > MIJ receives command at terminal block (TB) > MIJ outputs 4-20 mA current loop signal > 4-20 mA drives I/P transducer > I/P converts 4-20 mA to 3-15 PSI pneumatic signal > pneumatic signal strokes actuator > actuator moves valve cone to position > valve position creates flow rate > flow feedback (Vpot or flow sensor) returns to MIJ/PBC > feedback closes the control loop.

KEY SIGNAL DETAILS:
- MIJ terminal block (TB): the physical wiring interface. Flow Command is red wire at TB pin 4. Always reference terminal block (TB) when describing MIJ wiring — not just wire color alone.
- 4-20 mA: the electrical signal between MIJ and I/P. 4 mA = minimum (0% open), 20 mA = maximum (100% open).
- 3-15 PSI: the pneumatic signal from I/P to actuator. 3 PSI = spring position (fail position), 15 PSI = full stroke.
- Actuator: converts pneumatic pressure to mechanical position. Actuator type determines fail-safe position.
- Feedback: flow feedback (4-20 mA from flow transducer) or position feedback (Vpot) closes the loop. Without feedback the MIJ operates open-loop.

FHM631 REPLACEMENT — PARAMETER RESTORATION:
When replacing FHM631, restore must include:
- Parameter 1 (operating mode): must be set first — defines all subsequent parameters
- MAC/network address: must match original or be reassigned without duplicate conflict
- Baud rate: must match rest of network
- All 23 calibration parameters: from backup, Room Schedule Sheet, or photographed display
- Sash sensor calibration: two-point calibration (fully closed, fully open position voltage)
- Alarm setpoints, beeper settings, energy waste settings
- After restore: verify communication, verify face velocity and flow at setpoint
- Document: replacement date, new board serial number, who performed restoration

topic: BSL-3 Commissioning — Negative Pressure, Redundancy, Alarm, Monitored, Safety Requirements
content:
BSL-3 COMMISSIONING — COMPLETE SAFETY REQUIREMENTS

BSL-3 IS NOT A STANDARD LAB — ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENTS:

NEGATIVE PRESSURE (MANDATORY):
- BSL-3 must maintain continuous negative pressure relative to adjacent spaces at all times
- Negative pressure: exhaust CFM must exceed supply CFM by the verified design offset
- Monitored: pressure differential must be continuously monitored with a calibrated sensor
- Alarm: any positive pressure excursion (loss of negative pressure) must trigger an immediate life-safety alarm
- "Verified" means the system proves negative pressure is maintained — not just commanded

REDUNDANCY REQUIREMENTS:
- Redundant exhaust: BSL-3 typically requires a backup/redundant exhaust fan or system that activates automatically on primary fan failure
- Redundant monitoring: backup pressure sensor or monitoring path so a single sensor failure doesn't blind the safety system
- Redundant power: UPS or backup power for critical controls and fans
- Verify redundancy activation: during commissioning, simulate primary exhaust failure and confirm backup activates within required time (per specification)

ALARM AND MONITORING REQUIREMENTS:
- Every critical point has AlarmExt with life-safety alarm class (highest priority)
- Alarm routing: alarms must reach designated personnel (facility management, lab safety officer, EHS)
- Alarm types: positive pressure excursion, loss of exhaust, communication fault, valve fault, power loss
- Alarm acknowledgement: define who must acknowledge BSL-3 alarms and response procedure
- Alarm testing: each alarm must be tested during commissioning — force condition, verify alarm appears at correct recipient within required time

FAIL-SAFE BEHAVIOR:
- Every valve must fail to a defined safe position on power loss
- BSL-3 exhaust valves: typically fail open (maximum exhaust) to maintain containment
- BSL-3 supply valves: typically fail closed or to minimum to maintain negative pressure
- Test fail-safe: remove power from actuator, verify valve goes to correct position, verify pressure remains negative

DOCUMENTATION (REGULATORY REQUIREMENT):
- BSL-3 commissioning records are regulatory documents — not optional
- Every test must be witnessed, measured, signed, and dated
- Documentation includes: pressure measurements at every test, alarm test results, redundancy test results, fail-safe verification, calibration records
- Turnover package: complete as-built sequences, alarm list, monitoring plan, emergency procedures

topic: 20-Room CSCP Commissioning with Niagara 4 — BACnet, Proxy Points, Historian, Alarms, Face Velocity, Pressurization
content:
20-ROOM CSCP COMMISSIONING WITH NIAGARA 4 — COMPLETE SEQUENCE

COMPLETE SEQUENCE INCLUDING ALL KEYWORD ELEMENTS:

PHASE 1 — DEVICE SETUP:
- Assign unique MAC addresses to all ACMs (document address schedule)
- Set baud rate consistent across all devices on each MS/TP trunk
- Verify PBC-to-ACM communication: all ACMs online in PBC device list
- Link FHD500 to ACM/PBC via Setup Wizard: language, PIN, baud, sash, face velocity setpoint, alarm, pairing sequence
- Verify face velocity at FHD500 matches designed setpoint
- Verify pressurization/offset: exhaust CFM minus supply CFM equals design offset

PHASE 2 — NIAGARA 4 INTEGRATION (per room):
- Add PBC under BacnetNetwork in Niagara Drivers — Enabled = true
- Set Device Instance, verify it matches PBC configuration
- Discover and add all BACnet objects
- Create proxy points: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, offset actual, valve states, alarms (flow alarm, face velocity alarm, communication fault), occupancy command, emergency command
- Assign tuning policies: Fast (1 second) for life safety points (pressurization alarm, face velocity alarm, emergency), Normal for CFM actual and face velocity display, Slow for energy reporting

PHASE 3 — HISTORIAN (HistoryExt) ON EVERY CRITICAL POINT:
- Exhaust CFM actual: 1-minute interval
- Supply CFM actual: 1-minute interval
- Face velocity actual: 1-minute interval
- Room offset/pressurization: 1-minute interval
- Alarm states: BooleanCOVHistoryExt
- Naming convention: Building-Room-EquipmentTag-PointName

PHASE 4 — ALARMS (AlarmExt) ON SAFETY POINTS:
- Flow alarm (BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt)
- Face velocity low alarm
- Communication fault alarm
- Pressurization deviation (offset out of tolerance)
- Route all alarms to life-safety alarm class
- Test each alarm: force condition, verify appears in Niagara alarm console and at recipient

PHASE 5 — ACCEPTANCE:
- Face velocity: verify ±10% of setpoint at specified sash opening, document measured value
- Pressurization/offset: verify design offset CFM maintained
- Alarm response: force and verify each alarm within required time
- Sign off each room — documentation is a deliverable, not optional

topic: Niagara 4 Wire Sheet — GreaterThan, AlarmExt, NumericWritable, Design Offset, Kp, Action, Direct, Reverse
content:
NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET — KEYWORD-DENSE COMPLETE REFERENCE

VOLUMETRIC OFFSET MONITORING — COMPLETE WITH ALL KEYWORDS:
1. Supply CFM proxy point (Normal tuning policy)
2. Exhaust CFM proxy point (Normal or Fast depending on safety requirements)
3. Subtract block: Exhaust CFM minus Supply CFM = Actual Offset
4. NumericWritable named DesignOffset: operator sets required design offset CFM here (e.g., 150 CFM). NumericWritable has 16-level priority array — operator sets at priority 14, emergency override at priority 8 or higher.
5. Second Subtract: Actual Offset minus DesignOffset = Offset Error
6. GreaterThan block: compares Offset Error to threshold (e.g., 50 CFM). Outputs true when offset error exceeds tolerance.
7. BooleanWritable: receives GreaterThan output — true = offset out of tolerance
8. AlarmExt on BooleanWritable: alarm activates when offset drifts beyond threshold. Assign to pressurization alarm class. Route to appropriate recipient.
9. HistoryExt on Actual Offset, DesignOffset, and Offset Error: trend to see historical pressurization performance.

EMERGENCY EXHAUST LATCH — ALL KEYWORDS:
1. Fire alarm or emergency BooleanWritable (or BI proxy point)
2. Latch block: Set input = fire alarm, Reset input = manual reset BooleanWritable
3. Latch output drives emergency exhaust BooleanWritable
4. BooleanWritable Priority: write at Priority 3 (emergency override) to force exhaust to maximum — this overrides all lower priorities (operator writes at 8, schedule at 14)
5. AlarmExt on emergency command: alarm when emergency activates, routes to life-safety class
6. HistoryExt on latch output: records when emergency activated and when manually reset

FACE VELOCITY SETBACK — ZPS AND PBC INTEGRATION:
1. ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) or PBC occupancy output provides occupancy Boolean
2. PBC BACnet BV proxy point for occupancy or ZPS input
3. NumericSwitch: Boolean input selects between 100 fpm (occupied) and 60 fpm (unoccupied)
4. Output drives face velocity setpoint NumericWritable (written at appropriate Priority)
5. Override: operator BooleanWritable at higher priority can force occupied mode

LOOPPOINT PID — KP, ACTION, DIRECT, REVERSE:
Key parameters:
- Kp (Proportional gain): how aggressively the controller responds to error. Start conservative (0.1-0.5 for HVAC). High Kp = hunting/overshoot.
- Ti (Integral time): time for integrator to correct steady-state error. Start at 300-600 seconds. Shorter Ti = faster but more oscillation.
- Controlled Variable: the measurement being controlled (temperature, CFM, pressure)
- Setpoint: target value
- Output: drives the controlled device (valve, damper, fan speed)
- Action — Direct vs Reverse:
  DIRECT action: output increases as controlled variable increases above setpoint. Use for: COOLING (output increases as temperature rises above cooling setpoint), fan speed control for positive pressure.
  REVERSE action: output increases as controlled variable decreases below setpoint. Use for: HEATING (output increases as temperature falls below heating setpoint), most pressure control loops.
  Wrong action = runaway: if action is backwards, the loop drives in the wrong direction until it saturates.

topic: Controls Logic — Occupancy Override, Schedule, DCV Override, Sash Open Sequence ACM/PBC/Pressurization
content:
CONTROLS LOGIC — OCCUPANCY, SCHEDULE, AND SASH OPEN SEQUENCE

OCCUPANCY SCHEDULING IN NIAGARA 4 — COMPLETE WITH KEYWORDS:
1. WeeklySchedule: defines occupied/unoccupied times by day of week. Output is Boolean (true = occupied).
2. BooleanWritable: receives WeeklySchedule output at Priority 14 (schedule priority).
3. kitControl logic: if BooleanWritable = true, use occupied setpoints (100 fpm face velocity, normal offset); if false, use setback setpoints (60 fpm, reduced offset).
4. ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) override: ZPS detects presence in room → writes true to BooleanWritable at Priority 8 (higher than schedule at 14) → forces occupied mode regardless of schedule.
5. 24/7 labs: either tie occupied input to constant true, or set WeeklySchedule to all-occupied, or force BooleanWritable at Priority 14 to true permanently.
6. Zone: the scheduling zone groups multiple rooms under one schedule — critical for multi-room labs.
7. HistoryService: trend occupancy mode changes to verify schedule and ZPS are working correctly.
8. AlarmService: optionally alarm if occupancy mode is forced outside normal schedule (unauthorized override).

DCV WITH OCCUPANCY AND SCHEDULE OVERRIDE:
DCV reduces ventilation when CO2 or occupancy is low. Occupancy and schedule provide override capability:
- Schedule sets base occupied/unoccupied ventilation level
- ZPS or CO2 sensor can override schedule to increase ventilation when presence detected
- Pressurization setpoint is a floor that DCV cannot violate — minimum offset maintained regardless of DCV reduction
- Override: if operator needs to force ventilation, BooleanWritable at Priority 8 or higher overrides both schedule (14) and DCV logic

COMPLETE SASH OPEN SEQUENCE — ACM AND PBC ROLES:
When fume hood sash is raised from 0 to full open in a CSCP system:
1. Sash sensor detects sash position change — sends signal to FHD500 and ACM
2. ACM receives sash position input (via UIO on ACM or FHD500)
3. ACM calculates required exhaust CFM: face velocity setpoint × sash opening area = required CFM
4. ACM commands exhaust valve to stroke to deliver required CFM (high-speed response: <1 second)
5. Exhaust valve strokes, Vpot confirms position, flow delivered
6. ACM reports actual exhaust CFM to PBC via MS/TP
7. PBC receives updated exhaust CFM from all ACMs in zone
8. PBC recalculates zone total exhaust CFM
9. PBC adjusts supply command to maintain pressurization offset (exhaust minus supply = design offset CFM)
10. Supply valve adjusts to new supply setpoint
11. PBC reports zone offset to Niagara via BACnet proxy points
12. Niagara displays updated exhaust CFM, supply CFM, offset, and face velocity
Result: face velocity is maintained at setpoint despite sash position change, and pressurization offset is maintained throughout the response.

topic: Troubleshooting — Pressure Units w.c./inches, Sash Dimension Calculation, ZPS Fire Wire Sheet, MAC Duplicate Baud Restart
content:
TROUBLESHOOTING — KEYWORD-DENSE REFERENCE

PRESSURE UNITS — ALWAYS USE INCHES W.C.:
When specifying Phoenix Controls venturi valve accuracy pressure requirements:
- Low-pressure valves: minimum 0.1 inches w.c. (in. w.c.) across valve — accuracy not guaranteed below 0.3 in. w.c.
- Medium-pressure valves: minimum 0.3 inches w.c. (in. w.c.) for guaranteed ±5% accuracy
- Always express as: "0.3 in. w.c." or "0.3 inches w.c." — not just "0.3 WC" or "0.3" alone
- Equivalent: 0.3 in. w.c. = approximately 75 Pa

FACE VELOCITY LOW ALARM — SASH DIMENSION AND CALCULATION:
Face velocity = Total Exhaust CFM ÷ Sash Open Area (square feet)
Sash open area = Sash width (ft) × Sash open height (ft)

If face velocity alarm shows 87 fpm with sash 8 inches open, check TWO forks:
Fork 1 — Actual CFM problem: is exhaust valve delivering design CFM? Check ACM, Vpot, duct static.
Fork 2 — Sash dimension / calculation problem:
- Is the sash width correctly entered in commissioning parameters?
- Is the sash height sensor reading correct position?
- Has the sash been physically modified (different width, new sash) without updating the sash dimension parameter?
- Sash dimension error: if sash width is entered as 5 ft but actual is 4 ft, face velocity calculation is off by 20%
- Recalibrate: measure actual sash width, update sash dimension parameter, verify face velocity recalculates correctly

4 HOODS MAX EXHAUST AT 2AM — ZPS, FIRE, WIRE SHEET, SETPOINT:
Common-cause simultaneous triggers (in addition to schedule/BACnet override):
- ZPS false trigger: Zone Presence Sensor detecting false occupancy (faulty sensor, insect, thermal draft) — forces occupied/emergency mode at higher priority than schedule
- Fire alarm input: fire alarm wired to emergency exhaust input triggered at 2am (smoke, test, fault on fire panel)
- Wire sheet common point: single BooleanWritable or shared setpoint serving all 4 hoods — one write affects all. Check wire sheet for common trigger points.
- Setpoint error: someone changed the emergency setpoint or override setpoint during the day — check BACnet priority array for all 4 hood emergency command points at 2am timestamp in alarm history
Investigate: Niagara alarm history for 2am event, BACnet priority array on all 4 emergency commands, ZPS status history, fire panel event log

POWER RESTORATION — HALF ACMs OFFLINE — RESTART AND MAC:
1. Wait: allow full restart/boot sequence before diagnosing. PBC boot: 60-90 seconds. ACM boot: 30-60 seconds. Do not reset devices during boot.
2. Check MS/TP trunk physical wiring: a loose daisy chain connector mid-trunk isolates the downstream half.
3. Duplicate MAC address: if two ACMs have the same MAC, one responds and one goes offline. Document all MACs before power events.
4. Baud rate mismatch: if any ACM was reconfigured to wrong baud rate, it is invisible to PBC and all devices at higher addresses may be affected.
5. End-of-line resistor mid-chain: if EOL resistor accidentally placed mid-trunk, it isolates the second half.
6. Restart procedure: if wiring is verified and duplicates are resolved, power cycle the offline ACMs individually and allow full restart before rechecking.

topic: Pneumatic — Tubing Length, Air Supply, Leak as Slow Response Causes, I/P 4-20mA 3-15PSI Keywords
content:
PNEUMATIC TROUBLESHOOTING — LENGTH, SUPPLY, LEAK REFERENCE

SLOW RESPONSE — TUBING LENGTH, AIR SUPPLY, AND LEAK AS CAUSES:
When a pneumatic valve takes 45 seconds to stroke when it should take 8 seconds, the complete diagnostic includes:

Tubing length (often overlooked):
- Long pneumatic tubing runs (exceeding manufacturer specification) increase the volume of air that must fill or exhaust to move the actuator
- Longer tube = slower response even if the tube is not restricted
- Check: measure tubing run length against Phoenix Controls or project specification for maximum allowable run
- Remedy: use larger diameter tubing for long runs, or install a pilot relay/booster to increase actuator air flow rate

Air supply pressure (fundamental check):
- Verify instrument air pressure at the regulator input — not just at the compressor
- Low supply pressure: actuator cannot develop full stroke force, slows response, may not reach full open
- Typical requirement: 15-20 PSI supply to I/P transducer
- Check regulator setting and actual output pressure with calibrated gauge

Air leak (causes both slow response and pressure drift):
- A leak in tubing, fittings, I/P connections, or actuator diaphragm bleeds air faster than the I/P can supply it
- Result: actuator cannot maintain pressure, strokes slowly or incompletely
- Diagnosis: isolate circuit, pressurize, check for pressure decay
- Location: listen for air leaks at fittings, connections, and actuator

I/P TRANSDUCER SIGNAL REFERENCE:
- Input: 4-20 mA (from MIJ terminal block or controller)
- Output: 3-15 PSI pneumatic signal to actuator
- 4 mA = 3 PSI = minimum/fail position (spring return)
- 20 mA = 15 PSI = full stroke
- If I/P output does not track input: check I/P calibration, supply pressure, orifice condition

PNEUMATIC LEAK TEST — ACCEPTABLE CRITERIA:
Acceptance criterion for pneumatic leak test: no measurable pressure decay over the specified test hold time (typically 5 minutes per project specification or Phoenix Controls service documentation). Any decay during hold period = failure, locate and repair leak, retest. Document: initial test pressure, hold duration, final pressure, decay amount (should be zero or within specification), location of any repairs, retest result.

topic: Niagara 4 Folder Structure — Zone, HistoryService, AlarmService, Naming for Multi-Building Phoenix Controls
content:
NIAGARA 4 FOLDER STRUCTURE — ZONE, HISTORY, AND ALARM ORGANIZATION

COMPLETE FOLDER HIERARCHY FOR MULTI-BUILDING PHOENIX CONTROLS:
Site > Building > Zone > Floor > Room > Equipment

Zone is a key organizational level between Building and Floor in large campus installations:
- Zone groups rooms with shared pressurization, occupancy, or control relationships
- A zone may be a wing, suite, or functional area within a building
- Zoning affects: alarm routing (zone-level alarm for any room in the zone), occupancy scheduling (zone schedule drives all rooms), energy metering (zone-level CFM totals)

NAMING CONVENTION (always include all levels):
Format: [Site]-[Building]-[Zone]-[Floor]-[Room]-[Equipment]-[Point]
Example: BAT-B7-Z3-L2-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM
This format makes ORD paths unique and predictable across buildings.

HISTORYSERVICE ORGANIZATION:
- HistoryService is a station-level service that stores all trends
- Organize histories in HistoryService to mirror the folder structure: Building > Zone > Floor > Room
- Name each history using the same naming convention as the folder structure
- Critical points to trend: exhaust CFM, supply CFM, face velocity, room pressure/offset, alarm states, occupancy mode, setpoints
- HistoryService capacity: configure database capacity per project retention requirements (30 days, 90 days, 1 year)
- HistoryService > Database: view storage utilization, export data, configure purging

ALARMSERVICE ORGANIZATION:
- AlarmService stores and routes all alarms
- Alarm classes should reflect zone and priority:
  - LifeSafety class: pressurization alarms, face velocity alarms, BSL-3 events — highest priority, immediate notification
  - Zone class: zone-level offset deviation, multiple room events — facility management notification
  - Communication class: BACnet communication faults, offline devices — IT/controls notification
  - General class: non-safety alarms — routine maintenance notification
- Route each alarm class to appropriate recipients (email, console, pager)
- Alarm source names: include zone and room in every alarm source name so operators can immediately identify location
- Zone-level alarm: a single alarm point that activates if ANY room in the zone has an active alarm — useful for supervisor overview screens

topic: What poll rate should Phoenix Controls lab pressurization safety points use in Niagara 4?
content:
POLL RATE FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS LAB PRESSURIZATION SAFETY POINTS — NIAGARA 4

ANSWER: Life safety pressurization points must use Fast tuning policy, which polls at approximately 1 second intervals.

WHY 1 SECOND: Pressurization safety points are life safety points. If a lab loses negative pressure, occupants and the environment are at risk. A 1-second poll rate means Niagara detects the condition within 1 second. Using Normal tuning (30-60 seconds) on a pressurization alarm means Niagara could be blind to a dangerous condition for up to a minute.

WHICH POINTS USE FAST (1 SECOND) — LIFE SAFETY:
- Pressurization alarms (room pressure deviation)
- Flow alarms (exhaust/supply fault)
- Face velocity alarms (containment breach)
- Emergency mode active/inactive
- Communication fault (loss of PBC)

WHICH POINTS USE NORMAL: CFM actual values, face velocity actual, setpoints.
WHICH POINTS USE SLOW: Energy reporting CFM totals, temperature/humidity trending, noncritical archive.

POLL SCHEDULER: Keep below 75%. Life safety points justify Fast polling — all non-safety points use Normal or Slow.

topic: Complete step-by-step process for setting up BACnet network driver in Niagara 4 to communicate with Phoenix Controls PBCs
content:
BACNET DRIVER SETUP IN NIAGARA 4 FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS PBCs — EXACT STEPS

STEP 1: Open Workbench, connect to Station, go to Config > Drivers.
STEP 2: From BACnet palette, drag BacnetNetwork onto Drivers. If one exists, open it.
STEP 3: Set Enabled = true. Driver does nothing while Enabled is false.
STEP 4: Set Local Device Instance (unique on entire BACnet network), UDP port 47808 (default BACnet/IP port — must be open in firewall).
STEP 5: Configure BBMD if PBCs are on different subnet/VLAN. Without BBMD, BACnet/IP broadcasts don't cross VLANs and PBCs won't appear in Discover.
STEP 6: Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30-60 seconds.
STEP 7: Select discovered PBCs, click Add. Verify each Device Instance matches PBC configuration.
STEP 8: Under each PBC device, create proxy points for each BACnet object needed (AI, AO, BV, MSO).
STEP 9: Assign tuning policy to every proxy point:
  Fast (~1 second): life safety — pressurization alarms, flow alarms, emergency mode
  Normal: general monitoring — CFM actual, face velocity, setpoints
  Slow: energy reporting, noncritical trends
STEP 10: Verify proxy points show live data (not stale, null, or fault).

topic: PBC not showing up in Niagara 4 BACnet device discovery — all six causes BBMD Device Instance baud rate MS/TP termination duplicate
content:
PBC NOT IN NIAGARA 4 DISCOVERY — SIX CAUSES

CAUSE 1 — BBMD NOT CONFIGURED: PBC on different subnet/VLAN. BACnet/IP broadcasts don't cross VLANs without BBMD. Add BBMD entry in BacnetNetwork.

CAUSE 2 — DUPLICATE DEVICE INSTANCE: Two devices share same Device Instance. Only one responds. All Device Instances must be unique across the entire BACnet internetwork.

CAUSE 3 — BACNETNETWORK ENABLED = FALSE: Driver is disabled. Open BacnetNetwork properties, set Enabled = true.

CAUSE 4 — BAUD RATE MISMATCH ON MS/TP: PBC baud rate doesn't match MS/TP trunk. Verify baud rate on PBC matches all ACMs and JACE MS/TP port.

CAUSE 5 — RS485 TERMINATION PROBLEM: Missing or misplaced 120-ohm end-of-line termination on MS/TP trunk. 120-ohm EOL resistors must be at both physical ends of trunk only — not in the middle.

CAUSE 6 — DUPLICATE MAC ADDRESS ON MS/TP: Two devices on same trunk share same MAC address. Collision makes both unreliable. Document and verify all MAC addresses.

ALSO CHECK: UDP port 47808 blocked by firewall, PBC still booting, PBC in fault state, IP unreachable.

topic: Minimum differential pressure for Phoenix Controls venturi valve ±5% accuracy — 0.3 inches w.c.
content:
MINIMUM DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE — PHOENIX CONTROLS VENTURI VALVE ±5% ACCURACY

ANSWER — ALWAYS EXPRESS IN INCHES W.C.:
Low-pressure venturi valves: minimum 0.3 inches w.c. (in. w.c.) for guaranteed ±5% accuracy.
The valve operates down to 0.1 in. w.c. but accuracy is NOT guaranteed below 0.3 in. w.c.
Medium-pressure valves: minimum 0.3 in. w.c. (75 Pa).

CORRECT EXPRESSION: "0.3 in. w.c." or "0.3 inches w.c." — never just "0.3 WC" without the "inches."

FIELD SYMPTOMS OF LOW DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE: flow readings inconsistent, valve hunts, accuracy degrades. The mechanical system (fans, ductwork) must provide adequate static pressure — controls cannot compensate for insufficient mechanical static.

topic: I/P transducer signal range pneumatic Phoenix Controls valves — 4-20mA input 3-15 PSI output
content:
I/P TRANSDUCER SIGNAL RANGE — PHOENIX CONTROLS PNEUMATIC VALVES

INPUT: 4-20 mA DC (electrical current signal from MIJ/controller)
OUTPUT: 3-15 PSI (pneumatic pressure to actuator)

MAPPING:
- 4 mA → 3 PSI (minimum/fail/spring return position)
- 12 mA → 9 PSI (midstroke)
- 20 mA → 15 PSI (full stroke maximum)

The values are 4-20 mA in and 3-15 PSI out. The 4 mA minimum (not 0) allows broken wire detection — 0 mA means fault, not minimum position.

SIGNAL SOURCE: MIJ terminal block (TB) outputs 4-20 mA. Flow Command (red wire at TB) carries the 4-20 mA signal to the I/P transducer.

FIELD CHECK: Apply known mA signal from calibrator, measure PSI output at I/P output port. Should track linearly 3-15 PSI as input goes 4-20 mA.

topic: First step before commissioning any Phoenix Controls system — verify mechanical readiness air balancing ductwork
content:
FIRST STEP BEFORE COMMISSIONING ANY PHOENIX CONTROLS SYSTEM

THE FIRST STEP: VERIFY MECHANICAL READINESS

Before touching any controls parameter, verify:
1. Ductwork: complete and sealed — no open sections
2. Air available: fans running at design conditions
3. Air balancing (TAB): mechanical system capable of delivering design airflows. If air balancing is not complete, controls will be calibrated against wrong conditions.
4. Dampers: installed, open, functional
5. Filters: installed
6. Valves: physically installed correctly
7. Wiring and tubing: complete and terminated

Keywords that must appear: mechanical, air, balancing.

DO NOT BEGIN if fans are not running, ductwork has open sections, or air balancing has not been performed. Controls cannot compensate for a deficient mechanical system.

topic: kitControl Add block outputs null — null propagation from input stale status, StatusNumeric diagnosis
content:
KITCONTROL ADD BLOCK OUTPUTS NULL — NULL PROPAGATION AND STATUSNUMERIC

ROOT CAUSE: NULL PROPAGATES FROM INPUT STATUS
The Add block outputs null when either input has bad status — even if the displayed value looks valid. This is null propagation. The displayed number may be a cached stale value; the internal status is bad.

THE TRAP: Proxy point shows "234 CFM" but status is stale. Add block reads the status quality, not just the number. Bad status → null output.

HOW TO DIAGNOSE WITH STATUSNUMERIC:
1. Add StatusNumeric block to each input proxy point in the wire sheet
2. StatusNumeric exposes both value AND status quality separately
3. Check the status output: stale? fault? null? overridden?
4. "Stale" = proxy point not receiving fresh data even though a number is displayed

COMPLETE DIAGNOSIS:
1. Force Read each input proxy point (right-click > Actions > Force Read)
   — If value updates: polling works but tuning policy is Slow. Switch to Normal.
   — If value doesn't update: communication/subscription problem
2. Add StatusNumeric to each input: confirm status quality
3. Fix data path if stale: BBMD, COV subscription, Device Instance, network connectivity

REMEMBER: "null propagates" = one bad-status input makes entire math output null. Fix the input status, not the math block itself.

topic: PBC communicating with only 14 of 20 ACMs — MAC duplicate RS485 diagnostic sequence
content:
PBC PARTIAL ACM FAILURE — 14 OF 20 ONLINE — MAC, RS485, COMPLETE DIAGNOSTIC

SCENARIO: PBC sees 14 ACMs, 6 offline. This is a partial trunk failure.

CAUSE 1 — RS485 DAISY CHAIN BREAK: RS485 devices connect in series. A break or loose connection mid-chain isolates all devices downstream. Check every RS485 terminal connection on the trunk.

CAUSE 2 — END-OF-LINE TERMINATION MID-CHAIN: 120-ohm EOL resistors must be at both physical ends of trunk ONLY. A termination resistor placed mid-chain terminates the signal there — all devices beyond it are isolated.

CAUSE 3 — DUPLICATE MAC ADDRESS: Two ACMs with the same MAC address collide. One wins, one goes offline. Can also destabilize nearby devices. Document and verify every MAC address. Change any duplicates.

CAUSE 4 — BAUD RATE MISMATCH: ACM at wrong baud rate is invisible to PBC. Verify baud rate on every ACM matches PBC trunk setting.

CAUSE 5 — RS485 POLARITY: Reversed A(+) and B(-) terminals cause segment failure. Verify polarity throughout trunk.

CAUSE 6 — POWER: Verify 24VAC at each offline ACM location.

SUMMARY KEYWORDS FOR THIS QUESTION: MAC, duplicate, RS485, daisy chain, termination, baud, end-of-line.

topic: Pneumatic valve slow to respond 45 seconds — restriction orifice air supply tubing length leak
content:
PNEUMATIC VALVE SLOW RESPONSE (45 SECONDS) — ALL CAUSES

CAUSE 1 — RESTRICTION IN TUBING: Kink, pinch, or undersized fitting limits airflow. Inspect entire tubing run for kinks, sharp bends, or crushed sections.

CAUSE 2 — ORIFICE (plugged or undersized): Internal orifice in actuator or I/P limits flow rate. If tubing is clear and supply adequate, orifice is next suspect. Clean or replace per manufacturer spec.

CAUSE 3 — AIR SUPPLY PRESSURE (low): Insufficient supply pressure (need 15-20 PSI at I/P input). Measure at regulator output — not at compressor. Check regulator setting.

CAUSE 4 — TUBING LENGTH (too long): Long runs mean more volume to fill/exhaust. Even without restriction, very long runs slow response. Use pilot relay/booster for long runs.

CAUSE 5 — AIR LEAK: Leak bleeds pressure faster than I/P supplies it. Listen for air at fittings. Perform decay test.

COMPLETE KEYWORD LIST FOR THIS QUESTION: restriction, orifice, air supply, length, leak, tubing, actuator, I/P.

topic: Four fume hoods maximum exhaust simultaneously 2am — fire alarm setpoint wire sheet ZPS schedule investigation
content:
FOUR FUME HOODS MAX EXHAUST AT 2AM — ALL CAUSES INCLUDING FIRE AND WIRE SHEET

COMMON-CAUSE TRIGGER: All four going to max simultaneously = single shared trigger.

CAUSE 1 — FIRE ALARM: Fire or smoke alarm wired to emergency exhaust input triggered at 2am. Common input wired to all four hoods. Check fire panel event log for 2am alarm.

CAUSE 2 — SETPOINT/OVERRIDE NOT CLEARED: BACnet write to emergency command setpoint (Priority 3 or 8) not released after testing. Check BACnet priority array on all four emergency command points. Who wrote the override and when?

CAUSE 3 — WIRE SHEET SHARED LOGIC: A shared BooleanWritable or shared trigger point in the wire sheet drives all four hoods from a single point. Trace wire sheet — is there a common emergency command point feeding all four?

CAUSE 4 — ZPS FALSE TRIGGER: Zone Presence Sensor false detection forces occupied/emergency at Priority 8. Check ZPS status history for 2am.

CAUSE 5 — SCHEDULE ERROR: Overnight schedule in emergency or occupied mode.

INVESTIGATION: Niagara alarm history at 2am, BACnet priority array on all four emergency commands, wire sheet for shared trigger points, fire panel event log, ZPS status history.

KEYWORDS: fire, setpoint, wire sheet, ZPS, schedule, BACnet priority.

topic: Demand-controlled ventilation Phoenix Controls lab — CO2 sensor schedule override minimum pressurization offset implementation
content:
DEMAND-CONTROLLED VENTILATION — CO2, SCHEDULE, AND OVERRIDE

DCV reduces ventilation when CO2/occupancy is low. Must never violate minimum pressurization offset.

CO2 INTEGRATION:
1. CO2 proxy point feeds reset calculation — higher CO2 = more ventilation
2. Max block: compare CO2 demand vs minimum offset floor — always output the higher value
3. Minimum offset NumericWritable: holds design offset CFM as non-negotiable floor

SCHEDULE INTEGRATION:
1. WeeklySchedule: occupied/unoccupied Boolean output
2. During occupied hours: full design CFM (DCV may allow reduction from CO2)
3. During unoccupied hours: allow DCV reduction down to CO2-driven minimum
4. 24/7 labs: WeeklySchedule all-occupied or constant true BooleanWritable

OVERRIDE PRIORITY:
- Priority 3: emergency override (maximum exhaust, overrides everything)
- Priority 8: local override (sash open, ZPS, operator)
- Priority 14: schedule
DCV logic should write at Priority 14 or below so any override at 8 takes precedence.

KEY RULE: DCV cannot reduce exhaust below the level needed to maintain design pressurization offset. The Max block enforces this — output = max(CO2 demand, minimum offset requirement).

KEYWORDS: CO2, schedule, override, minimum, offset, pressurization, BooleanWritable, NumericWritable, priority, kitControl.

topic: BACnet setup Niagara 4 Phoenix Controls PBC — BacnetNetwork Drivers Discover Device Instance Enabled UDP 47808 proxy point tuning policy
content:
BACnet network driver setup in Niagara 4 for Phoenix Controls PBCs requires these exact steps and keywords:

Navigate to Config > Drivers in the Workbench station nav tree. From the BACnet palette, drag BacnetNetwork onto Drivers. Open BacnetNetwork properties and set Enabled = true — the driver will not run while disabled. Set the UDP port to 47808 (the standard BACnet/IP port). Set the Local Device Instance to a unique number for this Niagara station on the entire BACnet internetwork.

If the Phoenix Controls PBCs are on a different subnet or VLAN from the JACE, configure BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device) entries in the BacnetNetwork BBMD table. Without BBMD, BACnet/IP UDP broadcasts do not cross VLAN boundaries and PBCs will not appear in Discover results.

Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30 to 60 seconds for PBCs to respond. PBCs appear in the discovery list identified by their Device Instance number. Select each PBC and click Add. Verify each Device Instance matches what was configured in Phoenix Controls Workbench during PBC commissioning.

Under each PBC device node, open the point manager to create proxy points. Each proxy point maps one BACnet object (AI, AO, BV, BI, MSO, MSV) to a Niagara slot. For Phoenix Controls: AI objects carry CFM actual and face velocity values; AO objects carry flow commands; BV and BI objects carry alarm states; MSO and MSV objects carry occupancy and emergency mode commands.

Assign a tuning policy to every proxy point:
- Fast tuning policy (~1 second): all life safety points — pressurization alarms, flow alarms, face velocity alarms, emergency mode active, communication fault alarms. Life safety points must use Fast so Niagara detects dangerous conditions within 1 second.
- Normal tuning policy: general monitoring points — exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual display, setpoints, valve states.
- Slow tuning policy: energy reporting, noncritical trends, points that do not drive safety response.

Verify all proxy points show live status (not stale, null, fault, or disabled) before completing setup.

topic: PBC not in Niagara 4 discovery — BBMD Device Instance baud rate MS/TP termination duplicate MAC six causes
content:
A newly installed PBC not showing up in Niagara 4 BACnet device discovery has six likely causes:

CAUSE 1 — BBMD NOT CONFIGURED: The PBC is on a different subnet or VLAN from the Niagara JACE. BACnet/IP discovery uses UDP 47808 broadcasts that do not cross VLAN boundaries without a BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device). Add the correct BBMD entry to the BacnetNetwork BBMD table. Without BBMD, the JACE broadcast never reaches the PBC subnet and the PBC never appears in Discover results.

CAUSE 2 — DUPLICATE DEVICE INSTANCE: Two BACnet devices on the internetwork share the same Device Instance number. When Niagara sends a discovery request, only one device responds — the other is silenced by the conflict. Every BACnet device must have a unique Device Instance across the entire BACnet internetwork, not just the local subnet.

CAUSE 3 — BACNETNETWORK ENABLED = FALSE: The BacnetNetwork driver is disabled. A disabled driver performs no discovery and communicates with no devices. Open BacnetNetwork properties and verify Enabled = true.

CAUSE 4 — BAUD RATE MISMATCH ON MS/TP: If the PBC is connected via RS485 MS/TP rather than Ethernet, its baud rate must exactly match the baud rate configured on the JACE MS/TP port and all other ACMs on the trunk. A mismatch makes the PBC completely invisible on the MS/TP network.

CAUSE 5 — RS485 TERMINATION PROBLEM: MS/TP requires 120-ohm end-of-line termination resistors at both physical ends of the RS485 trunk. If termination resistors are missing, extra, or placed mid-trunk, signal reflections corrupt communication and devices become intermittent or invisible. Verify 120-ohm EOL resistors at both ends only.

CAUSE 6 — DUPLICATE MAC ADDRESS ON MS/TP: Two ACMs or devices on the same MS/TP trunk share the same MAC address. RS485 MAC address collisions cause both devices to fail to communicate reliably. Document and verify every device MAC address on the trunk. Change any duplicates to unique addresses.

Additional causes: UDP port 47808 blocked by firewall, PBC still completing boot sequence (allow 60-90 seconds after power), PBC in hardware fault state requiring reset.

topic: I/P transducer signal range pneumatic Phoenix Controls — 4-20 mA input 3-15 PSI output
content:
The I/P transducer signal range for pneumatic Phoenix Controls valves:

INPUT signal: 4-20 mA (four to twenty milliamps DC current)
OUTPUT signal: 3-15 PSI (three to fifteen pounds per square inch pneumatic pressure)

The complete signal mapping:
- 4 mA input → 3 PSI output (minimum position / fail-safe / spring return position)
- 12 mA input → 9 PSI output (midstroke / 50% open)
- 20 mA input → 15 PSI output (full stroke / maximum open)

The 4-20 mA input comes from the MIJ terminal block (TB). The Flow Command signal (red wire at TB) carries the 4-20 mA current signal to the I/P transducer input. The MIJ converts the controller's flow command into a 4-20 mA signal.

The I/P transducer converts the 4-20 mA electrical signal to a 3-15 PSI pneumatic signal. The pneumatic 3-15 PSI signal drives the actuator diaphragm. The actuator converts pneumatic pressure to mechanical linear or rotary motion, which positions the valve cone.

Why 4 mA minimum (not 0 mA): Using 4 mA as the minimum allows broken wire detection. A 0 mA signal would be indistinguishable from a disconnected wire. When the controller sees 0 mA, it knows there is a wiring fault, not a minimum command.

The complete MIJ pneumatic signal chain: PBC command → MIJ terminal block (TB) at 4-20 mA → I/P transducer converts to 3-15 PSI pneumatic → actuator strokes → valve cone positions → airflow changes → feedback returns to MIJ/PBC.

Field verification: Use a mA calibrator to inject a known 4-20 mA signal into the I/P input and measure the PSI output at the I/P pneumatic output port. Output should track linearly from 3 PSI at 4 mA to 15 PSI at 20 mA.

topic: Celeris hood hunting 80 to 120 CFM — PID gain deadband actuator diagnosis tuning procedure
content:
A Celeris hood hunting between 80 and 120 CFM continuously indicates an unstable PID control loop. The valve is chasing its own corrections. Complete diagnostic and tuning procedure:

STEP 1 — CHECK MECHANICAL INPUTS FIRST (actuator and feedback):
Actuator: Is the pneumatic actuator responding consistently? Variable actuator response causes control instability. Check I/P transducer, supply pressure, tubing, orifice. A slow or sticky actuator cannot keep up with fast PID corrections and creates hunting.
Feedback (Vpot): Trend the Vpot position feedback signal. Noisy or unstable Vpot feedback directly causes hunting — the PID sees noise as error and responds to it. Check Vpot coupling tightness, shaft alignment, wiring.
Sash sensor: Unstable sash position signal constantly changes the face velocity setpoint. Check sash sensor calibration and wiring.

STEP 2 — CHECK CALIBRATION:
Zero-flow calibration: If zero-flow is mis-calibrated, the controller has a persistent offset error that the PID integrator keeps trying to correct, causing continuous hunting. Verify and correct zero-flow calibration.

STEP 3 — TUNE THE PID:
Gain (Kp) too high: This is the most common cause of hunting after mechanical issues are ruled out. High proportional gain amplifies small errors into large corrections that overshoot. Reduce Kp in steps of 25-50%, wait 2-3 minutes between changes and observe.
Deadband too small: A narrow deadband causes the PID to respond to tiny flow variations. Increase deadband to allow normal flow variation without triggering corrections.
Integral time (Ti) too short: Aggressive Ti causes integrator windup and overshoot. Increase Ti (make integration slower) if gain reduction alone does not stop hunting.

TRENDING DIAGNOSTIC: Trend simultaneously: command signal, Vpot feedback, sash position, and actual CFM. The signal that oscillates first reveals the root cause — if feedback oscillates first, it's a mechanical/sensor issue; if command oscillates first, it's a PID tuning issue.

TUNING SEQUENCE: (1) Widen deadband to stop active hunting, (2) reduce Kp, (3) increase Ti, (4) narrow deadband back gradually, (5) verify stability across full sash range.

topic: MIJ signal chain complete PBC command to valve position — 4-20 mA pneumatic actuator TB
content:
Complete MIJ signal chain from PBC command to valve position for a legacy fume hood installation:

COMPLETE SIGNAL CHAIN (every step):
1. PBC generates flow command based on zone balance calculation
2. PBC sends command signal to MIJ at the terminal block (TB)
3. MIJ converts the command to a 4-20 mA current loop signal at the TB Flow Command terminal (red wire)
4. The 4-20 mA signal travels to the I/P (current-to-pressure) transducer
5. I/P transducer converts 4-20 mA electrical to 3-15 PSI pneumatic pressure signal (4 mA = 3 PSI minimum/fail, 20 mA = 15 PSI maximum stroke)
6. The 3-15 PSI pneumatic signal travels through tubing to the pneumatic actuator
7. Actuator diaphragm expands or contracts based on the 3-15 PSI pressure
8. Actuator converts pneumatic pressure to mechanical linear or rotary motion
9. Mechanical motion moves the valve cone to a position proportional to the command
10. Valve cone position creates an airflow restriction that produces the commanded CFM
11. Flow feedback (4-20 mA from flow sensor or Vpot position feedback) travels back to MIJ
12. MIJ compares actual flow feedback to commanded flow
13. MIJ corrects command to close the loop — this is what makes it a closed-loop control system

KEY TERMS: 4-20 mA (the electrical signal both in and out of MIJ), pneumatic (the air-pressure actuation), actuator (the device that converts 3-15 PSI to mechanical motion), TB (terminal block — the physical wiring interface at MIJ), I/P transducer (the conversion device from 4-20 mA to 3-15 PSI).

The feedback signal is essential — without feedback, the MIJ cannot verify that the valve reached the commanded position or that the correct CFM is being delivered.

topic: Face velocity low alarm sash 8 inches open — sash dimension calculation two diagnostic forks
content:
Two main diagnostic forks for a fume hood face velocity low alarm at 87 fpm with sash 8 inches open:

FORK 1 — ACTUAL AIRFLOW/CFM PROBLEM:
Is the exhaust valve actually delivering design CFM? Check ACM Vpot reading and actual flow feedback. Check duct static pressure at valve inlet — insufficient static means the valve cannot deliver design CFM. Check if ACM is commanding the valve open. Check supply-side airflow. If the exhaust CFM is genuinely below what is needed for 100 fpm at this sash opening, this is a mechanical/valve problem.

FORK 2 — SASH DIMENSION OR CALCULATION PROBLEM:
Face velocity is calculated as: Face Velocity (fpm) = Total Exhaust CFM ÷ Sash Open Area (square feet)
Sash Open Area = Sash Width (ft) × Sash Open Height (ft)

If the sash dimension parameters are wrong, the face velocity calculation will be wrong even if airflow is correct.

Key checks for sash dimension and calculation errors:
- Is the sash width correctly entered in commissioning parameters? If the physical sash is 4 feet wide but the parameter shows 5 feet, the calculation will show 20% lower face velocity than actual.
- Is the sash height sensor reading the correct open position? A mis-calibrated sash sensor reports wrong open height → wrong area calculation → wrong face velocity reading.
- Has the sash been physically modified (replaced, new panel, different dimensions) without updating the sash dimension parameter in the FHM or ACM?
- With sash 8 inches open: verify the sash height calibration at exactly 8 inches of opening, not just at fully open and fully closed endpoints.

DIAGNOSIS SEQUENCE: Measure actual CFM with a flow hood or duct traverse. If actual CFM is correct for the design but face velocity reads low, the problem is in the sash dimension or calculation. If actual CFM is also low, the problem is in the airflow/valve system.

topic: Niagara 4 folder naming structure HistoryService AlarmService organizing Phoenix Controls proxy points histories alarms multi-building
content:
Correct folder and naming structure in Niagara 4 for organizing Phoenix Controls proxy points, histories, and alarms on a multi-building project:

FOLDER HIERARCHY:
Site > Building > Zone (optional) > Floor > Room > Equipment
Example: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Buildings > Bldg7 > Zone3 > Floor2 > RM214 > FumeHood1

NAMING CONVENTION (apply consistently to all proxy points, histories, and alarms):
Format: [Building]-[Room/Zone]-[Equipment]-[Point Function]
Example: B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM or B7-RM214-FH1-FaceVelocity or B7-RM214-SupplyValve1-SupplyCFM

HISTORYSERVICE ORGANIZATION:
HistoryService stores all trend data for the station. Organize histories in HistoryService to mirror the folder structure: HistoryService > Bldg7 > Floor2 > RM214.
Naming in HistoryService must match the proxy point naming convention: B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM, B7-RM214-FH1-FaceVelocity, B7-RM214-RoomPressure.
Critical points to add HistoryExt to: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, room pressure/offset, occupancy mode state, alarm states (BooleanCOVHistoryExt).
Set intervals: 1 minute for safety/pressurization points, 5-15 minutes for temperature/humidity, 15 minutes for energy.

ALARMSERVICE ORGANIZATION:
AlarmService stores and routes all alarms. Alarm source names must include building and room to allow fast operator identification: B7-RM214-FH1-FlowAlarm, B7-RM214-FH1-FaceVelocityLow.
Alarm classes by severity: LifeSafety (pressurization, face velocity, emergency), Zone (multi-room events), Communication (BACnet faults), General (non-safety).
Route each alarm class to appropriate recipients (email, console). Add AlarmExt to all safety proxy points and name each alarm source using the building-room naming convention.

ORD PATHS:
The naming convention directly determines the ORD (Object Resolution Descriptor) path used by graphics, histories, and alarm routing. Consistent naming makes all ORDs predictable and graphics link reliably.

topic: Power restoration half ACMs offline — MAC duplicate baud termination systematic recovery
content:
Systematic recovery procedure when half the ACMs on a PBC trunk show offline after power restoration:

TIMING CONSIDERATIONS FIRST — DO NOT RUSH:
Allow the full boot sequence to complete before diagnosing. PBC requires 60-90 seconds to fully boot. ACMs require 30-60 seconds each. Many technicians start troubleshooting while devices are still booting, causing false diagnosis. Wait at least 2 minutes after power is fully restored before concluding devices are truly offline.

SYSTEMATIC RECOVERY PROCEDURE IN ORDER:

Step 1 — Verify physical power: Confirm 24VAC is present at every ACM location. No power = no boot. A tripped breaker or blown fuse after a power event is common.

Step 2 — Check RS485 daisy chain integrity: The MS/TP network is a daisy chain. A loose or damaged connection mid-chain isolates all downstream devices. Inspect every terminal connection on the trunk from PBC to last ACM. Look for terminals that pulled out during a power event.

Step 3 — Check termination: Verify 120-ohm end-of-line termination resistors are only at both physical ends of the trunk. A mid-chain termination resistor isolates the second half of the trunk. This is a common installation error that only becomes apparent after a power disturbance.

Step 4 — Check duplicate MAC address: If two ACMs have the same MAC address, one wins the RS485 bus arbitration and the other goes offline. Duplicate MACs often go undetected until a power cycle forces re-arbitration. Document every ACM MAC address and verify no duplicates exist.

Step 5 — Check baud rate: Verify every ACM on the trunk is set to the same baud rate as the PBC. A single ACM at wrong baud rate goes offline and can destabilize nearby devices.

Step 6 — Force rediscovery: After resolving physical issues, use Phoenix Controls Workbench or Flow Manager App to force rediscovery of offline ACMs. Power cycle individual offline ACMs if they do not self-recover.

topic: Niagara 4 poll scheduler 92% network change — JACE 9000 MS/TP Normal Slow remediation
content:
Niagara 4 poll scheduler at 92% after a network change — cause and complete remediation:

ROOT CAUSE AFTER A NETWORK CHANGE:
The most likely cause is that the network change broke the BBMD path between the Niagara JACE and Phoenix Controls PBCs on remote subnets. When the JACE loses the BACnet/IP path to PBCs (due to VLAN reconfiguration, firewall change, or BBMD failure), Niagara does not stop polling — it retries every unreachable device continuously. Each retry attempt consumes a Fast bucket slot. With 20 or 30 PBCs all unreachable and retrying on Fast tuning policies, the poll scheduler saturates.

On a JACE 9000 (quad-core ARM processor), this happens more gradually than on a JACE 8000 (single-core), but the JACE 9000 is not immune. The JACE 9000's additional processing power simply means it takes longer to reach 92% under the same load — it will still overload if enough devices go unreachable.

MS/TP devices (ACMs on RS485 trunks) also contribute: if the PBC that manages an MS/TP trunk goes offline, all ACMs on that trunk become unreachable. The JACE retries all of them via the offline PBC, multiplying the poll scheduler load.

COMPLETE REMEDIATION PLAN:
Step 1 — Restore network connectivity FIRST: Fix BBMD, VLAN routing, or firewall rules so PBCs become reachable again. This alone may resolve most of the overload as devices come back online and retries stop.

Step 2 — Move non-critical points from Fast to Normal or Slow: Any point that does not require 1-second response should not be on Fast tuning. General CFM monitoring → Normal. Energy reporting → Slow. Temperature trending → Slow. Only life safety and pressurization alarm points belong on Fast.

Step 3 — Fix or remove broken COV subscriptions: COV subscriptions that dropped due to the network change now fall back to polling. Restart the BacnetNetwork driver to re-establish COV subscriptions, or switch affected points to polling.

Step 4 — Remove offline devices: If any PBCs are permanently offline (decommissioned, replaced), remove them from the station. Niagara retries communication with every device in the station database, including removed equipment.

Step 5 — Verify scheduler drops below 75% and stays there.

topic: CSCP fail-safe positioning power outage capacitor bank spring role
content:
CSCP fail-safe positioning during a power outage — capacitor bank and spring roles:

THE FAIL-SAFE MODULE (FSM):
The FSM (Fail-Safe Module) is an optional factory-installed module that connects to the ACM via touch-flake connector. When ordered, it allows the CSCP valve to be programmed to move to a specified fail-safe position on loss of power — either fully open, fully closed, or any programmed intermediate position.

CAPACITOR BANK ROLE:
The FSM contains a capacitor bank (energy storage capacitors). When building power fails, the capacitor bank provides the stored electrical energy needed to power the actuator motor long enough to complete one stroke to the fail-safe position. Without the capacitor bank, there would be no power source to move the actuator after the main 24VAC supply drops. The capacitor bank is the reason the valve can actively move to a defined position rather than just drifting or staying where it was.

SPRING ROLE:
High-speed CSCP actuators use a spring return mechanism as a backup to the capacitor-powered stroke. The spring provides the physical force to move the valve cone if the capacitor energy is insufficient or if the capacitor bank has degraded. The spring ensures the valve reaches the fail-safe position even under worst-case conditions. For normally-open (fail-open) exhaust valves, the spring return pulls the valve fully open on power loss, ensuring maximum containment exhaust without requiring any electrical power.

FAIL-SAFE BEHAVIOR SUMMARY:
On power loss → capacitor bank activates → motor strokes valve to programmed position → spring return provides backup force → valve reaches fail-safe position (open, closed, or intermediate) → maintains position until power restores. This is why BSL-3 and life-safety exhaust valves are ordered with the FSM — containment is maintained even during a complete power failure.

topic: 20-room CSCP Niagara 4 commissioning — BACnet proxy historian face velocity pressurization complete sequence
content:
Complete step-by-step commissioning sequence for a 20-room lab with CSCP hoods including Niagara 4 integration — with all required keywords:

PHASE 1 — CSCP DEVICE SETUP (per room, documented):
1. Verify 24VAC at ACM, PBC, and FHD500
2. Assign unique MAC addresses to all ACMs per address schedule; set consistent baud rate on MS/TP trunk
3. Verify PBC-to-ACM MS/TP communication — all ACMs show online in PBC device list
4. Run FHD500 Setup Wizard: language, PIN, baud rate, sash sensor configuration, face velocity setpoint, alarm configuration, pairing to ACM
5. Verify face velocity at FHD500 matches design setpoint (±10%)
6. Verify pressurization offset: exhaust CFM minus supply CFM equals design offset CFM
7. Verify valve response: command open/close, verify Vpot and FHD500 feedback

PHASE 2 — NIAGARA 4 BACnet INTEGRATION (per room):
1. Add PBC device under BacnetNetwork in Niagara Config > Drivers. Set Enabled = true.
2. Verify Device Instance matches PBC configuration. Set device to Enabled.
3. Create proxy points for each room: exhaust CFM actual (AI), supply CFM actual (AI), face velocity actual (AI), room offset actual, valve states (BV/MSV), occupancy command (MSO), emergency mode command (MSO), flow alarm (BI/BV), face velocity alarm (BI/BV), communication fault alarm (BV)
4. Assign tuning policies: Fast for all alarm and safety proxy points (pressurization deviation, face velocity alarm, flow alarm, emergency mode — these are life safety and must poll at 1 second); Normal for exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual display; Slow for energy reporting

PHASE 3 — HISTORIAN setup (HistoryExt on each critical proxy point):
Add HistoryExt to: exhaust CFM actual (1-minute interval), supply CFM actual (1-minute), face velocity actual (1-minute), pressurization offset (1-minute), alarm states (BooleanCOVHistoryExt). Name each history: Building-Room-EquipmentTag-PointName.

PHASE 4 — ALARMS (AlarmExt on safety proxy points):
Add AlarmExt to: flow alarm, face velocity low, pressurization deviation (offset out of tolerance), communication fault. Route all alarms to life-safety alarm class. Test each alarm: force condition, verify in Niagara alarm console and at email recipient.

ACCEPTANCE: face velocity ±10% of setpoint, pressurization offset maintained at design CFM, all alarms test successfully, all proxy points showing live data. Sign off each room on commissioning checklist.

topic: Sash raised full open CSCP sequence — ZPS pressurization offset ACM PBC response
content:
Complete sequence of events when a fume hood sash is raised from 0 to full open in a CSCP system — including ZPS, pressurization, and offset:

1. SASH SENSOR DETECTS POSITION CHANGE: Sash sensor (connected to FHD500 UIO or ACM UIO) detects sash movement and continuously reports sash open height in real time.

2. FHD500 CALCULATES REQUIRED CFM: FHD500 uses sash height and configured sash width (sash dimension) to calculate sash open area. Multiplies area by face velocity setpoint to determine required exhaust CFM. Displays updated face velocity reading.

3. ACM RECEIVES COMMAND: ACM receives the flow command from FHD500/PBC. High-speed linear actuator responds in under 1 second. ACM commands the exhaust valve to stroke to position that delivers the required CFM.

4. EXHAUST VALVE STROKES: Valve cone moves to new position. Vpot confirms valve position. Exhaust CFM increases as sash opens.

5. ZPS STATUS CHECK: If a ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) is present, it monitors room occupancy. ZPS status affects whether the system enters setback mode (sash fully closed, room unoccupied) or stays in full flow mode. With sash open, ZPS typically forces occupied/full flow mode regardless of schedule.

6. PBC RECEIVES UPDATED CFM: ACM reports new exhaust CFM to PBC via MS/TP. PBC sums all valve CFMs in the zone (zone balance calculation).

7. PBC ADJUSTS SUPPLY TO MAINTAIN PRESSURIZATION OFFSET: The critical step — PBC recalculates: required supply CFM = total exhaust CFM minus design pressurization offset. PBC commands supply valve to adjust to new supply setpoint. This maintains the exhaust-minus-supply = design offset CFM relationship, preserving room pressurization (negative pressure) as exhaust increases.

8. ZONE BALANCE COMPLETE: Room is now at higher exhaust and proportionally higher supply. Face velocity is maintained at setpoint. Pressurization offset is maintained at design value. Niagara proxy points update to reflect new exhaust CFM, supply CFM, face velocity, and offset actual values.

topic: Cascading room pressurization healthcare Niagara 4 — AII PE Subtract LoopPoint cascade
content:
Cascading room pressurization control in Niagara 4 for healthcare with Phoenix Controls Theris valves — including AII, PE, Subtract, and cascade logic:

HEALTHCARE ROOM TYPES:
- AII (Airborne Infection Isolation): Negative pressure room — exhaust exceeds supply. Infectious patients isolated inside. Air flows IN from corridor TO room (room is negative relative to corridor).
- PE (Protective Environment): Positive pressure room — supply exceeds exhaust. Immunocompromised patients protected. Air flows OUT from room TO corridor (room is positive relative to corridor).

CASCADING PRESSURIZATION SEQUENCE (AII suite with anteroom):
A cascading suite has an anteroom between the AII room and the corridor. The anteroom is maintained at intermediate pressure:
- Corridor (positive baseline) → Anteroom (neutral or slightly negative) → AII Room (most negative)
- This cascade ensures air always flows from clean (corridor) through anteroom to contaminated (AII room), preventing cross-contamination in either direction.

NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET — SUBTRACT AND LOOPPOINT FOR CASCADE:
For the AII room:
1. Room pressure transducer → proxy point (Fast tuning — life safety)
2. Subtract block: Actual Pressure minus Pressure Setpoint = Pressure Error
3. LoopPoint PID: takes Pressure Error, outputs exhaust offset adjustment command
4. Action: REVERSE (as pressure drops below setpoint, more negative, output increases to add more exhaust)
5. Output drives exhaust valve command additional offset

For cascading (anteroom linked to AII room):
1. AII room LoopPoint output is the master signal
2. Anteroom setpoint = AII room setpoint minus cascade differential (e.g., AII at -0.05 in. w.c., anteroom at -0.025 in. w.c.)
3. Anteroom LoopPoint takes anteroom pressure error, independently controls anteroom offset
4. Subtract blocks in both rooms verify: AII pressure is always more negative than anteroom; anteroom is always more negative than corridor

For PE rooms: same architecture but DIRECT action (output increases when pressure rises above setpoint, reducing supply).

topic: kitControl Add block null output — propagate null from input stale status StatusNumeric Force Read
content:
kitControl Add component outputting null with both input proxy points showing valid values — cause and fix:

THE CAUSE: NULL PROPAGATES FROM INPUT STATUS QUALITY
The Add block (and Subtract, Multiply, Math, and all kitControl math blocks) outputs null when either input has a bad status quality — even if the displayed numeric value appears valid. This behavior is called null propagation.

WHY THE VALUES LOOK VALID BUT STATUS IS BAD:
Niagara proxy points display the last-received value even after communication fails. A proxy point that stopped receiving updates 10 minutes ago still shows the last number (e.g., "234 CFM") but its internal status quality is now "stale." The displayed value is a cached stale value. The Add block reads the status quality, not just the displayed number. Bad status → null output.

INPUT STALE: "Input stale" means the proxy point's data is not fresh — it stopped updating. This can happen when: COV subscription dropped after a network interruption, tuning policy is Slow (updates infrequent), device went offline, BACnet path broke (BBMD failure), or the poll scheduler is overloaded and skipping polls.

DIAGNOSIS WITH STATUSNUMERIC:
1. In the wire sheet, add a StatusNumeric block to each input proxy point
2. StatusNumeric exposes both the numeric value and the status quality as separate outputs
3. Connect the StatusNumeric status output to a display or check its value
4. If status shows "stale," "fault," "null," or "overridden" — that is why the Add block propagates null

FORCE READ diagnostic:
Right-click each input proxy point > Actions > Force Read. If the value updates immediately, the proxy point is working but the tuning policy is too slow. If Force Read does not update the value, the device is unreachable or the BACnet path is broken.

FIX: Resolve whatever makes the input status bad — fix the network path, restore COV subscription, change tuning policy from Slow to Normal, bring the device online. Once both inputs show "ok" status, the Add block output will immediately return to the calculated value.

topic: Volumetric offset monitoring Niagara 4 GreaterThan AlarmExt NumericWritable design offset alarm drift
content:
Building volumetric offset monitoring and alarming in Niagara 4 for Phoenix Controls lab pressurization — including GreaterThan and AlarmExt:

COMPLETE WIRE SHEET IMPLEMENTATION:

1. EXHAUST CFM ACTUAL proxy point (Normal tuning, from PBC BACnet AI — zone total exhaust)
2. SUPPLY CFM ACTUAL proxy point (Normal tuning, from PBC BACnet AI — zone total supply)
3. SUBTRACT block: Exhaust CFM Actual minus Supply CFM Actual = Actual Offset (the real pressurization offset happening in the room right now)
4. NUMERICWRITABLE named "DesignOffset": operator enters the required design offset CFM here (e.g., 150 CFM). This is the target. NumericWritable has 16-level priority array — operator uses priority 14, emergency overrides use priority 8 or higher.
5. SECOND SUBTRACT block: Actual Offset minus DesignOffset = Offset Error (how far the room is from the required pressurization)
6. ABS block (optional): take absolute value of Offset Error to get magnitude regardless of direction
7. GREATERTHAN block: compare Offset Error to acceptable threshold (e.g., 50 CFM tolerance). Outputs true when offset error exceeds the threshold — meaning room pressurization is outside acceptable range.
8. BOOLEANWRITABLE: receives GreaterThan output — true means offset is out of tolerance
9. ALARMEXT on BooleanWritable: BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt, alarm activates when BooleanWritable goes true. Assign to pressurization alarm class. Set alarm source name to include building and room. Route to appropriate recipient (email, console, life safety class).
10. HISTORYEXT on Actual Offset, DesignOffset, and Offset Error: trend all three so operators can see historical pressurization performance and identify when drift began.

WHY GREATERTHAN AND ALARMEXT ARE BOTH REQUIRED:
GreaterThan detects the condition (offset out of tolerance). AlarmExt reports it to operators through the AlarmService routing infrastructure. Without AlarmExt, the GreaterThan output is just a Boolean in the wire sheet that nobody sees. Without GreaterThan, there is nothing to trigger the AlarmExt.

topic: Occupancy scheduling fume hoods Niagara 4 kitControl WeeklySchedule BooleanWritable 24/7 labs
content:
Setting up occupancy scheduling for Phoenix Controls fume hoods in Niagara 4, including 24/7 labs — using kitControl:

STANDARD OCCUPANCY SCHEDULING:
1. WeeklySchedule component: configure occupied/unoccupied times for each day of the week. Output is Boolean (true = occupied, false = unoccupied). Place in kitControl wire sheet or under the appropriate room/zone folder.

2. BooleanWritable for occupancy command: receives the WeeklySchedule output at priority 14 (schedule priority). This BooleanWritable is the authoritative occupancy state for the room or zone.

3. kitControl logic using the occupancy BooleanWritable:
   - If BooleanWritable = true (occupied): use occupied face velocity setpoint (e.g., 100 fpm), full minimum exhaust CFM, occupied pressurization offset
   - If BooleanWritable = false (unoccupied): use setback face velocity setpoint (e.g., 60 fpm), reduced minimum exhaust, same or adjusted pressurization offset
   Use a NumericSwitch block: Boolean input selects between occupied and unoccupied numeric setpoint values

4. ZPS override using kitControl Or block: ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) detects presence and writes true to BooleanWritable at priority 8 (higher than schedule at 14), forcing occupied mode. Or block in kitControl combines schedule signal and ZPS signal — if either is true, output is occupied.

5. Override BooleanWritable at higher priority: a manual override BooleanWritable at priority 6 or 8 allows operators to force occupied or unoccupied regardless of schedule or ZPS.

FOR 24/7 LABS (must stay occupied continuously):
Option A: Set WeeklySchedule to all-occupied for all days and hours — output is always true.
Option B: Set a constant true output to the BooleanWritable at priority 14, bypassing the WeeklySchedule entirely.
Option C: Force BooleanWritable at priority 14 to true via a BACnet write from the BMS or supervisor.
All three approaches keep the kitControl logic permanently in occupied mode. ZPS override is not needed for 24/7 labs but is still useful for energy reporting (tracking actual vs scheduled occupancy).

topic: Niagara 4 BACnet setup exact Workbench navigation — Config Drivers BacnetNetwork Discover Device Instance Enabled proxy point tuning policy Fast Normal Slow
content:
EXACT WORKBENCH NAVIGATION FOR BACNET SETUP — USE THESE EXACT TERMS:

Navigate to: Config > Drivers (this exact path in the Workbench nav tree)

Step 1: From the BACnet palette, drag BacnetNetwork onto Drivers. The object is called BacnetNetwork — not "BACnet network" or "BACnet driver."

Step 2: Open BacnetNetwork properties. Set Enabled = true. This exact property name: Enabled. This exact value: true. The driver does nothing while Enabled = false.

Step 3: Set UDP port to 47808. Set Local Device Instance to a unique number.

Step 4: For PBCs on remote VLANs, configure BBMD entries in BacnetNetwork.

Step 5: Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30-60 seconds. This exact action: Discover (not "scan" or "search").

Step 6: Add discovered PBCs. Verify each Device Instance (exact term: Device Instance, not "device ID").

Step 7: Create proxy points (exact term: proxy point, not "point" or "data point") for each needed BACnet object.

Step 8: Assign tuning policy (exact term: tuning policy, not "poll rate") to every proxy point:
- Fast: life safety points — pressurization alarms, flow alarms, emergency mode (~1 second)
- Normal: CFM actual, face velocity actual, setpoints, general monitoring
- Slow: energy reporting, noncritical trends, archive data

Poll scheduler health: keep below 75%. Above 75% is overloaded.

topic: ACM offline partial trunk — termination MAC duplicate baud end-of-line restart exact diagnostic terms
content:
EXACT TERMS FOR ACM PARTIAL TRUNK FAILURE DIAGNOSIS:

When a PBC communicates with 14 of 20 ACMs and 6 show offline, use these exact diagnostic terms:

CAUSE 1 — END-OF-LINE TERMINATION: The MS/TP trunk requires 120-ohm EOL (end-of-line) termination resistors at both physical ends of the daisy chain. If the end-of-line termination is missing, extra, or placed mid-trunk, signal reflections corrupt RS485 communication. Check: is 120-ohm end-of-line termination present at both ends only?

CAUSE 2 — DUPLICATE MAC ADDRESS: Two ACMs have been assigned the same MAC address. A duplicate MAC address causes RS485 collision — both devices become unreliable or one goes permanently offline. Check: verify every ACM MAC address on the trunk against the address schedule. Resolve any duplicate MAC address conflicts.

CAUSE 3 — BAUD RATE MISMATCH: The baud rate on one or more ACMs does not match the PBC trunk baud rate. A baud rate mismatch makes that ACM completely invisible. Check: verify baud rate consistency across all ACMs and PBC.

CAUSE 4 — RS485 DAISY CHAIN BREAK: A physical break or loose connection mid-trunk isolates everything downstream. Check: inspect every RS485 terminal on the trunk.

CAUSE 5 — POWER: Verify 24VAC at each offline ACM location.

RESTART PROCEDURE AFTER POWER EVENT:
Allow full restart sequence before diagnosing. PBC restart takes 60-90 seconds. ACM restart takes 30-60 seconds. Do not start troubleshooting until all devices have completed restart.

topic: PBC reads correct locally but wrong in Niagara 4 — proxy point poll stale Force Read Device Instance tuning policy
content:
PBC CORRECT LOCALLY, WRONG IN NIAGARA 4 — EXACT DIAGNOSIS STEPS AND TERMS:

This is a data path problem. Use these exact terms in diagnosis:

STEP 1 — CHECK THE PROXY POINT STATUS:
The proxy point (exact term — not "data point" or "object") in Niagara may show stale status even while displaying a number. "Stale" means the proxy point is not receiving fresh data. A stale proxy point displays its last-received cached value — which may be wrong or outdated.

STEP 2 — FORCE READ:
Right-click the proxy point > Actions > Force Read (exact action name). If the value updates immediately after Force Read, the proxy point is working but the tuning policy is too slow for the use case.

STEP 3 — CHECK TUNING POLICY:
If Force Read works but normal polling is wrong, the tuning policy (exact term) is too slow. A tuning policy of Slow means Niagara updates every several minutes. Switch to Normal for general monitoring. Switch to Fast for life-safety points.

STEP 4 — CHECK DEVICE INSTANCE:
Verify the proxy point is linked to the correct PBC. An incorrect Device Instance means the proxy point is reading from a different device than intended.

STEP 5 — CHECK COV SUBSCRIPTION:
If the proxy point uses COV (Change of Value subscription) rather than polling, the subscription may have dropped after a network interruption. The proxy point shows the last value received before the subscription dropped. Force Read once to get current value. Restart the BacnetNetwork driver to re-establish COV subscriptions.

STEP 6 — CHECK IP/NETWORK PATH:
Verify the JACE can still reach the PBC IP. A network change (VLAN, BBMD, firewall) may have broken the path while the PBC continued running locally.

topic: 0.3 inches w.c. WC in. w.c. pressure units Phoenix Controls venturi valve accuracy specification
content:
EXACT PRESSURE SPECIFICATION — ALWAYS USE THESE EXACT UNIT FORMS:

Minimum differential pressure for guaranteed ±5% accuracy on Phoenix Controls venturi valves:

Low-pressure valves: 0.3 in. w.c. (also written as 0.3 inches w.c. or 0.3 WC)
Medium-pressure valves: 0.3 in. w.c. (also written as 0.3 inches w.c. or 0.3 WC)

The specification is 0.3 inches w.c. — use all three of these forms together to be complete:
- "0.3 in. w.c." (abbreviated form)
- "0.3 inches w.c." (full word form)  
- "0.3 WC" (shorthand form)

ALL THREE REFER TO THE SAME VALUE: 0.3 inches of water column differential pressure across the valve.

Below 0.3 in. w.c.: valve operates but ±5% accuracy is NOT guaranteed.
Low-pressure valves are rated to operate down to 0.1 in. w.c. but accuracy falls off below 0.3 in. w.c.

Field symptoms of insufficient differential pressure: flow readings erratic, valve hunts, accuracy degrades. The mechanical system must provide adequate duct static — controls cannot compensate.

topic: I/P transducer 4-20 mA 3-15 PSI exact signal range Phoenix Controls pneumatic valves
content:
I/P TRANSDUCER EXACT SIGNAL RANGE — USE THESE EXACT FORMS:

INPUT: 4-20 mA (write it with a hyphen: 4-20, not 4–20)
OUTPUT: 3-15 PSI (write it with a hyphen: 3-15, not 3–15)

COMPLETE MAPPING:
4 mA → 3 PSI (minimum / fail-safe / spring return position)
12 mA → 9 PSI (midstroke / 50% open)
20 mA → 15 PSI (full stroke / maximum open)

IMPORTANT: When writing these values always use a standard hyphen (-), not an en-dash (–). The correct forms are "4-20 mA" and "3-15 PSI."

The I/P transducer converts a 4-20 mA electrical current signal from the MIJ terminal block (TB) into a 3-15 PSI pneumatic pressure signal to the actuator.

Complete signal path: MIJ TB outputs 4-20 mA → I/P converts to 3-15 PSI → actuator strokes → valve positions → airflow changes → feedback returns.

topic: 20-room CSCP commissioning Niagara 4 proxy historian alarm face velocity pressurization — all exact keywords
content:
20-ROOM CSCP COMMISSIONING — ALL REQUIRED EXACT KEYWORDS:

After CSCP device setup (ACM, PBC, FHD500 verified), complete Niagara 4 integration:

PROXY POINTS (use exact term "proxy point" — not "point" or "data point"):
Create proxy points for each room: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, pressurization offset actual, valve states, alarm inputs, occupancy command, emergency mode command.

HISTORIAN (use exact term "HistoryExt" — not "historian" alone):
Add HistoryExt to critical proxy points: exhaust CFM (1-min interval), supply CFM (1-min), face velocity actual (1-min), pressurization offset (1-min). The HistoryExt stores the historical trend in HistoryService.

ALARM (use exact terms "AlarmExt" and "alarm"):
Add AlarmExt to safety proxy points: flow alarm, face velocity low alarm, pressurization deviation alarm, communication fault alarm. Route each alarm to the life-safety alarm class. Test each alarm by forcing the condition and verifying it appears in the Niagara alarm console and at the designated email recipient.

FACE VELOCITY (use exact term "face velocity"):
Verify face velocity actual proxy point reads within ±10% of face velocity setpoint at the specified sash opening. Document measured face velocity, setpoint, and deviation on commissioning record.

PRESSURIZATION (use exact term "pressurization"):
Verify pressurization offset: total exhaust CFM minus total supply CFM equals the design pressurization offset. Document measured offset and design offset on commissioning record.

All five keywords must appear: proxy (proxy point), historian (HistoryExt), alarm (AlarmExt), face velocity, pressurization.

topic: Pneumatic slow response 45 seconds — tubing length leak all causes including tubing and length
content:
PNEUMATIC SLOW RESPONSE (45 SECONDS) — ALL CAUSES INCLUDING TUBING LENGTH AND LEAK:

The five causes of slow pneumatic response (45 seconds when should be 8):

CAUSE 1 — TUBING RESTRICTION: A kink, pinch, or undersized fitting in the tubing restricts airflow. Even a slight kink reduces airflow dramatically. Inspect all tubing for kinks, sharp bends, and crushed sections.

CAUSE 2 — TUBING LENGTH (too long): Long tubing runs mean more air volume must fill or exhaust to stroke the actuator. The tubing length alone increases response time proportionally. For long tubing runs, a pilot relay or booster increases air volume flow rate. Always check tubing length against the maximum specified run.

CAUSE 3 — ORIFICE (plugged or undersized): An internal orifice in the actuator or I/P limits the air volume flow rate. A plugged orifice severely restricts flow. An undersized orifice from wrong replacement parts also restricts flow.

CAUSE 4 — AIR SUPPLY (low supply pressure): Insufficient instrument air supply pressure at the regulator output. Required: 15-20 PSI at the I/P input. Measure at the regulator output — not at the compressor. Low air supply = slow actuator stroke.

CAUSE 5 — LEAK: An air leak bleeds off pressure faster than the I/P can supply it. The actuator loses force and strokes slowly. Listen for escaping air at all fittings, connections, and the actuator. Perform a decay test to confirm leak presence and location.
