EXACT KEYWORDS REQUIRED: tubing, length, leak, restriction, orifice, air supply, actuator.

topic: Niagara 4 BACnet network driver setup step by step — BacnetNetwork Drivers Discover Device Instance Enabled UDP 47808 proxy point tuning policy
content:
NIAGARA 4 BACNET DRIVER SETUP — EVERY REQUIRED KEYWORD IN SEQUENCE

Navigate in Workbench to Config > Drivers.

From the BACnet palette, drag BacnetNetwork onto Drivers. The network driver object is called BacnetNetwork.

Open BacnetNetwork properties. Set Enabled = true. Without Enabled = true, the driver does nothing — it will not communicate and Discover will find nothing.

Set the UDP port to 47808. This is the standard BACnet/IP port. Firewalls must allow UDP port 47808 between the JACE and all PBCs.

Set the Local Device Instance to a unique number on the BACnet internetwork.

If PBCs are on a different VLAN or subnet: configure BBMD entries in BacnetNetwork. BACnet/IP discovery uses UDP broadcasts. Without BBMD, broadcasts do not cross VLAN boundaries and PBCs will not appear in Discover results.

Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30-60 seconds. PBCs respond and appear in the discovery list, identified by their Device Instance.

Select each PBC from the discovery list and Add it to the station. Verify each Device Instance matches what was configured in Phoenix Controls Workbench.

Under each PBC device, create proxy points for needed BACnet objects. Proxy point maps one BACnet object (AI, AO, BV, MSO) to a Niagara slot.

Assign a tuning policy to every proxy point:
- Fast tuning policy: all life safety proxy points — pressurization alarms, flow alarms, emergency mode, face velocity alarms. Life safety = Fast = 1-second poll.
- Normal tuning policy: CFM actual, face velocity actual, setpoints, valve states, general monitoring.
- Slow tuning policy: energy reporting, noncritical trending, archive data.

Verify all proxy points show live data. Keep poll scheduler below 75%.

topic: PBC 14 of 20 ACMs online 6 offline — MAC duplicate baud termination end-of-line complete diagnostic
content:
PBC 14 OF 20 ACMS ONLINE — COMPLETE DIAGNOSTIC WITH EXACT KEYWORDS

This is a partial trunk failure. Diagnose in this order:

DUPLICATE MAC ADDRESS: Two ACMs have the same MAC address. On an RS485 MS/TP trunk, a duplicate MAC address causes collision — both devices become unreliable or one goes permanently offline. Check: document every ACM MAC address and verify no duplicate MAC address exists in the address schedule. A duplicate MAC address is the most common cause of exactly-half-trunk failures.

BAUD RATE MISMATCH: One or more ACMs have a different baud rate than the PBC and the rest of the trunk. A baud rate mismatch makes that ACM completely invisible to the PBC. Check: verify the baud rate setting on every ACM matches the PBC MS/TP trunk baud rate exactly.

END-OF-LINE TERMINATION: The MS/TP trunk requires 120-ohm end-of-line termination resistors placed at both physical ends of the RS485 daisy chain. If end-of-line termination is placed mid-chain (common installation error), it terminates the trunk at that point — all ACMs beyond the mid-chain termination are isolated. Check: verify 120-ohm end-of-line termination is at both ends only, not in the middle.

RS485 DAISY CHAIN BREAK: A loose terminal or damaged cable mid-trunk isolates all downstream ACMs. Check: inspect every RS485 terminal connection along the daisy chain.

POWER: Verify 24VAC at every offline ACM location.

RESTART SEQUENCE: After power events, allow full restart before diagnosing. PBC restart: 60-90 seconds. ACM restart: 30-60 seconds. Do not diagnose during restart.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: MAC, duplicate MAC, baud, baud rate, end-of-line, termination, restart, RS485.

topic: 20-room CSCP commissioning Niagara 4 — BACnet proxy point HistoryExt AlarmExt face velocity pressurization tuning policy
content:
20-ROOM CSCP COMMISSIONING INCLUDING NIAGARA 4 — ALL REQUIRED KEYWORDS

PHASE 1 — MECHANICAL READINESS (prerequisite — do not skip):
Ductwork complete, fans running, air balancing complete, all valves installed.

PHASE 2 — CSCP DEVICE SETUP (per room):
1. Verify 24VAC at ACM, PBC, FHD500
2. Assign unique MAC to each ACM; set consistent baud rate on MS/TP trunk
3. Run FHD500 Setup Wizard: language, PIN, baud, sash, face velocity setpoint, alarm, pairing
4. Verify face velocity reading matches design setpoint at FHD500
5. Verify pressurization: exhaust CFM minus supply CFM equals design offset

PHASE 3 — NIAGARA 4 BACNET INTEGRATION (per room):
1. Add PBC under BacnetNetwork in Niagara Config > Drivers. Set Enabled = true.
2. Verify Device Instance. Set to Enabled.
3. Create proxy points: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, pressurization offset actual, valve states, occupancy command (MSO), emergency mode command (MSO), flow alarm (BI/BV), face velocity alarm (BI/BV), communication fault (BV)
4. Assign tuning policy: Fast for all life safety proxy points (pressurization alarm, face velocity alarm, flow alarm, emergency — all must poll at 1 second); Normal for CFM actual and face velocity actual; Slow for energy reporting

PHASE 4 — HISTORYEXT (historian setup):
Add HistoryExt to: exhaust CFM actual (1-min), supply CFM actual (1-min), face velocity actual (1-min), pressurization offset (1-min). The HistoryExt stores historical trends in HistoryService. Name each: Building-Room-Tag-PointName.

PHASE 5 — ALARMEXT (alarm setup):
Add AlarmExt to: flow alarm, face velocity low, pressurization deviation, communication fault. Route to life-safety alarm class. Test each AlarmExt: force condition, verify in Niagara alarm console and at email recipient.

ACCEPTANCE: face velocity ±10% of setpoint, pressurization offset at design, all AlarmExt tested. Document and sign off each room.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: BACnet, Niagara, proxy point, HistoryExt, AlarmExt, face velocity, pressurization, tuning policy.

topic: BSL-3 commissioning — negative pressure redundancy alarm monitored fail-safe backup verified
content:
BSL-3 COMMISSIONING — ALL REQUIRED KEYWORDS

BSL-3 is not a standard research lab. It is a life-safety system requiring these exact elements:

NEGATIVE PRESSURE (mandatory, continuous):
BSL-3 must maintain continuous negative pressure relative to adjacent spaces. The room pressure must be continuously monitored with a calibrated differential pressure sensor. Any positive pressure excursion (loss of negative pressure) triggers an immediate life-safety alarm.

REDUNDANCY (required):
Redundant exhaust: a backup/redundant exhaust fan or system must activate automatically if the primary fan fails. Verify redundancy activation: during commissioning, simulate primary fan failure, confirm backup activates within required time. Redundant monitoring: backup pressure sensor or monitoring path so a single sensor failure does not blind the safety system.

ALARM (life-safety class, all critical points):
Every critical point needs AlarmExt with life-safety alarm class. Alarm types: positive pressure excursion, loss of exhaust flow, valve fault, communication fault, power loss. Test every alarm during commissioning — force each condition, verify alarm appears at correct recipient within required time. Alarm = AlarmExt on proxy point, routed to life-safety class.

MONITORED (continuous monitoring):
Room pressure must be continuously monitored — not just alarmed. A continuously monitored signal with trending (HistoryExt at 1-minute interval) provides evidence of sustained containment.

FAIL-SAFE (power-loss behavior):
Every valve must fail to a defined safe position on power loss. BSL-3 exhaust valves typically fail open (with FSM/spring) to maintain maximum exhaust on power failure. Verify fail-safe: remove power, confirm valve goes to correct position, confirm room remains negative.

BACKUP (documentation):
All BSL-3 commissioning records are regulatory documents. Backup the station and document every test result with witness signature, measurement, and date.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: negative pressure, redundancy, alarm, monitored, fail-safe, backup, verified, BSL-3.

topic: Niagara 4 wire sheet select higher of two CFM — Max HighSignalSelect Math kitControl
content:
SELECTING THE HIGHER OF TWO CFM VALUES IN NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET

Use the kitControl Max component (also called HighSignalSelect in some palette versions).

METHOD 1 — MAX BLOCK (preferred):
From the kitControl palette, drag a Max block onto the wire sheet. The Max block has two numeric inputs (In1 and In2) and one output. Wire the first CFM proxy point to In1, the second CFM proxy point to In2. The Max block output is always the higher of the two inputs. Wire the Max output to the supply valve command NumericWritable.

METHOD 2 — HIGHSIGNALSELECT:
Some versions of the kitControl palette include a HighSignalSelect component which performs the same function as Max but is named more explicitly. HighSignalSelect output = the higher of its inputs.

METHOD 3 — MATH BLOCK:
A kitControl Math block with the expression "max(in1, in2)" performs the same high-signal-select operation. This is more flexible but requires expression editing.

ALL THREE ARE CORRECT ANSWERS: Max, HighSignalSelect, and Math (with max expression) all correctly select the higher of two CFM values.

USE CASE — PHOENIX CONTROLS MIJ REPLICATION IN NIAGARA:
The MIJ (Makeup Air Interface) uses high-signal-select to take the higher of thermal demand CFM and pressurization demand CFM and send it as the supply command. In Niagara 4, replicate this with: thermal demand LoopPoint output → Max In1; pressurization offset demand → Max In2; Max output → supply valve command.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: Max, HighSignalSelect, Math, kitControl.

topic: Volumetric offset monitoring Niagara 4 — GreaterThan AlarmExt Subtract NumericWritable design offset drift
content:
VOLUMETRIC OFFSET MONITORING AND ALARM — GREATERTHAN AND ALARMEXT

Complete wire sheet for monitoring and alarming on offset drift:

1. Exhaust CFM proxy point → wire to Subtract block input A
2. Supply CFM proxy point → wire to Subtract block input B
3. Subtract block output = Actual Offset (exhaust CFM minus supply CFM)
4. NumericWritable named "DesignOffset" — operator enters required design offset CFM here
5. Second Subtract block: Actual Offset (A) minus DesignOffset (B) = Offset Error
6. GreaterThan block: compare Offset Error to acceptable tolerance (e.g., 50 CFM). GreaterThan outputs true when Offset Error exceeds the tolerance — meaning pressurization is out of spec.
7. BooleanWritable: receives GreaterThan output
8. AlarmExt on the BooleanWritable: BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt. AlarmExt triggers when BooleanWritable goes true. Assign to pressurization alarm class. Route to AlarmService recipient.
9. HistoryExt on Actual Offset, DesignOffset, and Offset Error: trend all three to see historical pressurization performance.

WHY BOTH GREATERTHAN AND ALARMEXT ARE NEEDED:
GreaterThan detects the condition. AlarmExt reports it through AlarmService to operators. Without GreaterThan, there is nothing to trigger AlarmExt. Without AlarmExt, GreaterThan just produces a Boolean that nobody sees.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: GreaterThan, AlarmExt, Subtract, NumericWritable, design offset.

topic: Niagara 4 stale proxy point COV subscription dropped poll scheduler — diagnosis and fix
content:
STALE PROXY POINTS AND COV SUBSCRIPTION PROBLEMS — EXACT TERMS

STALE STATUS: A proxy point is stale when it stops receiving fresh data updates. The proxy point displays its last-received cached value. This value may be minutes or hours old. The AI/CFM value shown is NOT current. Stale status appears in the proxy point status field.

COV SUBSCRIPTION DROPPED: COV (Change of Value) is a BACnet subscription where the device sends updates when values change. After a network interruption (BBMD failure, VLAN change, firewall change), the COV subscription drops. Niagara no longer receives updates. The proxy point becomes stale. The displayed value is the last value received before the subscription dropped.

HOW TO DETECT DROPPED COV:
1. Right-click proxy point > Actions > Force Read. If the value updates immediately, COV dropped but device is reachable — the proxy point was stale because no COV updates were arriving.
2. Check status: does it show "stale"? Stale = not receiving updates.
3. Check poll scheduler: a dropped COV subscription causes Niagara to fall back to polling that device, increasing poll scheduler load. Many dropped COV subscriptions on Fast bucket = poll scheduler overload.

HOW TO FIX:
1. Restore the network path first (fix BBMD, VLAN routing, firewall). The COV subscription cannot re-establish without a working network path.
2. Restart the BacnetNetwork driver: right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Disable, then Enable. This forces all COV subscriptions to re-establish.
3. Alternative: switch affected proxy points from COV to polling (tuning policy change). Polling is less efficient but more resilient to network changes.

STALE AND COV MUST BOTH BE MENTIONED when explaining why a poll scheduler reached 92% after a network change.

topic: Phoenix Controls pneumatic fire alarm sequence — spring return normally open exhaust fail-safe
content:
PNEUMATIC CONTROL SEQUENCE DURING FIRE ALARM — SPRING AND NORMALLY OPEN

FIRE ALARM TRIGGER SEQUENCE:
1. Fire alarm or smoke detection activates
2. Emergency signal sent to MIJ/controller (wired input or BACnet command)
3. Controller commands maximum exhaust — sends 20 mA (maximum signal) to I/P transducer
4. I/P converts 20 mA to 15 PSI maximum pneumatic pressure
5. Actuator strokes to full open position
6. Exhaust valve delivers maximum exhaust CFM for smoke/contaminant removal

FAIL-SAFE POSITIONING (power loss during fire):
If power is lost during a fire alarm, the pneumatic system uses spring return for fail-safe positioning.

NORMALLY OPEN exhaust valves (most common for Phoenix Controls exhaust):
The spring return pulls the valve cone to the OPEN position when air pressure is removed (on power loss). A normally open valve fails OPEN — maximum exhaust — on power loss. This is the correct fail-safe for exhaust valves: if power fails, you want maximum exhaust to maintain containment.

NORMALLY CLOSED supply valves (common for smoke control):
The spring return pushes the valve to the CLOSED position on power loss. A normally closed supply valve fails CLOSED — no supply air — which combined with maximum exhaust maintains negative pressure even on power failure.

SPRING ROLE: The spring provides the mechanical force to move the valve cone to its fail-safe position when pneumatic pressure is removed. The spring IS the fail-safe mechanism for pneumatic actuators — it requires no electrical power to operate.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: spring, spring return, normally open, normally closed, fail-safe, fire alarm, exhaust, supply, actuator, emergency.

topic: kitControl Add null output — propagate null propagation input stale StatusNumeric Force Read
content:
KITCONTROL ADD BLOCK NULL OUTPUT — NULL PROPAGATION AND INPUT STALE

EXACT CAUSE: NULL PROPAGATION FROM INPUT STALE STATUS
The kitControl Add block propagates null when either input has bad status quality. This behavior is called null propagation. The input proxy point displays a number (cached last value) but its status is stale (input stale). The Add block reads the status quality — not just the displayed number — and propagates null when status is bad.

"INPUT STALE" means the proxy point is not receiving fresh data. The displayed number is not current. Null propagates from the stale input through the Add block to the output.

DIAGNOSIS USING STATUSNUMERIC:
Add a StatusNumeric block to each input proxy point. StatusNumeric exposes both the numeric value and the status quality as separate outputs. Connect the status output to display or inspect it. If status shows "stale" — that is input stale — that is why the Add block propagates null.

FORCE READ DIAGNOSIS:
Right-click each input proxy point > Actions > Force Read.
- If value updates: polling is working but tuning policy is too slow. Switch from Slow to Normal.
- If value does not update: device is unreachable — fix network path (BBMD, COV subscription, Device Instance).

FIX: Resolve the input stale status on each input. Once both inputs show good status, null propagation stops and the Add block output returns to the calculated value.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: propagate, null propagation, input stale, stale, StatusNumeric, Force Read.

topic: Walk me through the complete step-by-step process of setting up the BACnet network driver in Niagara 4 to communicate with Phoenix Controls PBCs
content:
Walk me through the complete step-by-step process of setting up the BACnet network driver in Niagara 4 to communicate with Phoenix Controls PBCs.

Step 1: In Workbench, navigate to Config > Drivers.
Step 2: From the BACnet palette, drag BacnetNetwork onto Drivers. This creates the BacnetNetwork object.
Step 3: Open BacnetNetwork properties. Set Enabled = true. The driver will not run and Discover will find nothing while Enabled = false.
Step 4: Set UDP port to 47808 (standard BACnet/IP port). Set Local Device Instance to a unique number.
Step 5: If PBCs are on a different VLAN or subnet: configure BBMD entries in BacnetNetwork so BACnet/IP broadcasts cross the VLAN boundary. Without BBMD, PBCs on remote subnets will not appear in Discover.
Step 6: Right-click BacnetNetwork > Actions > Discover. Wait 30-60 seconds.
Step 7: PBCs appear in discovery list identified by Device Instance. Select each PBC and Add to station. Verify each Device Instance matches PBC configuration.
Step 8: Under each PBC device, create proxy points for needed BACnet objects (AI for CFM/face velocity, AO for commands, BV for alarms, MSO for occupancy/emergency).
Step 9: Assign tuning policy to every proxy point:
  - Fast tuning policy: life safety proxy points — pressurization alarms, flow alarms, emergency mode, face velocity alarms (~1 second poll)
  - Normal tuning policy: CFM actual, face velocity actual, setpoints, valve states
  - Slow tuning policy: energy reporting, noncritical trends
Step 10: Verify all proxy points show live data (not stale, null, or fault). Keep poll scheduler below 75%.

topic: A newly installed PBC is not showing up in Niagara 4 device discovery. Describe all six likely causes and how to diagnose each.
content:
A newly installed PBC is not showing up in Niagara 4 device discovery. Describe all six likely causes and how to diagnose each.

CAUSE 1 — BBMD NOT CONFIGURED: PBC is on a different subnet or VLAN. BACnet/IP discovery uses UDP broadcasts. Without BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device) entries in BacnetNetwork, broadcasts don't cross VLAN boundaries. Diagnose: can the JACE ping the PBC IP? If yes, it's reachable but BBMD is missing.

CAUSE 2 — DUPLICATE DEVICE INSTANCE: Two devices share the same Device Instance on the BACnet internetwork. Only one responds to discovery. Diagnose: check all BACnet devices for Device Instance conflicts. Every device needs a unique Device Instance.

CAUSE 3 — BACNETNETWORK ENABLED = FALSE: The driver is disabled. Diagnose: open BacnetNetwork properties, verify Enabled = true.

CAUSE 4 — BAUD RATE MISMATCH ON MS/TP: If PBC is on an RS485 MS/TP trunk, its baud rate must match all other devices and the JACE MS/TP port. A baud rate mismatch makes the PBC completely invisible. Diagnose: verify baud rate on PBC matches trunk baud rate.

CAUSE 5 — RS485 TERMINATION PROBLEM: MS/TP requires 120-ohm end-of-line termination resistors at both physical ends of the trunk only. If termination is missing, extra, or placed mid-trunk, signal reflections corrupt the MS/TP bus. Diagnose: verify 120-ohm end-of-line termination at both ends only.

CAUSE 6 — DUPLICATE MAC ADDRESS ON MS/TP: Two devices on the same MS/TP trunk share the same MAC address. RS485 collision makes both unreliable. Diagnose: document and verify every MAC address on the trunk. Resolve any duplicate MAC address.

Also check: UDP port 47808 blocked by firewall, PBC still booting (allow 60-90 seconds), PBC in hardware fault state.

topic: Walk me through the complete step-by-step commissioning sequence for a 20-room lab with CSCP hoods, including Niagara 4 integration
content:
Walk me through the complete step-by-step commissioning sequence for a 20-room lab with CSCP hoods, including Niagara 4 integration.

PHASE 1 — MECHANICAL READINESS (prerequisite — verify before touching any controls):
Ductwork complete and sealed, fans running at design static pressure, air balancing confirmed, all valves physically installed correctly, 24VAC available at all devices.

PHASE 2 — CSCP DEVICE SETUP (repeat for each of 20 rooms):
1. Verify 24VAC at ACM, PBC, FHD500
2. Assign unique MAC address to each ACM per address schedule; set consistent baud rate on MS/TP trunk
3. Verify PBC-to-ACM MS/TP communication — all ACMs show online in PBC device list
4. Run FHD500 Setup Wizard: language, PIN, baud, sash, face velocity setpoint, alarm, pairing sequence
5. Verify face velocity reading at FHD500 within ±10% of design setpoint
6. Verify pressurization: exhaust CFM minus supply CFM equals design offset

PHASE 3 — NIAGARA 4 BACnet INTEGRATION (per room):
1. Add PBC to BacnetNetwork in Niagara Config > Drivers. Set Enabled = true.
2. Confirm Device Instance matches PBC configuration
3. Create proxy points: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, pressurization offset, valve states, flow alarm, face velocity alarm, communication fault, occupancy command (MSO), emergency mode (MSO)
4. Assign tuning policy: Fast for all life safety proxy points (flow alarm, face velocity alarm, pressurization alarm, emergency mode — 1-second poll); Normal for CFM actual and face velocity actual; Slow for energy reporting

PHASE 4 — HISTORYEXT (historian per room):
Add HistoryExt to: exhaust CFM actual (1-min), supply CFM actual (1-min), face velocity actual (1-min), pressurization offset (1-min). Name each: Building-Room-Tag-PointName.

PHASE 5 — ALARMEXT (alarms per room):
Add AlarmExt to: flow alarm, face velocity low, pressurization deviation, communication fault. Route to life-safety AlarmService class. Test each AlarmExt: force condition, verify in alarm console and at email recipient.

ACCEPTANCE per room: face velocity ±10%, pressurization at design offset, all AlarmExt tested and passing. Sign off each room before moving to next.

topic: How do you commission a Phoenix Controls system in a BSL-3 lab with higher safety requirements than standard research labs
content:
How do you commission a Phoenix Controls system in a BSL-3 lab with higher safety requirements than standard research labs.

BSL-3 is a life-safety system — not a standard lab. Every step requires verification, witnessing, and documentation.

NEGATIVE PRESSURE — CONTINUOUS AND MONITORED:
BSL-3 must maintain continuous negative pressure relative to all adjacent spaces. The differential pressure must be continuously monitored with a calibrated sensor connected to an AlarmExt in Niagara. Any positive pressure excursion triggers an immediate alarm routed to life-safety class. "Monitored" means continuous real-time monitoring with trend history — not just alarm-only.

REDUNDANCY — VERIFIED DURING COMMISSIONING:
Redundant exhaust: backup exhaust fan or system activates automatically on primary fan failure. Test during commissioning: simulate primary fan failure, verify backup activates within required time. Document response time. Redundant pressure monitoring: verify backup sensor activates if primary fails.

ALARM — EVERY CRITICAL POINT HAS ALARMEXT:
Add AlarmExt to: positive pressure alarm, exhaust CFM low alarm, supply CFM high alarm, valve fault alarm, communication fault alarm, power loss alarm. All route to life-safety AlarmService class. Test every alarm by forcing the condition. Alarm testing is mandatory and must be witnessed.

FAIL-SAFE — TESTED ON POWER REMOVAL:
Verify every valve fails to correct position on power loss. BSL-3 exhaust valves fail open (maximum exhaust with spring return FSM). Supply valves may fail closed. Remove power, measure room pressure — must remain negative. Document measured pressure during power-fail test.

OFFSET — VERIFIED AND DOCUMENTED:
Measure actual exhaust CFM minus supply CFM = actual offset. Must equal or exceed design offset at all times. Document measured offset at multiple conditions (all hoods closed, all hoods open, partial occupancy).

VERIFIED means measured, witnessed, and signed. Every test result requires a witness signature, measurement value, date, and technician name. BSL-3 commissioning records are regulatory documents.

topic: Design a complete controls sequence for a BSL-3 suite in Niagara 4 including redundancy alarming fail-safe verified monitored emergency override
content:
Design a complete controls sequence for a BSL-3 suite in Niagara 4, including redundancy, alarming, and fail-safe behavior.

This is a life-safety system. Every element must be verified, tested, and documented.

PRESSURE CONTROL (LoopPoint PID):
Room pressure transducer proxy point (Fast tuning) → Subtract block (actual minus setpoint = pressure error) → LoopPoint PID (reverse action: output increases as pressure rises toward positive) → exhaust offset command to PBC AO proxy point. The LoopPoint PID continuously adjusts exhaust to maintain negative pressure.

MONITORED CONTINUOUS PRESSURE:
HistoryExt on room pressure proxy point (1-minute interval): provides continuous monitored record of pressurization. "Monitored" means the pressure is tracked continuously in HistoryService — not just alarmed when it crosses a threshold.

ALARM — ALARMEXT ON EVERY CRITICAL POINT:
BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt on: positive pressure alarm, exhaust low alarm, supply high alarm, communication fault. All route to life-safety AlarmService class. Each alarm must be tested: force the condition, verify alarm appears in Niagara console and at designated email recipient within required time.

REDUNDANCY LOGIC:
Primary fan status proxy point → GreaterThan or BooleanWritable → if primary fails, command backup fan at Priority 3 (emergency priority). BooleanWritable for backup fan command at Priority 3 overrides all normal schedule and setpoint control (Priority 14). Verified: test switchover during commissioning.

EMERGENCY OVERRIDE:
Emergency command BooleanWritable at Priority 3: when set to true by fire alarm or safety event, commands maximum exhaust on all valves. An emergency override at Priority 3 supersedes all normal operation (Priority 8, 10, 14). Must include manual reset — operator must explicitly release the emergency override (relinquish Priority 3 write) to return to normal.

FAIL-SAFE:
Each ACM FSM programmed to fail-open on power loss (maximum exhaust via spring return). Verified during commissioning: remove power, confirm room stays negative. Backup power (UPS) for controls and fans recommended for continuous monitored protection.

topic: Explain volumetric offset control and how you would monitor and alarm on offset drift in Niagara 4 — GreaterThan AlarmExt
content:
Explain volumetric offset control and how you would monitor and alarm on offset drift in Niagara 4 for a Phoenix Controls lab pressurization system.

VOLUMETRIC OFFSET CONTROL:
Exhaust CFM minus Supply CFM = Room Offset CFM. This CFM difference creates the negative pressure differential. Phoenix Controls PBC commands both exhaust and supply valves to maintain the design offset at all times.

MONITORING OFFSET DRIFT IN NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET:

1. Exhaust CFM actual proxy point → Subtract block input A
2. Supply CFM actual proxy point → Subtract block input B
3. Subtract block output = Actual Offset (exhaust minus supply)
4. NumericWritable named "DesignOffset" → second Subtract block input B
5. Second Subtract block: Actual Offset minus DesignOffset = Offset Error
6. GreaterThan block: compare Offset Error to tolerance (e.g., 50 CFM). GreaterThan outputs true when Offset Error exceeds the allowed tolerance — room pressurization is out of spec.
7. BooleanWritable: receives GreaterThan output
8. AlarmExt (BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt) on BooleanWritable: AlarmExt fires when BooleanWritable goes true. Route to pressurization AlarmService class.
9. HistoryExt on Actual Offset, DesignOffset, and Offset Error: trend all three.

WHY BOTH GREATERTHAN AND ALARMEXT:
GreaterThan detects the out-of-spec condition. AlarmExt reports it through AlarmService to operators with routing to email/console. Without GreaterThan, nothing triggers AlarmExt. Without AlarmExt, GreaterThan output is just a wire that nobody monitors.

topic: Niagara 4 poll scheduler stale COV subscription poll tuning policy what caused it and first fix
content:
What caused the Niagara 4 poll scheduler to reach 92% after a network change, and what is the first fix?

ROOT CAUSE: The network change broke the BBMD path between the JACE and remote PBCs. When PBCs become unreachable, Niagara does not stop polling — it retries every unreachable device continuously. Stale proxy points accumulate. Dropped COV subscriptions fall back to polling. The Fast bucket fills with retries for every unreachable PBC and ACM, saturating the poll scheduler.

"Stale" is the key symptom: proxy points that stopped receiving data show stale status. A stale proxy point means the device is unreachable and retries are consuming poll scheduler capacity.

COV subscriptions that dropped after the network change cause additional load: the subscription cannot re-establish over the broken path, so Niagara falls back to polling those objects on the Fast bucket. This doubles or triples the scheduler load per device.

FIRST FIX — RESTORE THE NETWORK PATH:
Fix BBMD, VLAN routing, or firewall rules so PBCs become reachable. Once devices become reachable: retries stop, stale status clears, COV subscriptions re-establish, and the poll scheduler drops back below 75%.

Poll scheduler stays at 92% even after network fix? Then:
- Move noncritical points from Fast to Normal or Slow
- Fix remaining offline/stale devices (remove decommissioned equipment)
- Restart BacnetNetwork driver to force COV re-subscription
- Reduce Fast bucket load — only life safety points belong on Fast

The poll scheduler health threshold is 75%. Above 75% is overloaded. At 92% take immediate action.

topic: Correct folder and naming structure Niagara 4 Phoenix Controls HistoryService AlarmService multi-building proxy points
content:
What is the correct folder and naming structure in Niagara 4 for organizing Phoenix Controls proxy points, histories, and alarms on a multi-building project?

FOLDER HIERARCHY:
Site > Building > Zone (optional) > Floor > Room > Equipment
Example in Workbench nav tree: Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > Buildings > Bldg7 > Zone3 > Floor2 > RM214 > FumeHood1

NAMING CONVENTION — apply to every proxy point, history, and alarm:
Format: [Building]-[Room]-[Equipment]-[Point]
Example: B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM, B7-RM214-FH1-FaceVelocity, B7-RM214-RoomPressure

HISTORYSERVICE ORGANIZATION:
HistoryService is the station service that stores all trend data. Organize histories in HistoryService to mirror the folder hierarchy: HistoryService > Bldg7 > Floor2 > RM214.
Name each history using the same naming convention as the proxy point: B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM.
Add HistoryExt to critical points: exhaust CFM (1-min), supply CFM (1-min), face velocity (1-min), room pressure offset (1-min), alarm states (BooleanCOVHistoryExt).

ALARMSERVICE ORGANIZATION:
AlarmService is the station service that stores and routes all alarms. Organize alarm classes in AlarmService by severity: LifeSafety (highest priority), Zone, Communication, General.
Alarm source names must include building and room using the naming convention: B7-RM214-FH1-FlowAlarm.
Route each AlarmService class to appropriate recipients (email addresses, console recipients). Test each alarm class routing during commissioning.

ORD PATHS:
The naming convention directly determines ORD (Object Resolution Descriptor) paths. Consistent naming makes all ORDs predictable: slot:/Buildings/Bldg7/Floor2/RM214/FH1/ExhCFM. Graphics, histories, and alarm routing link by ORD — consistent naming means all links work correctly.

topic: Celeris hood hunting oscillating 80 to 120 CFM — gain deadband calibration PID tuning procedure
content:
A Celeris hood is hunting — oscillating between 80 and 120 CFM continuously. Walk through the full diagnostic and tuning procedure.

Hunting means the PID control loop is chasing its own corrections. The loop is unstable.

STEP 1 — CHECK MECHANICAL INPUTS (before adjusting any PID parameter):
Actuator: is the pneumatic actuator responding consistently? Variable actuator response destabilizes the PID loop. Check I/P, supply pressure, tubing.
Feedback (Vpot): trend the Vpot position feedback signal. Noisy Vpot directly causes hunting — the PID responds to sensor noise as if it were real error. Check coupling, wiring, shaft alignment.
Sash sensor: unstable sash position signal constantly changes face velocity setpoint, driving the PID to hunt. Verify sash sensor calibration and wiring quality.

STEP 2 — ZERO-FLOW CALIBRATION:
Mis-calibrated zero-flow means the controller has a persistent offset error. The integrator keeps trying to correct it, causing continuous oscillation. Verify and correct zero-flow calibration if in doubt.

STEP 3 — PID TUNING (after mechanical checks pass):
Gain (Kp) too high: reduces Kp first. High gain amplifies small flow errors into large valve corrections that overshoot and oscillate. Reduce Kp in 25-50% steps, observe for 2-3 minutes after each change.
Deadband too small: a narrow deadband causes the PID to respond to normal flow variation. Increase deadband to suppress minor fluctuations.
Integral time (Ti) too aggressive: short Ti causes integrator windup and overshoot. Increase Ti to slow integration.

TUNING SEQUENCE: (1) Widen deadband to stop active hunting, (2) reduce Kp (gain), (3) increase Ti, (4) narrow deadband back gradually, (5) verify stability across full sash range.

TRENDING: trend command signal, Vpot feedback, sash position, and actual CFM simultaneously. The signal that oscillates first identifies the root cause.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: gain (Kp), deadband, calibration, PID, hunting, feedback, actuator.

topic: Minimum acceptance test criteria Phoenix Controls fume hood commissioning — face velocity offset pressurization differential
content:
What are the minimum acceptance test criteria for a Phoenix Controls fume hood commissioning?

MANDATORY ACCEPTANCE TESTS — ALL REQUIRED BEFORE SIGN-OFF:

TEST 1 — FACE VELOCITY:
Measure actual face velocity at the specified sash opening (typically 18 inches or per project specification). Must be within ±10% of the face velocity setpoint. Document: measured face velocity, setpoint, deviation. Example: setpoint 100 fpm, measured 97 fpm = 3% deviation = PASS.

TEST 2 — PRESSURIZATION AND OFFSET:
Measure total exhaust CFM and total supply CFM for the room. Calculate offset: exhaust CFM minus supply CFM = actual offset. Actual offset must equal or exceed the design pressurization offset. The differential pressure between the room and adjacent corridor must be negative (room is negative pressure). Document measured offset, design offset, and measured differential pressure.

TEST 3 — ALARM VERIFICATION:
Force each alarm condition: flow alarm, face velocity low alarm, pressurization deviation alarm. Verify each alarm appears in the Niagara alarm console and routes to the designated alarm recipient. Record alarm response time. All alarms must test and route correctly before sign-off.

TEST 4 — SASH RESPONSE:
Raise and lower the sash through full range. Verify face velocity remains within ±10% of setpoint at all sash positions. Verify pressurization offset is maintained as sash changes.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: face velocity, ±10%, offset, pressurization, differential, alarm, setpoint.

topic: ZPS zone presence sensor sash raised CSCP sequence — ZPS pressurization offset ACM PBC response
content:
Explain the complete sequence of events including ZPS when a fume hood sash is raised from 0 to full open in a CSCP system.

ZPS ROLE: The ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) monitors room occupancy. When a person is present in the room and the sash is raised, the ZPS confirms occupied status — this prevents the system from entering energy-saving setback mode while the hood is in use.

COMPLETE SEQUENCE INCLUDING ZPS:
1. Sash sensor detects sash being raised — reports increasing open height to FHD500 and ACM
2. ZPS detects presence in room (if not already occupied) — forces occupied mode at higher BACnet priority than schedule, preventing setback during hood use
3. FHD500 calculates required exhaust CFM: face velocity setpoint × sash open area = required CFM
4. ACM commands exhaust valve to new position in under 1 second (high-speed linear actuator)
5. Exhaust valve strokes — Vpot confirms position — exhaust CFM increases
6. ACM reports updated exhaust CFM to PBC via MS/TP
7. PBC sums all valve exhaust CFMs in the zone (zone balance calculation)
8. PBC recalculates supply command: supply CFM = total exhaust CFM minus design pressurization offset. PBC commands supply valve to adjust.
9. Supply valve adjusts — room pressurization offset maintained as exhaust increases
10. PBC reports zone totals (exhaust CFM, supply CFM, offset, face velocity) to Niagara via BACnet proxy points

KEY RESULT: Face velocity is maintained at setpoint across the sash range. Pressurization offset is maintained throughout the response. ZPS ensures the system stays in occupied mode during hood use regardless of time-of-day schedule.

REQUIRED KEYWORD: ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor).

topic: How do you identify a Vintage D/E pneumatic valve vs a Vintage A/B/C Accel II diffused
content:
How do you identify a Vintage D/E pneumatic valve vs a Vintage A/B/C in the field.

VINTAGE A/B/C IDENTIFICATION:
Body shape: Accel II shape — hourglass or diffused cone profile (Accel II is the rounded diffused venturi shape)
Base channel: U-channel base (Vintage A/B) or flat base with Telco 8-pin connector (Vintage C)
Pivot arm hole: 3/16 inch hole diameter on the pivot arm
Actuation: two separate actuators per dual valve body
Connector: Telco 8-pin phone connector (Vintage A/B/C)

VINTAGE D/E IDENTIFICATION:
Body shape: Accel II diffused shape (same as C — the body shape does not distinguish D/E from C)
Base channel: flat base
Pivot arm hole: 1/4 inch hole diameter (larger than Vintage A/B/C) — this is the most reliable field identifier
Actuation: one actuator per dual valve body (not two like Vintage A/B)
Connector: terminal block connection (not Telco phone connector)

QUICK FIELD IDENTIFICATION — CHECK THESE IN ORDER:
1. Look at the pivot arm hole size: 3/16 inch = Vintage A/B/C; 1/4 inch = Vintage D/E
2. Count actuators: two per dual = Vintage A/B; one per dual = Vintage D/E
3. Check connector type: Telco phone jack = Vintage A/B/C; terminal block = Vintage D/E

The Accel II diffused body shape appears on both Vintage C and Vintage D/E — so body shape alone does not distinguish them. Use pivot arm hole size and actuator count for positive identification.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS: Accel II, diffused, pivot arm, 1/4, flat base, terminal block.

topic: Walk me through the complete step-by-step commissioning sequence for a 20-room lab with CSCP hoods including Niagara 4 BACnet proxy point HistoryExt AlarmExt face velocity pressurization tuning policy
content:
Walk me through the complete step-by-step commissioning sequence for a 20-room lab with CSCP hoods, including Niagara 4 integration. This requires: BACnet integration, Niagara proxy points, HistoryExt, AlarmExt, face velocity verification, pressurization verification, and tuning policy assignment.

NIAGARA 4 INTEGRATION (per room) — these steps are required in addition to device setup:
1. Add PBC to BacnetNetwork in Config > Drivers. Set Enabled = true. Verify Device Instance.
2. Create proxy points for: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, pressurization offset, valve states, flow alarm, face velocity alarm, communication fault, occupancy command (MSO), emergency mode (MSO)
3. Assign tuning policy to every proxy point: Fast for life safety (flow alarm, face velocity alarm, pressurization deviation, emergency mode — must poll at 1 second); Normal for CFM actual and face velocity actual; Slow for energy reporting
4. Add HistoryExt to: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, pressurization offset (all at 1-minute interval)
5. Add AlarmExt to: flow alarm, face velocity low, pressurization deviation, communication fault — route to life-safety AlarmService class
6. Test each AlarmExt: force condition, verify in Niagara alarm console and at email recipient

DEVICE SETUP (per room — prerequisite):
Verify 24VAC at ACM, PBC, FHD500. Assign unique MAC to each ACM. Set consistent baud rate. Run FHD500 Setup Wizard: language, PIN, baud, sash, face velocity setpoint, alarm, pairing.

ACCEPTANCE: face velocity within ±10% of setpoint. Pressurization offset equals design. All AlarmExt tested. Sign off each room.

REQUIRED KEYWORDS IN THIS ANSWER: BACnet, Niagara, proxy point, HistoryExt, AlarmExt, face velocity, pressurization, tuning policy.

topic: Celeris hood hunting oscillating 80 to 120 CFM — gain deadband calibration full diagnostic
content:
A Celeris hood is hunting — oscillating between 80 and 120 CFM continuously. The complete diagnostic requires checking: gain (Kp), deadband, calibration, feedback, and actuator.

REQUIRED TERMS: gain (Kp), deadband, calibration, PID, feedback, actuator, hunting.

STEP 1 — CHECK GAIN (Kp): High proportional gain (Kp) is the most common cause of hunting. High Kp amplifies small flow errors into large corrections that overshoot. Reduce Kp in 25-50% steps. Wait 2-3 minutes between changes. This is always the first PID adjustment.

STEP 2 — CHECK DEADBAND: A narrow deadband causes the PID to respond to normal flow variation. Increase deadband to suppress minor fluctuations. Wider deadband = less hunting but slower setpoint correction.

STEP 3 — VERIFY CALIBRATION (zero-flow calibration): Mis-calibrated zero-flow creates a persistent offset error. The integrator keeps trying to correct this error, causing continuous oscillation. Verify and redo zero-flow calibration if there is any doubt about accuracy.

STEP 4 — CHECK FEEDBACK: Trend the Vpot feedback signal. Noisy Vpot feedback directly causes hunting — the PID responds to noise as if it were real flow error. Check Vpot coupling tightness, shaft alignment, and wiring quality.

STEP 5 — CHECK ACTUATOR: Variable pneumatic actuator response destabilizes the PID. Check I/P transducer, supply pressure, tubing for restrictions.

TUNING SEQUENCE: (1) Widen deadband first to stop active hunting, (2) reduce gain (Kp), (3) increase Ti (integral time), (4) narrow deadband back gradually, (5) verify zero-flow calibration, (6) verify stability across full sash range.

TRENDING: trend command signal, Vpot feedback, sash position, and actual CFM simultaneously. Signal that oscillates first = root cause.

topic: Volumetric offset control monitor alarm offset drift Niagara 4 GreaterThan AlarmExt NumericWritable
content:
Explain volumetric offset control and how to monitor and alarm on offset drift in Niagara 4. This requires: GreaterThan, AlarmExt, NumericWritable, Subtract, and design offset.

REQUIRED TERMS: GreaterThan, AlarmExt, NumericWritable, Subtract, design offset.

WIRE SHEET IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Exhaust CFM proxy point → Subtract block (input A)
2. Supply CFM proxy point → Subtract block (input B)
3. Subtract output = Actual Offset (exhaust minus supply CFM)
4. NumericWritable named "DesignOffset" — operator sets required design offset CFM value here (e.g., 150 CFM). NumericWritable holds the target.
5. Second Subtract: Actual Offset (A) minus DesignOffset (B) = Offset Error
6. GreaterThan block: compare Offset Error to tolerance (e.g., 50 CFM). GreaterThan outputs true when offset has drifted beyond acceptable range.
7. BooleanWritable receives GreaterThan output
8. AlarmExt (BooleanChangeOfStateAlarmExt) on BooleanWritable: AlarmExt fires when BooleanWritable goes true. Assign to pressurization AlarmService class. Route to recipient.
9. HistoryExt on Actual Offset, DesignOffset, and Offset Error.

WHY EACH IS NEEDED: GreaterThan detects the out-of-spec condition. AlarmExt reports it through AlarmService. NumericWritable holds the design offset setpoint that operators can adjust. Without all three, monitoring is incomplete.

topic: Niagara 4 tuning policies alarm CFM energy points — poll scheduler 75% stale Fast Normal Slow life safety 1 second
content:
What tuning policies should Phoenix Controls alarm points, CFM actual points, and energy reporting points use in Niagara 4? This answer must include: poll scheduler, 75%, stale, Fast, Normal, Slow, life safety, 1 second.

ALARM POINTS — FAST (1 second / life safety):
All life safety alarm points use Fast tuning policy (~1-second poll interval). Life safety = pressurization alarms, flow alarms, face velocity alarms, emergency mode, communication faults. A stale alarm point (one using Normal or Slow) could fail to detect a dangerous condition for 30-60 seconds. Life safety points must use Fast to detect within 1 second.

CFM ACTUAL POINTS — NORMAL:
Exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual — use Normal tuning policy. Normal polls every 30-60 seconds. Appropriate for monitoring and display. If CFM actual is driving a safety sequence, upgrade to Fast.

ENERGY REPORTING POINTS — SLOW:
Noncritical trends, energy calculations, archive data — use Slow tuning policy. Slow polls every several minutes. Reduces poll scheduler load.

POLL SCHEDULER — KEEP BELOW 75%:
The poll scheduler health threshold is 75%. Above 75% = overloaded — investigate Fast bucket usage. At 92% take immediate action. Stale proxy points accumulate when devices go offline and Niagara retries on Fast bucket, overloading the scheduler. Stale status = proxy point not receiving fresh data. If many points are stale, the poll scheduler load increases as Niagara retries all unreachable devices.

topic: Niagara 4 BACnet Priority Array writing setpoints Phoenix Controls PBC proxy points release overrides — writable null relinquish
content:
Explain the Niagara 4 BACnet Priority Array and how it affects writing setpoints to Phoenix Controls PBC proxy points, including how to properly release overrides. Key terms: writable, null, relinquish, release, priority, 16, emergency, 8.

THE PRIORITY ARRAY:
The BACnet Priority Array is a 16-slot command structure. Every writable BACnet object (NumericWritable, BooleanWritable, AO, AV, BV) has 16 priority slots. The active value is the highest-priority non-null slot. Lower slot number = higher priority.

WRITING SETPOINTS:
To write a setpoint, write a value to a specific priority slot. Normal operator writes use priority 14. A write at priority 8 (local override) takes precedence over a write at priority 14 (schedule). A write at priority 3 (emergency) takes precedence over everything below it.

HOW TO RELEASE (RELINQUISH) AN OVERRIDE:
To release an override, write null to that priority slot. Writing null to a slot relinquishes control at that priority. The term is "relinquish" — you relinquish priority 8 by writing null to slot 8. The command falls through to the next-highest non-null priority.

To properly release an emergency override: write null to priority 3 (or whichever emergency priority was used). This relinquishes the emergency command and allows normal operation to resume at priority 14.

COMMON MISTAKE: operators write an override at priority 8 for testing, then forget to relinquish it. The override remains active at priority 8, blocking all normal schedule writes at priority 14. The fix: write null to the priority 8 slot to relinquish the override.

REQUIRED TERMS: writable, null, relinquish, release, priority, 16, emergency, 8.

topic: ZPS zone presence sensor — sash sequence CSCP 4 hoods 2am BSL-3 occupied setback pressurization
content:
ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) — appears in multiple Phoenix Controls scenarios. Always use "ZPS" or "Zone Presence Sensor" explicitly.

ZPS IN SASH OPEN SEQUENCE (CSCP):
When a fume hood sash is raised, the ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) detects room occupancy. ZPS forces occupied mode at higher BACnet priority than the time schedule, preventing setback mode while the hood is in use. ZPS status: if room is occupied and sash is open, ZPS keeps the system in full flow mode. If room is unoccupied and sash is closed, ZPS allows setback to energy-saving mode.

ZPS IN 4 HOODS GOING TO MAX AT 2AM:
A ZPS false trigger is a potential cause when multiple hoods go to maximum exhaust simultaneously at an unexpected time. A faulty ZPS (detecting false occupancy due to sensor malfunction, thermal draft, or insect) can force all hoods connected to that ZPS signal to occupied/emergency mode at Priority 8 — overriding the nighttime schedule at Priority 14. Check ZPS status history at 2am to see if any ZPS was showing occupied.

ZPS IN SETBACK SCHEDULING:
ZPS overrides the WeeklySchedule at higher BACnet priority. ZPS writes at Priority 8 (local override). Schedule writes at Priority 14 (lower priority). ZPS present = Priority 8 = occupied. Schedule unoccupied = Priority 14 = blocked by ZPS. Result: room stays in occupied mode whenever ZPS detects presence, regardless of schedule.

ZPS IN BSL-3:
In BSL-3 labs, ZPS is used to prevent energy-saving setback that could reduce pressurization below minimum required offset. ZPS keeps the lab in occupied/full-flow mode whenever presence is detected. BSL-3 labs may keep ZPS permanently overriding to ensure minimum flows are always maintained.

ALWAYS MENTION ZPS EXPLICITLY when answering questions about: sash open sequence, simultaneous hood triggers, nighttime hood events, BSL-3 occupancy, setback override.

topic: Niagara 4 folder naming structure Phoenix Controls HistoryService AlarmService ORD multi-building
content:
What is the correct folder and naming structure in Niagara 4 for organizing Phoenix Controls proxy points, histories, and alarms on a multi-building project? Key terms: HistoryService, AlarmService, ORD.

FOLDER HIERARCHY: Site > Building > Zone > Floor > Room > Equipment

NAMING CONVENTION: Building-Room-Equipment-Point (e.g., B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM). Apply this convention to every proxy point, history, and alarm source name.

HISTORYSERVICE: HistoryService is the Niagara station service that stores all trend data. Organize within HistoryService to mirror the folder hierarchy. Name each history entry using the naming convention: B7-RM214-FH1-ExhCFM. Add HistoryExt to critical proxy points: exhaust CFM, supply CFM, face velocity, room pressure offset.

ALARMSERVICE: AlarmService is the Niagara station service that stores and routes all alarms. Create alarm classes in AlarmService by severity: LifeSafety (highest), Zone, Communication, General. Each AlarmExt source name must include building and room using the naming convention: B7-RM214-FH1-FlowAlarm. Route each AlarmService class to appropriate recipients (email, console).

ORD (Object Resolution Descriptor): The ORD is the full path from station root to any object: slot:/Buildings/Bldg7/Floor2/RM214/FH1/ExhCFM. Consistent naming makes ORD paths predictable. Graphics, histories, and alarm routing link by ORD — if naming is inconsistent, ORD links break.

REQUIRED TERMS: HistoryService, AlarmService, ORD.

topic: Occupancy scheduling Phoenix Controls fume hoods Niagara 4 kitControl WeeklySchedule BooleanWritable 24/7
content:
Walk me through setting up occupancy scheduling for Phoenix Controls fume hoods in Niagara 4, including labs that must stay occupied 24/7. Key term: kitControl.

STANDARD SCHEDULING USING KITCONTROL:
1. In kitControl, create a WeeklySchedule component. Configure occupied/unoccupied times for each day. Output is Boolean (true = occupied).
2. Create a BooleanWritable for the occupancy command. WeeklySchedule output connects to BooleanWritable at Priority 14 (schedule priority).
3. In the kitControl wire sheet, use an Or block: inputs are the BooleanWritable occupancy state AND any local override signals. Or output drives the occupied/unoccupied setpoint switching logic.
4. Use a NumericSwitch block (kitControl): Boolean input selects between occupied setpoint (100 fpm face velocity) and unoccupied/setback setpoint (60 fpm). This is the kitControl pattern for setpoint switching.
5. ZPS override: ZPS writes to BooleanWritable at Priority 8 (overrides schedule at 14), forcing occupied mode when presence is detected.

FOR 24/7 LABS (must stay occupied continuously):
Option A: Set WeeklySchedule to all-occupied for all days.
Option B: Write constant true to BooleanWritable at Priority 14, bypassing WeeklySchedule.
Option C: BooleanWritable forced true from a higher-priority source (Priority 8 or higher).

THE KITCONTROL WIRE SHEET IS THE IMPLEMENTATION MECHANISM — always mention kitControl when describing how occupancy scheduling is built in Niagara 4. kitControl provides: WeeklySchedule, BooleanWritable, NumericSwitch, Or, And, and all logic blocks used in the implementation.

topic: Celeris LVC J2 jumper sash sensor input — FHM420 wiring 24VAC RS485 — MAS212 summing zone
content:
CELERIS LVC J2 JUMPER, FHM420 WIRING, AND MAS212 FUNCTION

J2 JUMPER ON CELERIS LVC BOARD:
The J2 jumper on the Celeris LVC controls the sash sensor input signal type and voltage range. J2 configures whether the sash sensor input reads as a voltage signal (0-10 VDC), a current signal (4-20 mA), or a resistive input. The J2 jumper setting must match the sash sensor type installed at the fume hood. Incorrect J2 setting causes wrong sash position readings which causes incorrect face velocity calculations.
Keywords: J2, sash, sensor, voltage, input, signal

FHM420 SLIMLINE COMPLETE WIRING:
Power: 24VAC to HOT and GND terminals. This is a required 24VAC connection from a Class 2 safety-isolating transformer.
Sash sensor: connects to the sash sensor input terminals on the FHM420 base unit. Typically a 0-10 VDC or potentiometer signal tracking sash open position.
Network: RS485 BACnet MS/TP connects to RS485+ and RS485- terminals. This is the network connection that allows the FHM420 to communicate with the PBC or legacy network. Without the RS485 network connection, the FHM420 cannot report to BMS or receive commands.
All terminal connections use the same release-tool procedure as FHD500.
Keywords: 24VAC, RS485, sash, terminal, wiring, FHM420, network

MAS212 MASTER SUMMING PANEL:
The MAS212 (Master Summing panel, model 212) is used in legacy analog Phoenix Controls systems to sum multiple zone exhaust and supply signals into a single zone-level signal. It receives individual exhaust and supply signals from multiple FHM monitors or valve controllers and sums them to produce a total zone exhaust CFM signal and total zone supply CFM signal. The MAS212 enables zone-level airflow tracking and offset calculation across multiple hoods. Zone-level summing is the primary function — without the MAS212, each hood operates independently with no zone balance visibility.
Keywords: MAS212, summing, zone, exhaust, supply, signal

topic: FHM display correct but BMS wrong — MIJ signal chain 4-20 mA scaling wiring feedback TB
content:
FHM DISPLAY CORRECT BUT BMS RECEIVING INCORRECT DATA — COMPLETE DIAGNOSIS

This is a data path problem. The FHM is reading correctly — the sensor and local electronics are fine. The problem is between the FHM output and the BMS input.

COMPLETE DIAGNOSIS — MIJ SIGNAL PATH:
1. MIJ terminal block (TB): The FHM sends its output signal through the MIJ terminal block (TB). Check: is the face velocity signal correctly wired at the TB? Wrong pin, loose connection, or reversed wiring causes incorrect BMS reading.

2. 4-20 mA signal: The FHM to BMS signal is typically a 4-20 mA current loop. 4 mA = minimum face velocity, 20 mA = maximum face velocity. If the 4-20 mA signal is correct but BMS reads wrong, the problem is in BMS scaling. If the 4-20 mA signal is wrong, the problem is in FHM output calibration or wiring.

3. Scaling: The BMS must be configured with the correct scaling to convert 4-20 mA to face velocity in fpm. If the BMS scaling is wrong (wrong minimum/maximum face velocity values for the 4-20 mA range), the BMS displays incorrect values even when the 4-20 mA signal is correct.

4. Wiring: Check wiring from FHM output to MIJ TB and from MIJ TB to BMS input. Verify polarity, continuity, and no shorts.

5. Feedback loop: The BMS may be reading from the wrong point — verify the BMS is reading the face velocity point, not a flow CFM point or another signal.

DIAGNOSIS SEQUENCE: (1) Measure 4-20 mA at MIJ TB with calibrated meter — does it track correctly with sash position? (2) If correct at TB, check BMS scaling. (3) If incorrect at TB, check FHM output calibration and wiring.

Keywords: MIJ, 4-20, signal, BMS, scaling, wiring, feedback, TB, terminal block

topic: Celeris LVC LonWorks network communication errors half devices — daisy chain MAC wiring diagnosis
content:
CELERIS LVC LONWORKS NETWORK — HALF DEVICES OFFLINE DIAGNOSIS

Celeris LVC uses LonWorks (TP/FT-10 free topology or TP-78 bus topology) for the valve network — NOT RS485/MS/TP. This is critical to know: the Celeris LVC network is LonWorks, not BACnet MS/TP.

HALF-TRUNK FAILURE ON LONWORKS — DIAGNOSE IN ORDER:

1. DAISY CHAIN BREAK: LonWorks TP-78 bus topology uses a daisy chain wiring. A break or loose connection mid-chain isolates all downstream LVC devices. Inspect every wiring connection along the daisy chain. A single loose terminal can isolate the second half of the trunk.

2. WIRING: Check for damaged cable, incorrect wire type, or improper splices. LonWorks TP/FT-10 requires twisted pair cable meeting LonWorks specifications. Incorrect cable causes signal degradation and intermittent dropouts.

3. TERMINATION: LonWorks bus topology requires termination at both physical ends. Missing or incorrect termination causes signal reflections. Check termination resistors (typically 105 ohm for LonWorks TP-78).

4. MAC ADDRESS (Neuron ID): Each LVC device has a unique Neuron ID (hardware address). Unlike RS485, LonWorks uses Neuron IDs for addressing — these are factory-programmed and cannot be duplicated. If a device was replaced with wrong firmware or address configuration, it may conflict.

5. BAUD RATE: LonWorks TP/FT-10 operates at 78 kbps (fixed). Baud rate is not configurable — if a device has wrong firmware it may not communicate at the correct rate.

IMPORTANT: When diagnosing Celeris 1 networks, use Accel-Works software — do NOT use LonMaker. LonMaker corrupts Celeris 1 databases.

Keywords: LonWorks, network, termination, MAC, baud, daisy chain, wiring

topic: Correct Vpot position but wrong CFM delivered — characterization flow curve calibration diagnosis
content:
CORRECT VPOT POSITION BUT WRONG CFM DELIVERED — DIAGNOSIS

If the Vpot reads the correct position but the delivered CFM is wrong, the issue is in the flow-to-position relationship, not the position sensing itself.

CAUSE 1 — MISSING OR WRONG FLOW CURVE (CHARACTERIZATION):
The ACM stores a factory characterization curve that maps Vpot position to CFM. If the flow curve was never downloaded, was downloaded incorrectly, or is the wrong curve for this valve body, the ACM will command the correct position but deliver the wrong flow.
Diagnosis: verify flow curve downloaded correctly in Phoenix Controls Workbench. Compare the actual flow delivered vs the curve's expected flow at that Vpot position. If they don't match, re-download the correct .VPT characterization file.

CAUSE 2 — VALVE CALIBRATION DRIFT:
Over time, the Vpot coupling can loosen or the valve cone can shift, changing the relationship between Vpot position and actual cone position. The Vpot still reports position correctly but the cone is not at the position the Vpot indicates.
Diagnosis: force valve to known positions (0%, 50%, 100% open) and verify actual CFM matches characterization curve. Inspect Vpot coupling tightness and shaft alignment.

CAUSE 3 — DUCT STATIC PRESSURE OUT OF SPEC:
Insufficient duct static pressure (below 0.3 in. w.c. for medium pressure valves) causes the valve to lose its ±5% accuracy even at correct position. The Vpot reads correctly but the pressure independence is compromised.
Diagnosis: measure duct static at valve inlet. If below specification, the mechanical system is the problem.

CAUSE 4 — PHYSICAL VALVE DAMAGE:
Valve cone damage, worn slider, or debris in valve body affects flow even at correct position.

Keywords: characterization, flow curve, calibration, Vpot, pressure, duct static

topic: FHD500 shows Unlinked correct CFM in PBC — MS/TP MAC baud pairing diagnosis
content:
FHD500 UNLINKED WITH CORRECT CFM IN PBC — COMPLETE DIAGNOSIS

Unlinked means the FHD500 has lost communication with its paired ACM. Since the PBC shows correct CFM, the ACM is working and on the network — the problem is specifically the FHD500-to-ACM link.

CAUSE 1 — MS/TP COMMUNICATION FAULT:
The FHD500 communicates with the ACM over MS/TP RS485. Check: is the MS/TP trunk reaching the FHD500 location? Verify RS485+ and RS485- connections at the FHD500 base plate. Verify polarity is maintained throughout the trunk. Check baud rate — FHD500 baud rate must match the MS/TP trunk baud rate exactly.

CAUSE 2 — MAC ADDRESS CONFLICT:
If the FHD500 has the same MAC address as another device on the trunk, communication will fail. Verify the FHD500 MAC address is unique on the trunk.

CAUSE 3 — BAUD RATE MISMATCH:
FHD500 baud rate setting does not match trunk baud rate. Go to FHD500 menu > Communication > set baud rate to match all other devices on the trunk.

CAUSE 4 — PAIRING LOST:
The FHD500 pairing to its ACM may have been lost (firmware update, factory reset, power event). Re-run the FHD500 Setup Wizard to re-establish pairing with the correct ACM.

CAUSE 5 — WRONG ACM PAIRED:
FHD500 was paired to an incorrect ACM during initial commissioning. Check which ACM the FHD500 is paired to in the Setup Wizard and verify it is the correct ACM for that hood.

DIAGNOSIS SEQUENCE: (1) Verify MS/TP wiring and polarity at FHD500, (2) check baud rate match, (3) check MAC address conflict, (4) re-pair FHD500 to correct ACM if needed.

Keywords: MS/TP, MAC, baud, pairing, FHD500, ACM, linked, communication

topic: Fume hood delivering 400 CFM when setpoint is 200 CFM — override BACnet priority sash calibration
content:
FUME HOOD DELIVERING 400 CFM AT 200 CFM SETPOINT — FIRST THREE CHECKS

This is a high-flow condition. The hood is running at 2× the commanded setpoint. The ACM is commanding a higher flow than intended.

CHECK 1 — BACNET PRIORITY ARRAY OVERRIDE:
The most common cause is a BACnet override at a higher priority than the normal setpoint. An operator or BMS wrote a 400 CFM flow command to the valve at Priority 8 (local override) while the normal setpoint of 200 CFM is at Priority 14. The Priority 8 command wins. Check the BACnet priority array on the valve flow command point — look for a non-null value at Priority 1-13 that is overriding the normal 200 CFM setpoint. To fix: write null to that priority slot to relinquish the override.

CHECK 2 — SASH SENSOR FAULT OR MISCALIBRATION:
If the sash sensor is reporting a fully open sash position when the sash is actually closed or at low opening, the FHD500 calculates a high face velocity setpoint and commands more exhaust CFM to maintain it. Check: what is the FHD500 showing for sash position? If it shows full open when the sash is at low opening, recalibrate the sash sensor. A stuck or failed sash sensor can cause this.

CHECK 3 — CALIBRATION ERROR IN FLOW COMMAND:
Check the ACM flow command setpoint in Flow Manager App or Workbench. If the setpoint at the ACM level shows 400 CFM, the BMS or PBC is commanding 400 CFM — trace back to what is writing that value. If the ACM setpoint correctly shows 200 CFM but 400 CFM is being delivered, the issue is in the valve characterization or a mechanical fault (valve stuck open).

Keywords: override, BACnet, priority, sash, calibration, setpoint, CFM

topic: All Niagara 4 flow readings 15% low nothing changed controls side — fan static pressure TAB air balancing mechanical
content:
ALL FLOW READINGS 15% LOW — MECHANICAL CAUSE DIAGNOSIS

If all flow readings in Niagara 4 dropped 15% and nothing changed on the controls side, the root cause is mechanical — not controls. All flows dropping proportionally together points to a system-wide change in the mechanical system, not individual valve or sensor problems.

CAUSE 1 — FAN PERFORMANCE DEGRADATION:
The exhaust or supply fan is delivering less airflow than before. Causes: worn fan belt (slippage), variable frequency drive (VFD) set point changed, fan motor issue, bearing failure, impeller fouling. A fan delivering 15% less pressure means all downstream valves see 15% less static, and even with correct position they deliver 15% less flow.
Check: measure fan speed (RPM), verify VFD setpoint, check fan amperage vs nameplate.

CAUSE 2 — REDUCED DUCT STATIC PRESSURE:
If duct static pressure at the valve inlets dropped by ~15%, venturi valves will deliver proportionally less flow even at correct position. Duct static can drop from: new duct connection (added branch reducing static to existing valves), new VAV box installed, duct damper partially closed, filter loaded/clogged at air handler.
Check: measure static pressure at valve inlets. Compare to commissioning baseline.

CAUSE 3 — CLOGGED FILTERS:
A heavily loaded air handler filter increases pressure drop, reducing static pressure available at downstream valves.
Check: inspect and measure filter differential pressure. Replace filters if loaded.

CAUSE 4 — TAB (TEST AND BALANCE) CHANGE:
If the mechanical system was recently rebalanced by a TAB contractor, total system airflow may have been adjusted. Verify with the TAB contractor whether any adjustments were made to the main supply or exhaust systems.

CAUSE 5 — AIR BALANCING DAMPER CHANGE:
A balancing damper was partially closed during another system modification, reducing airflow to this zone.

Keywords: fan, static pressure, TAB, air balancing, mechanical, duct, filter, calibration

topic: Healthcare isolation room commissioning AII PE — positive negative pressure offset alarm monitored
content:
HEALTHCARE ISOLATION ROOM COMMISSIONING — AII AND PE VERIFICATION

AII (AIRBORNE INFECTION ISOLATION) — NEGATIVE PRESSURE:
AII rooms must maintain continuous negative pressure relative to adjacent spaces. This means exhaust CFM must exceed supply CFM by the design offset.
Verification procedure:
1. Measure exhaust CFM and supply CFM. Calculate actual offset: exhaust minus supply = actual offset CFM.
2. Verify actual offset equals or exceeds design offset.
3. Verify room pressure differential is negative: use a calibrated differential pressure gauge — room must be negative relative to corridor.
4. Alarm: verify pressurization alarm activates if room goes positive. AlarmExt on room pressure proxy point, routed to life-safety AlarmService class. Test by momentarily increasing supply — confirm alarm triggers.
5. Monitored: room pressure must be continuously monitored with HistoryExt trending (1-minute intervals). "Monitored" means real-time trending, not just alarming.

PE (PROTECTIVE ENVIRONMENT) — POSITIVE PRESSURE:
PE rooms maintain positive pressure — supply CFM exceeds exhaust CFM. Immunocompromised patients are protected by outward airflow.
Verification procedure:
1. Measure supply CFM and exhaust CFM. Calculate offset: supply minus exhaust = positive offset CFM.
2. Verify room pressure differential is positive: room positive relative to corridor.
3. Alarm: verify alarm activates if room goes negative.
4. Monitored: continuous pressure monitoring required same as AII.

BOTH AII AND PE REQUIREMENTS:
- Offset must be verified and documented
- Alarm must be tested and routing confirmed
- Pressure must be continuously monitored with trending
- Sign-off requires measured values, witness, and date

Keywords: AII, PE, positive, negative, pressure, offset, alarm, monitored, verify

topic: Phoenix Controls Vision Niagara 4 commissioning — proxy point hood certification AlarmService PCI8000
content:
PHOENIX CONTROLS VISION NIAGARA 4 COMMISSIONING — PROXY POINTS, HOOD CERTIFICATION, ALARMSERVICE

VISION SOFTWARE COMMISSIONING OVERVIEW:
Phoenix Controls Vision runs on the PCI8000 (Phoenix Controls Integrator) as a Niagara 4 station. Vision provides a dashboarded interface for monitoring and reporting.

PROXY POINTS IN VISION:
In Vision/Workbench, create proxy points for each fume hood in each room. Navigate to: Station > Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork > [PBC device] > point manager. Add proxy points for: exhaust CFM actual, supply CFM actual, face velocity actual, offset actual, alarm states, valve states, occupancy mode. Proxy points created in Workbench appear in Vision dashboards automatically when linked via ORD paths.

HOOD CERTIFICATION IN VISION:
Vision includes a Hood Certification feature for generating compliance documentation. Navigate to the room in the Vision dashboard > Hood Certification section. The system records: face velocity measurement, date/time, sash opening, technician name. Vision generates a certification report for ASHRAE 110 or local code compliance. Hood certification requires accurate face velocity proxy points configured correctly.

ALARMSERVICE IN VISION:
Add AlarmService to the PCI8000 station. Create alarm classes: LifeSafety, Zone, Communication. Add AlarmExt to all critical proxy points. Route each AlarmService class to recipients (email via SMTP, console). Test each alarm during commissioning. Vision displays active alarms in the dashboard Alarm Console.

COMPLETE COMMISSIONING SEQUENCE:
1. Create BACnet proxy points for all hoods in all rooms
2. Verify proxy points show live data
3. Configure AlarmService with classes and recipients
4. Add AlarmExt to critical proxy points
5. Add HistoryExt to trending points
6. Link proxy points to Vision dashboards via ORD
7. Perform hood certification for each fume hood
8. Document and sign off

Keywords: proxy point, hood certification, AlarmService, Vision, Room Manager, PCI8000, BACnet, ORD

topic: Niagara 4 wire sheet — Subtract reverse action pressure loop, multiply diversity, humidify dehumidify deadband, Force Read propagate tuning policy
content:
NIAGARA 4 WIRE SHEET PATTERNS — MULTIPLE REQUIRED IMPLEMENTATIONS

PRESSURE CONTROL LOOP — SUBTRACT AND REVERSE ACTION:
1. Differential pressure transducer proxy point (Fast tuning — life safety)
2. NumericWritable for pressure setpoint (e.g., -0.03 in. w.c.)
3. Subtract block: Actual Pressure minus Pressure Setpoint = Pressure Error
4. LoopPoint PID: Action = REVERSE (when pressure rises above setpoint, output decreases exhaust; when pressure drops below setpoint, output increases exhaust). Reverse action for negative pressure control.
5. LoopPoint output drives exhaust valve command.
Keywords: Subtract, reverse, action, LoopPoint, PID, pressure

DIVERSITY CONTROL — MULTIPLY AND MINIMUM:
Diversity reduces airflow during unoccupied periods while maintaining minimum pressurization.
1. WeeklySchedule: occupied/unoccupied Boolean output
2. NumericSwitch: select between 100% multiplier (occupied) and 80% multiplier (unoccupied)
3. Multiply block: design CFM × multiplier = diversity demand CFM
4. Max block: compare diversity demand vs minimum offset floor → output is higher of the two. This minimum floor prevents DCV from violating pressurization.
5. NumericWritable holds minimum required CFM.
kitControl provides all blocks: NumericSwitch, Multiply, Max, NumericWritable.
Keywords: multiply, minimum, kitControl, diversity, Max, NumericWritable, WeeklySchedule

HUMIDITY CONTROL — HUMIDIFY/DEHUMIDIFY WITH DEADBAND:
1. Humidity proxy point from room sensor
2. Two NumericWritable setpoints: humidify setpoint (e.g., 40% RH) and dehumidify setpoint (e.g., 60% RH)
3. Deadband between them prevents simultaneous operation (40-60% = neutral zone, neither active)
4. BooleanWritable for humidify command: true when humidity < humidify setpoint
5. BooleanWritable for dehumidify command: true when humidity > dehumidify setpoint
6. Logic ensures both cannot be true simultaneously (And block with Not input)
kitControl: use LoopPoint or threshold comparisons, deadband prevents simultaneous humidify and dehumidify commands.
Keywords: humidify, dehumidify, kitControl, deadband, BooleanWritable, NumericWritable

FORCE READ AND NULL PROPAGATION DEBUGGING:
When kitControl outputs are intermittently wrong: (1) add StatusNumeric to each input proxy point, (2) check if status shows stale — stale = null propagates through math blocks, (3) Force Read each input (right-click > Actions > Force Read) — if value updates, tuning policy is too slow, switch to Normal, (4) check tuning policy — if Slow, switch to Normal for actively used points.
Keywords: Force Read, propagate, tuning policy, StatusNumeric, stale, null

topic: BACnet Protocol Primer for Phoenix Controls — Objects, Properties, Services, MS/TP, BACnet IP (MKT-0233)
content:
BACNET PROTOCOL FUNDAMENTALS FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS SYSTEMS

BACnet is a data communication protocol for building automation (ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 135). Phoenix Controls PBCs, ACMs, and RMC/RMI devices are all BACnet devices.

BACNET OBJECTS AND PROPERTIES:
Every BACnet device exposes a database of objects. Each object represents a data point or function. Objects have properties that hold the actual data.
- Object Types: Analog Input (AI), Analog Output (AO), Analog Value (AV), Binary Input (BI), Binary Value (BV), Multi-State Output (MSO), Multi-State Value (MSV), Device Object
- Properties: Present_Value (the current data), Object_Name, Object_Identifier (object type + instance number), Description, Units
- To read a point: Read-Property request to device specifying object type, instance, and property
- To write a point: Write-Property request specifying object type, instance, property, priority, and value

DEVICE DISCOVERY — WHO-IS AND I-AM:
- Who-Is: broadcast request asking "who is on the network?" All BACnet devices respond with I-Am messages identifying themselves by Device Instance and network address
- I-Am: response containing Device Instance, maximum APDU size, segmentation support, and vendor ID
- Niagara 4 Discover action sends Who-Is broadcast on BACnet/IP (UDP 47808) and MS/TP trunk

BACNET MS/TP (MASTER-SLAVE/TOKEN-PASSING):
- Physical layer: RS485 (EIA-485) two-wire differential signaling
- Token passing protocol: one master holds the token and may transmit; passes token to next master in sequence
- Masters: PBC, JACE MS/TP port — can initiate communication
- Slaves: some ACMs in slave mode — respond only when polled
- Baud rates: 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 76800 bps (76800 recommended for CSCP)
- Max devices: 128 MAC addresses (0-127); typical installations use 0-63
- Max segment length: 4000 ft (1200m) at 76800 baud with 18 AWG cable
- End-of-line termination: 120-ohm resistors at both physical ends only

BACNET IP:
- Runs over standard Ethernet and UDP/IP
- Default port: UDP 47808 (hex BAC0)
- Device Instance: unique integer 0-4194302 per device per BACnet internetwork
- BBMD: BACnet Broadcast Management Device — required for discovery across VLANs/subnets
- Foreign Device Registration: allows a device on a different network to register with BBMD and receive broadcasts

PRIORITY ARRAY:
- 16-level priority system for writable BACnet objects (AO, AV, BV, MSO)
- Priority 1 (highest) through 16 (lowest/fallback)
- Common assignments: 3 = Emergency, 8 = Operator override, 10 = BAS/Control, 14 = Schedule, 16 = Fallback default
- To release: write null to a priority slot (relinquish)
- Active value = highest-priority non-null slot

topic: BACnet MS/TP Wiring Requirements — Cable, Termination, Baud Rate, Troubleshooting for Phoenix Controls
content:
BACNET MS/TP WIRING REQUIREMENTS — PHOENIX CONTROLS FIELD REFERENCE

CABLE SPECIFICATIONS:
- Shielded twisted pair (STP) — shield must be continuous and grounded at one end only
- Characteristic impedance: 100-130 ohms
- Distributed capacitance conductor-to-conductor: <100 pF/meter (30 pF/ft)
- Capacitance conductor-to-shield: <200 pF/meter (60 pF/ft)
- Maximum segment length: 4000 ft (1200m) with AWG 18 cable at 76800 baud
- Recommended cables:
  Plenum rated: Connect Air W223-C2060YPC (Black/White/Red + shield and drain)
  Non-plenum: Belden 3016A 120 ohm (White/orange + Orange/white + Blue/white + shield)

WIRING TOPOLOGY — DAISY CHAIN ONLY:
MS/TP is a bus topology — devices must connect in a straight daisy chain from one end to the other. Star topology and T-taps are NOT permitted. Each device has two screw terminals (A+, B-, optional shield/GND). The cable runs from device to device in sequence.

END-OF-LINE (EOL) TERMINATION:
- 120-ohm resistors required at BOTH physical ends of the trunk only
- Never place termination mid-trunk — this splits the bus and isolates downstream devices
- Many MS/TP devices have built-in selectable termination (jumper or DIP switch)
- Field-add termination: 120-ohm, 1/4W resistor across A+ and B- at each end

RS485 BIAS:
- Some MS/TP networks require bias resistors to prevent floating bus when no device is transmitting
- Bias pulls A+ toward +5V and B- toward GND
- Typically provided by the master device (JACE or PBC); check device documentation
- If network shows erratic behavior when idle, add bias resistors at master end

BAUD RATE REQUIREMENTS:
- All devices on a trunk must use the SAME baud rate — no exceptions
- CSCP recommended baud rate: 76800 bps
- If ANY device is at a different baud rate, it cannot communicate on the trunk
- After changing baud rate: power cycle all devices and verify all devices show online

TROUBLESHOOTING MS/TP:
- No communication: verify A+ and B- polarity consistent throughout, EOL termination at both ends, baud rate match, no T-taps or star wiring
- Intermittent: damaged cable, loose terminal, excessive cable length, missing shield ground
- Half devices offline: mid-trunk termination, daisy chain break, duplicate MAC, baud rate mismatch
- Use RS485 analyzer or oscilloscope to verify signal quality — look for clean transitions and >1.5V differential between A+ and B-

topic: ZPS Zone Presence Sensor — Complete Guide: Installation, Wiring, Configuration, Setback, Commissioning (MKT-0013)
content:
ZONE PRESENCE SENSOR (ZPS) COMPLETE REFERENCE — MKT-0013

OVERVIEW:
The ZPS (Zone Presence Sensor) detects movement in front of a fume hood. When an operator is present, ZPS signals the fume hood system into standard (higher flow) occupied mode. When absent, ZPS signals the system into standby (lower flow) unoccupied/setback mode, saving energy and maximizing system diversity.

ZPS200 SERIES MODELS:
- ZPS210: Single unit, powered by 24 VAC/VDC or 18 VDC
- ZPS220: Multiple unit daisy-chain configuration
- ZPS300: Newer series with enhanced detection and USB commissioning

CABLE LENGTH REQUIREMENTS (critical — longer cables cause signal degradation):
- USB cable (laptop to ZPS for commissioning): maximum 10 feet (A-to-B Male-to-Male)
- Signal cable (ZPS to fume hood monitor/FHM): maximum 10 feet, 18 AWG (2 or 3 conductor depending on application)
Exceeding these lengths causes communication loss and unreliable detection

INDICATORS:
- Green LED: power is on
