- Red LED: motion/presence detected (illuminates when motion detected, off when no motion)
- LEDs visible only when ZPS cover is removed

WIRING (ZPS210 — SINGLE UNIT):
- JP3: must be installed (jumper) for single ZPS installation
- TB1 (Terminal Block 1) connections: +18VDC/24VAC, Ground, User Status, Secondary In, ISO Ground
- Signal cable connects ZPS to FHM or fume hood controller

EXPRESS SETUP PROCEDURE (4 steps):
1. Verify proper installation (7-pin connector plugged in, JP3 installed)
2. Verify the detection zone — the ZPS should detect motion in the area in front of the fume hood
3. Verify the setback time — confirm the system enters setback after the programmed delay when no motion is detected; confirm the ZPS detects objects properly (test with slow hand movement in front of sensor)
4. Replace the enclosure cover

SETBACK TIME:
The setback time is the delay between the last detected motion and the system transitioning to unoccupied/setback mode. Typical range: 1-30 minutes. Set conservatively to avoid nuisance setback (operator steps away briefly). Too short = frequent unnecessary setbacks. Too long = delayed energy savings.

DETECTION ZONE:
The ZPS uses passive infrared (PIR) detection. It detects heat signatures in motion. The detection zone is configured during commissioning to cover the area directly in front of the fume hood. If the detection zone is too narrow, the ZPS misses technicians at the sides of the hood. If too wide, it picks up traffic in the corridor and never enters setback.

ADVANCED SETUP (ZPS Commissioning Software):
1. Install ZPS Commissioning Software on laptop
2. Connect USB cable from laptop to ZPS (max 10 ft)
3. Open software, create Job File (database) for the project
4. Add rooms, fume hoods, and ZPS devices to the job file
5. Connect to ZPS via USB
6. Configure detection zone, setback time, sensitivity settings
7. Verify detection area visually in software (shows heat map of detected zones)
8. Save settings and disconnect
9. Apply templates for consistent settings across multiple ZPS units

TROUBLESHOOTING:
- ZPS not entering setback: setback time too long, detection zone too wide, wiring fault, cable too long
- ZPS never exits setback: JP3 not installed (single unit), wiring fault, insufficient motion for detection
- False triggers: detection zone too wide catching corridor traffic, HVAC drafts causing thermal interference, direct sunlight on sensor

topic: X30 Fume Hood Monitor — Complete Parameter Reference, Calibration Modes, CV/Two-Position/VAV (MKT-0012)
content:
X30 FUME HOOD MONITOR — COMPLETE REFERENCE (MKT-0012)

OVERVIEW:
X30 Series Fume Hood Monitors (FHMs) are used on fume hoods with Phoenix Controls valves. Airflow control is achieved with:
- CV (Constant Volume Valve / CVV): maintains constant flow regardless of sash position
- Two-Position Valve (PEV/BEV): switches between two fixed flow rates based on sash position
- VAV (Variable Air Volume): varies flow based on face velocity setpoint and sash position

PARAMETER 1 — OPERATING MODE (MUST SET FIRST):
Parameter 1 sets the operating mode — it must be configured first. All other parameters depend on it.
- Parameter 1 = 1: VAV/face velocity control (sash-responsive, maintains face velocity setpoint)
- Parameter 1 = 2: CV (constant volume — fixed high flow setpoint)
- Parameter 1 = 3: Two-Position (high flow when sash open, low flow when sash closed)
- Parameter 1 = 4: Drive mode (external signal drives valve position)
Without Parameter 1 set, the board is in Er_c (uncommissioned) state and will not operate.

CALIBRATION MODE NAVIGATION:
To enter Calibration Mode: press and hold the UP and DOWN buttons simultaneously for 3+ seconds. The display shows "CAL" and the parameter number.
Navigation:
- Press UP/DOWN to increment/decrement the parameter value
- Press ENTER to save and advance to next parameter
- Press BACK to return to previous parameter without saving
- Hold ENTER to exit calibration mode
The X30 FHM has 23 total calibration parameters (Parameter 1 through Parameter 23). All must be set correctly for the unit to operate per design.

KEY PARAMETERS:
- Parameter 1: Operating mode (1=VAV, 2=CV, 3=Two-Position, 4=Drive)
- Parameter 2: Face velocity setpoint (in fpm) — for VAV mode
- Parameter 3: High flow setpoint (CFM) — for CV or high position of Two-Position
- Parameter 4: Low flow setpoint (CFM) — for low position of Two-Position or minimum flow
- Parameter 5: Setback flow (CFM) — flow when ZPS detects unoccupied
- Parameter 6: Sash open/close threshold (inches) — for two-position mode
- Parameter 7: Alarm flow setpoint — alarm triggers if flow falls below this value
- Parameter 8: Sash sensor calibration — voltage at fully closed sash position
- Parameter 9: Sash sensor calibration — voltage at fully open sash position
- Parameters 10-23: Additional alarm setpoints, network settings, special functions

SASH SENSOR CALIBRATION (Parameters 8 and 9):
Two-point calibration of the sash position sensor:
1. Close sash completely — measure voltage at sash sensor input. Enter as Parameter 8.
2. Open sash fully — measure voltage at sash sensor input. Enter as Parameter 9.
The FHM uses these two endpoints to linearly interpolate all intermediate sash positions.
If sash sensor calibration is wrong: face velocity calculations are wrong, setback triggering is wrong.

FACE VELOCITY DISPLAY vs SETPOINT:
Face velocity is calculated as: Flow CFM ÷ Sash Open Area (sq ft) = Face Velocity (fpm)
Sash open area = Sash Width × Sash Open Height
If the displayed face velocity is consistently wrong: check sash width parameter and sash sensor calibration.

ER_C (UNCOMMISSIONED STATE):
Er_c on the display means the FHM631/X30 monitor is in factory/uncommissioned state. The board requires Parameter 1 to be set before it will operate. This state occurs when: new board installed, factory reset, or EEPROM data lost.
Resolution: enter calibration mode, set Parameter 1 to correct operating mode, then complete all 23 parameters from the Room Schedule Sheet.

topic: RMC300/RMI300 Room Controller Room Integrator — Installation, Wiring, Networking, BACnet Setup (MKT-0364)
content:
RMC300 / RMI300 ROOM CONTROLLER AND ROOM INTEGRATOR — INSTALLATION GUIDE (MKT-0364)

OVERVIEW:
- RMC300 (Room Controller): a Niagara 4 controller that manages one room or zone. Communicates with ACMs over MS/TP, connects to BMS via BACnet/IP or MS/TP.
- RMI300 (Room Integrator): a Niagara 4 server that aggregates data from multiple RMC300s. Acts as a data concentrator and BACnet router.
Both are part of the Vantage platform (older generation before PBC 500 series).

ENVIRONMENTAL REQUIREMENTS:
- Operating temperature: 32-122°F (0-50°C)
- Humidity: 5-95% RH non-condensing
- Mounting: DIN rail (35mm standard) or surface mount

OPTION CARDS AND ACCESSORY MODULES:
The RMC300 base board accepts option cards for additional I/O or communications. Install option cards before mounting:
1. Power off the unit
2. Align the option card with the connector on the base board
3. Press firmly until fully seated
4. Secure with the retention screw

MOUNTING (DIN RAIL):
1. Hook the top of the DIN rail clip over the DIN rail
2. Rotate the unit down until the bottom clip snaps onto the rail
3. Verify unit is firmly secured — cannot be pulled off the rail

WIRING SEQUENCE:
1. Ground the unit first
2. Connect 24VAC/VDC power (follow polarity — GND before HOT)
3. Connect communications wiring (Ethernet for LAN, RS485 for MS/TP)
4. Connect I/O wiring last

POWER WIRING:
- 24VAC power module (included): connect HOT and GND to power terminal. Class 2 transformer required (same as PBC requirement).
- Input voltage range: 24VAC ±10%, 50/60 Hz
- Power consumption varies by option cards installed

COMMUNICATIONS WIRING:
- Ethernet (LAN1, LAN2): standard RJ45, 10/100 Mbps. LAN1 is primary. LAN2 for IT network isolation.
- RS485 (MS/TP): A+, B-, GND/Shield. 120-ohm EOL termination at both ends. Max 63 devices per trunk.
- RS232 (COM1): for local serial connection if needed

STATUS LEDs:
- Power LED: green = power on
- Status LED: various patterns indicate startup, normal operation, fault
- Network LED: activity on Ethernet port

SRAM BACKUP:
RMC/RMI includes SRAM battery backup to retain the date/time and some configuration data during power loss. If SRAM battery depletes, time/date resets on power-up. Replace battery per maintenance schedule.

BACNET/IP SETUP FOR RMC/RMI:
1. Connect Workbench to RMC platform (Fox port 4911)
2. Navigate to Config > Drivers > BacnetNetwork
3. Set Local Device ID (unique Device Instance for this RMC on the entire BACnet internetwork)
4. Configure IP Port: select network adapter, set Network Number
5. Enable IP Port: right-click > Actions > Enable
6. Enable BacnetNetwork: set Enabled = true
7. Discover other BACnet devices on the network
8. Add RMI or BMS devices to the database

topic: Phoenix Controls Workbench Tips — Job Backup, Station Credentials, LON to BACnet Mapping, Verifying BACnet Points
content:
PHOENIX CONTROLS WORKBENCH TIPS AND BACNET VERIFICATION

WORKBENCH JOB BACKUP PROCEDURE:
To transfer files as backup or hand-off to another field service engineer:
1. Copy the job station folder AND ALL RMI/RMC station folders
2. Copy the .bog file for the job station
Both the folder and .bog file are required for a complete backup.

DEFAULT CREDENTIALS:
- Job credentials (used for Workbench station connection): admin / Phoenix123
- Lab Verification credentials: Admin12345 (capital A, numbers)
- Platform daemon: run Platform Daemon, open Workbench, open Local Job, enter platform credentials, then job credentials
Note: Change default credentials immediately on production systems.

LON TO BACNET IP MAPPING:
To map LonWorks points to BACnet IP in Phoenix Controls Point Manager:
1. Configure BACnet/IP settings in the RMI station
2. Assign unique Device Instance that does not conflict with any other BACnet device
3. Save settings — Phoenix Controls Point Manager shows configured BACnet IP mappings
4. Verify in BMS that RMI appears as a BACnet device

LON TO BACNET MS/TP MAPPING:
Important: MSTP address assignment range is 0 to 127. Never assign an MSTP address outside this range (e.g., do NOT use 1001 as an MS/TP address — this caused an error in the station save). The MS/TP address must be a valid value from 0-127.

VERIFYING BACNET POINTS IN NIAGARA WORKBENCH:
Complete procedure for verifying RMI/RMC BACnet communication:
1. Set BACnet Device ID: navigate to {job station}/Config/Drivers/BacnetNetwork/Local Device. Set a unique Device Instance that will not conflict with any other BACnet device on the network.
2. Configure Network Number: expand BACnet Comm > Network > double-click IP Port. Change Network Number to match the BACnet IP network number used by the BMS.
3. Select Network Adapter: expand Link slot, select correct network adapter in the Adapter dropdown. Click Save.
4. Enable IP Port: right-click IP Port > Actions > Enable.
5. Enable BacnetNetwork: right-click BacnetNetwork > View > Property Sheet. Change Enabled from False to True. Click Save.
6. Discover devices: change view to BACnet Device Manager. Click Discover button. In popup, select the network number or check Send Global. Click OK.
7. Add devices: drag discovered RMI/RMC from Device Discovered list to Database pane, or select and click Add.
8. View BACnet points: expand RMI/RMC in System Tree, double-click Points container. If points not visible, click Discover. Add points to database by dragging or clicking Add.

topic: Phoenix Controls Valve Upgrade Kit — Celeris 1, Pneumatic, High-Speed Electric Removal and Installation (MKT-0269)
content:
PHOENIX CONTROLS VALVE UPGRADE KIT INSTALLATION GUIDE (MKT-0269)

OVERVIEW:
The Valve Upgrade Kit replaces legacy valve controllers (Celeris 1, analog pneumatic, high-speed electric) with CSCP electronics while reusing the existing valve body and base channel.

CRITICAL RULE: The entire room must be converted simultaneously. You cannot convert just one or two valves in a room — all valves in the room must be converted at the same time.

SOFTWARE/HARDWARE REQUIREMENTS:
- Phoenix Controls Workbench v5.2.1 or later (MANDATORY — earlier versions will not complete the upgrade)
- One upgrade kit per valve converted
- New room-level communications cabling (MS/TP RS485)
- Appropriate 24VAC transformers for new CSCP devices
- Laptop with Workbench installed

UPGRADE DATABASE BUILD:
Before removing any hardware, build the upgrade database in Workbench:
1. Understand the job site: document all existing valve models, vintages, room assignments, and network configuration
2. Build the upgrade database in Workbench: create the room structure, add each valve, assign MAC addresses (0-63 per PBC trunk), configure zone settings
3. Review wiring and cabling requirements: plan new MS/TP cable routes, wall-mounted sensor locations, reheat actuator connections, fume hood component connections

CABLING REVIEW:
Room-level communications cabling: plan MS/TP (RS485) daisy chain from PBC to each ACM. This is new cable — cannot reuse analog control wiring.
Wall-mounted sensor cabling: temperature, humidity, ZPS connections require new or verified cable runs.
Reheat actuator cabling: check for compatibility with new CSCP outputs.
Fume hood components: FHD500 requires 24VAC power and MS/TP connection.

CELERIS 1 REMOVAL PROCEDURE:
1. Power down valve, disconnect air supply, seal pneumatic line
2. Disconnect all wiring from Celeris 1 controller — document all connections (photograph)
3. Remove and discard pneumatic connection from controller to actuator
4. Dismount Celeris 1 controller from base channel
5. Note pressure switch and hose orientation — photograph before removing
6. Remove pressure switch, hoses, and wiring
7. Disable actuator mechanical clamps, stroke actuator until shaft collar is clear
8. Remove hairpins and clevis pins from linkage
9. Remove actuator from base channel

ANALOG PNEUMATIC REMOVAL:
1. Shut off and bleed instrument air supply
2. Disconnect I/P transducer pneumatic connections (photograph all connections)
3. Disconnect 4-20 mA wiring at MIJ terminal block
4. Remove I/P transducer from mounting
5. Remove pneumatic actuator (follow same procedure as Celeris 1)
6. Vpot removal: remove Vpot cover screw, loosen Vpot-to-pivot-arm coupling, remove Vpot assembly

HIGH-SPEED ELECTRIC REMOVAL:
1. Power down valve electronics
2. Disconnect motor leads, Vpot signal, and network connections
3. Remove electronics enclosure from base channel
4. Remove Vpot assembly (same as above)
5. Do NOT discard Vpot — reuse or replace only if damaged

FLOW CURVE DOWNLOAD (MANDATORY AFTER INSTALLATION):
After installing upgrade controller:
1. Connect laptop with Workbench v5.2.1+ to PBC via Ethernet or Bluetooth
2. For Vintage A/B/C: use Default Curve Import Wizard (standard curve applies)
3. For Vintage D/E: import the specific .VPT characterization file for this valve serial number — DO NOT use default curve for D/E; each valve has a unique characterization
4. Download flow curve to ACM
5. Verify download successful in Workbench
6. Record flow curve file name and serial number on valve upgrade label

topic: Vantage Networking — RMC/RMI Architecture, LON to BACnet, MS/TP Setup, IP Routing for Phoenix Controls
content:
VANTAGE NETWORKING — PHOENIX CONTROLS ARCHITECTURE REFERENCE

VANTAGE SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE:
Vantage is the Phoenix Controls room-level control platform using RMC300 (Room Controller) and RMI300 (Room Integrator) hardware running Niagara 4.
- RMC300: One per zone/room. Connects via MS/TP RS485 to ACMs in the zone. Connects via Ethernet to the RMI300.
- RMI300: One per building section or per IT network segment. Aggregates data from multiple RMC300s. Provides BACnet router function. Connects to BMS via BACnet/IP.

LON (LONWORKS) DEVICES IN VANTAGE:
Legacy Celeris devices communicate via LonWorks (TP/FT-10). The RMC300 can include a LON option card to communicate with legacy Celeris devices. The RMC300 then translates LonWorks data to BACnet for the BMS.

NETWORK HIERARCHY:
Laptop → (Ethernet) → RMI300 (Niagara 4 station) → (Ethernet) → RMC300 (Niagara 4 station) → (RS485 MS/TP or LonWorks) → ACMs/valves

BACNET ROUTING THROUGH VANTAGE:
The RMI300 acts as a BACnet/IP to BACnet MS/TP router. BMS on the BACnet/IP network can communicate with individual ACMs and room-level data points through the RMI300's routing function. Device Instance must be unique across the entire BACnet internetwork including all RMC and RMI devices.

COMMISSIONING SEQUENCE:
1. Commission RMC300s first: configure MS/TP network, add ACMs, verify LON or MS/TP communication with all valves
2. Then commission RMI300: add RMC300s, configure BACnet routing, verify IP connectivity to BMS
3. Verify BACnet points from BMS: use Device Manager to discover RMI/RMC, add points, verify live data
4. Use Workbench Tip #4 or #5 for LON-to-BACnet IP or LON-to-BACnet MS/TP point mapping

DEFAULT IP ADDRESS SCHEME:
- Each RMC300 gets a static IP address on the local network
- RMI300 has two Ethernet ports: one for the IT/BMS network, one for the RMC300 network (isolation)
- If DHCP is used: DHCP reservations based on MAC address are recommended for RMC/RMI — DHCP leases that change break Workbench connections

TROUBLESHOOTING VANTAGE NETWORKING:
- Cannot connect Workbench to RMC: verify IP address, verify Fox port 4911 is open, verify RMC is powered
- BMS cannot see BACnet points: verify RMI BACnet/IP is enabled, Device Instance unique, network number correct, BACnet routing enabled
- RMC cannot see all ACMs: verify MS/TP baud rate, EOL termination, MAC address uniqueness, cable integrity

topic: Niagara 4 Relations — RelationMark, Drag and Drop, Relation Sheet, Wire Sheet, Hierarchy Building
content:
NIAGARA 4 RELATIONS — COMPLETE REFERENCE (Tridium Relations Guide, 2015)

WHAT RELATIONS ARE:
Relations provide metadata used primarily in building hierarchies for logical views of a system based on relationships between components. You can organize the display of components in various meaningful ways — for example, grouping all VAV controllers by the AHU supplying them, regardless of the actual station folder structure.

Relations work with tags: a relation links two components with a RelationId. Tags added to a relation provide additional metadata for more specific filtering when building hierarchies.

ADDING A RELATION USING RELATIONMARK:
1. Select one or more components to mark as the "from" or "to" side of the relation
2. Right-click on the selection → click Relation Mark
3. Select the other side of the relationship (the other component)
4. Right-click → click either "Relate To" or "Relate From" depending on which side was marked first
5. A Relation dialog appears — select the desired RelationId from the pull-down list
6. The list is populated from RelationInfoLists defined in the Tag Dictionaries installed in the TagDictionaryService

ADDING A RELATION USING DRAG AND DROP (Wire Sheet):
1. In Wire Sheet view, click the Workbench Wire Sheet menu → click Show Relations
2. On the desired "relate to" component, click the component's footer and drag to the footer of the desired "relate from" component
3. A Relation dialog appears — select the desired RelationId from the pull-down list
4. In Wire Sheet view, the relation is shown as a dashed line between the two components

EDITING A RELATION FROM RELATION SHEET:
1. Select the desired component and open the Relation Sheet view
2. Right-click on the desired relation row → click Edit
3. In the Edit dialog: change the tag dictionary or tag name in the RelationId, or change the Source ORD

TAGDICTIONARYSERVICE:
The TagDictionaryService holds all tag dictionaries used for Relations and Tagging. The RelationId pull-down list in the Relation dialog is populated from RelationInfoLists defined in the Tag Dictionaries installed in the TagDictionaryService. Both Niagara and Haystack tag dictionaries can be installed here.

topic: Niagara 4 Tagging — TagDictionaryService, Haystack, Ad Hoc Tags, Batch Editor, Tag Groups, Implied Tags, License
content:
NIAGARA 4 TAGGING — COMPLETE REFERENCE (Tridium Tagging Guide, July 2016)

WHAT TAGGING IS AND WHY:
Adding tags to station objects streamlines setting up large systems. Instead of mapping data point by point, tags facilitate the process and improve search results and hierarchical navigation. If you add tags using standard Tag Dictionaries, other applications can discover station content without understanding the naming convention used by the installer. Typical tagging includes: networks, devices, points, control blocks — also mapped to domain-specific entities like buildings, systems, and equipment.

LICENSE REQUIREMENTS:
The tags license is required to use the TagDictionaryService and tag dictionaries. The Dictionary.1limit attribute limits the number of tag dictionaries available. By default, you are limited to the first two tag dictionaries. Dictionaries added above the license limit will be in fault — tags in a faulted dictionary are not available in the Edit Tags dialog.

TAGGING PROCESS:
1. Identify your purpose: enterprise navigation, maintenance views, or end-user navigation
2. Ensure you have the needed dictionaries:
   - Niagara Tag Dictionary: included by default in TagDictionaryService in every new station
   - Haystack Tag Dictionary: add from the haystack palette if needed
   - Ad Hoc tags: create custom tags without a dictionary
3. Add tags to components (one at a time, using Tag Groups, or using Batch Editor)

DICTIONARIES AND SHORTCUTS:
- Search field shortcuts: type "hs:" for Haystack, "n:" for Niagara dictionary — filters the tag list immediately
- Niagara and Haystack Smart Tag Dictionaries are the most common; community-developed Smart Tag Dictionaries may eliminate tagging effort for common equipment types

COMMON TAGGING TASKS:
Creating a tagged device: Right-click on device → Edit Tags → select dictionary → select tag or tag group → Add. You can add multiple tag types to one device.

Adding Ad Hoc tags: Tags not in any installed dictionary. Right-click component → Edit Tags → select "Ad Hoc" → type tag name and value. Ad Hoc tags provide flexibility when standard dictionaries don't cover the needed metadata.

Removing a tag: Right-click component → Edit Tags → select the tag → click Remove.

Adding tags during Discovery: In the Discovered pane (during BACnet or other protocol discovery), you can add tags to discovered devices before adding them to the database. This pre-tags devices at import time.

Adding tags in Database pane: select component in Database pane → right-click → Edit Tags.

TAG GROUPS:
Tag Groups are predefined collections of tags that can all be added with a single Add action. Instead of adding ten tags one at a time to every device of the same type, create a Tag Group with all ten tags and apply the group in one step. Right-click component → Edit Tags → select Tag Group from the pull-down → click Add.

BATCH EDITOR:
The Batch Editor allows adding, modifying, or removing tags across many components at once. Instead of editing each component individually, Batch Editor shows all components in a table and allows mass tag operations. Access via: right-click on a folder → Tag Batch Editor.

IMPLIED TAGS:
Implied tags are tags that are automatically inherited from a parent component or from a tag group assignment. They are not directly added to the component but are inferred from the hierarchy or tag group. View implied tags: Edit Tags dialog (shows implied tags in italics), or use Spy view on the component.

EXPORTING AND IMPORTING TAG DICTIONARIES:
Tag dictionaries can be exported as .xml files and imported into other stations. This allows consistent tagging across multiple jobs without recreating dictionaries from scratch.

topic: NiagaraAX to Niagara 4 Migration — Platform Compatibility, JACE Support, Resource Requirements, Workflow
content:
NIAGARAAX TO NIAGARA 4 MIGRATION — COMPLETE REFERENCE (Tridium AX to N4 Migration Guide, May 2016)

WHY MIGRATION IS NEEDED:
NiagaraAX (AX) and Niagara 4 (N4) are different platforms. After installing N4 Workbench, you cannot simply use stations saved in NiagaraAX format. Migration converts AX artifacts (.bog files, Px files) to N4 format using the N4 migration tool.

PLATFORM COMPATIBILITY — WHICH JACE CONTROLLERS MIGRATE:
CANNOT migrate to N4 (not supported):
- JACE-2 (NPM2)
- JACE-202 (NPM2)
- JACE-403
- JACE-545
These platforms are too old and lack the resources for N4. They can remain as AX stations under the NiagaraNetwork of an N4 Supervisor (data exchange is still possible).

CAN migrate to N4 (supported):
- JACE-NXT
- JACE-NXS
- SoftJACE
- Any supported PC/server

PLATFORM RESOURCE REQUIREMENTS FOR MIGRATION:
N4 requires MORE disk space, MORE Java heap, and MORE system RAM than an equivalent AX station:
- Disk space: N4 requires 14-16 MB more than AX (due to Java 8 VM vs Java 5, and new UX features)
- Java heap: N4 requires 10 MB more baseline heap than equivalent AX station
- History RAM disk (JACE-6 only): maximum reduced from 64MB to 32MB in N4 to free system memory

Minimum free resources before migration (approximate):
- JACE-NXT/NXS: verify minimum 24 MB free disk space, 29 MB free heap
- Run the AX Resource Checker BOG file before migrating to verify platform meets minimum requirements

MIGRATION WORKFLOW:
1. Upgrade AX station to AX-3.8 first (required baseline for migration tool)
2. Verify system operates correctly at AX-3.8 (communications, histories, alarms working)
3. Make complete station backups (.bog files) from each controller
4. Run the N4 migration tool on each station backup — converts .bog to N4 format
5. Install N4 on the controllers (upgrade firmware)
6. Restore migrated station to each controller
7. Perform migrated database cleanup (fix any components that don't convert cleanly)
8. Preliminary N4 station checkout (verify operation matches pre-migration)
9. System verification (check histories, alarms, BACnet communication, graphics)

WIRE COMPATIBILITY:
Wire compatibility means AX and N4 controllers can coexist on the same NiagaraNetwork and communicate with each other during a phased migration. A migrated N4 Supervisor can still communicate with AX JACE controllers that haven't been migrated yet. This allows phased migration instead of requiring all controllers to be upgraded simultaneously.

CONFIGURING FOR USER MIGRATION:
Before migrating users from AX to N4: a configuration flag must be set in the station to properly migrate user accounts and credentials. Failure to set this flag before migration may result in user accounts not transferring correctly.

FILE LOCATIONS IN N4:
N4 stores station files in different locations than AX. Key paths:
- Station home: varies by platform type and supervisor vs JACE
- Protected station home: corrected path noted in May 2016 update — verify against current N4 Platform Guide

AX END OF LIFE:
NiagaraAX reached End of Life April 30, 2020. No new AX licenses are available. All new systems must use Niagara 4. Existing AX systems should plan migration.

topic: Niagara 4 bFormat Strings — %out.value%, %displayName%, %parent.name%, %user()%, Dynamic Labels
content:
NIAGARA 4 BFORMAT STRINGS — COMPLETE REFERENCE (Niagara 4 Certification Training)

bFormat strings are used in Niagara 4 Px graphics and labels to create dynamic text that displays live values and component information. Text inside % signs is dynamic; text outside % signs is static.

BASIC VALUE AND STATUS:
%out.value%       — Value of the Out slot (the component's output)
%out.status%      — Status of the Out slot (ok, stale, fault, etc.)
%out.value% %out.status%  — Both value and status together (two bFormats with a space between)

TIME ELAPSED:
%Hours%           — Total elapsed time in hours (use for burn time, runtime counters)
%Minutes%         — Total elapsed time in minutes
IMPORTANT: Use either %Hours% OR %Minutes% — NOT BOTH in the same label, since both display total elapsed time (not hours + remaining minutes).
Example: "Burn Time = %Hours% HRS" displays as "Burn Time = 568 HRS"

COMPONENT NAMING:
%displayName%           — Display name of the slot/component
%parent.name%           — Name of the parent component (one level up in the hierarchy)
%parent.parent.name%    — Name of the parent's parent (two levels up)
Example: "%parent.name% > %displayName%" for a control relay inside LightingSystem folder displays as "LightingSystem > ControlRelay"

ANY PROPERTY:
%property.value%        — Value of any component property (replace "property" with actual slot name)
%anyproperty.value%     — Value of any specific named property slot
%high.value% / %low.value%  — Example combining two property values with static text between them

CURRENT USER:
%user()%               — Currently logged in Niagara user name. Useful for audit displays and user-specific panels.

COMBINING BFORMATS:
Multiple bFormat expressions can be strung together separated by spaces:
"%high.value% / %low.value% >> %weatherSummary%: %precipChance%" 
displays as: "65 F / 44 F >> Rain Likely: 67.0%"

ADDING STATIC TEXT:
All text outside the % delimiters is static and always displayed. Insert ANY text before or after % to provide labeling without needing a separate DisplayName label.
Example: "Text = %out.value%" where "Text = " is static and %out.value% is dynamic.

USE IN PX GRAPHICS:
Apply bFormat strings to Label widgets, BoundLabel, or any widget that accepts a format string in its properties. This eliminates the need for separate static label components next to dynamic value displays.

topic: LonWorks Platform for Niagara 4 — Neuron Chip, LonTalk, SNVT, TP/FT-10, Celeris Integration Context
content:
LONWORKS PLATFORM — REFERENCE FOR NIAGARA 4 AND PHOENIX CONTROLS INTEGRATION

WHAT LONWORKS IS:
LonWorks (by Echelon Corporation) is a building automation communications technology using the proprietary LonTalk protocol. Unlike BACnet (an open standard), LonWorks is proprietary. LonWorks uses Echelon Neuron Chip application processors in devices. Phoenix Controls Celeris systems use LonWorks (TP/FT-10 free topology or TP-78 bus).

LONTALK PROTOCOL KEY CONCEPTS:
Network Variables (NVs): LonWorks uses Network Variables to exchange data between devices. Each device publishes input and output network variables. Connecting an output NV of one device to the input NV of another creates a binding — data flows automatically.
Standard Network Variable Types (SNVTs): Standard data types that allow interoperability between devices from different manufacturers. SNVT_flow, SNVT_temp, SNVT_switch are examples. Using SNVTs means devices from different vendors can exchange data without custom programming.
Message Types: explicit (addressed to specific device), implicit (broadcast), acknowledged, unacknowledged. For critical data use acknowledged messages.
Addressing: LonWorks addresses devices by Domain, Subnet, and Node ID. The Neuron ID (48-bit hardware address factory-programmed into each Neuron chip) uniquely identifies each device. Configuration uses the Neuron ID for network commissioning.

THE NEURON CHIP:
Every LonWorks device contains an Echelon Neuron chip. The Neuron chip handles both the LonTalk communications protocol AND application logic. The Neuron chip runs two communication microprocessors (handling the LonTalk protocol) and one application microprocessor (running device-specific logic). This architecture means the LonWorks protocol is implemented in hardware/firmware — not in software like BACnet.

CHANNEL TYPES — CRITICAL FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS:
TP/FT-10 (Free Topology): most common for building automation. Free topology means devices can be wired in bus, star, ring, or any combination. Uses twisted pair at 78 kbps. Maximum total cable: 500m (1640 ft) without repeater. Termination: two termination capacitors (one at each end of the channel for bus topology).
TP-78 (Bus Topology): older bus-only topology. Devices must connect in daisy chain. 78 kbps.
Both are used in Phoenix Controls Celeris installations.

LONMARK INTEROPERABILITY:
LonMark International defines guidelines for LonWorks interoperability. A LonMark Certified device implements standard SNVTs and functional profiles, allowing it to work with other LonMark devices from any manufacturer. Celeris LVC controllers are LonMark certified.

BACNET VS LONWORKS FOR PHOENIX CONTROLS:
- BACnet: open ANSI/ASHRAE/ISO standard. Native to CSCP/PBC 500 series. Supported by virtually all BMS vendors.
- LonWorks: proprietary (Echelon). Used in legacy Celeris systems. Requires a gateway (PCI8000 or RMC300 with LON option card) to bridge LonWorks to BACnet for BMS integration.
- The debate between BACnet and LonWorks has been resolved in the industry — BACnet has become the dominant open standard, while LonWorks remains in legacy installations.
- For Phoenix Controls: CSCP (new installations) uses BACnet/MS/TP. Celeris (legacy) uses LonWorks. Migration from Celeris to CSCP eliminates the LonWorks dependency.

NIAGARA 4 LONWORKS INTEGRATION:
Niagara 4 connects to LonWorks networks via a LON option card in the RMC300 or via a PCI8000 gateway. The Niagara LonWorks driver (OpenLDV or similar) manages communication with the LonWorks channel. Discovered LonWorks devices appear as proxy points in the Niagara station, same as BACnet devices. Use Accel-Works for Celeris 1, LonMaker for Celeris 2 — NEVER use LonMaker on Celeris 1.

topic: Niagara Web Start — JNLP, Java Web Start, Workbench Outside Browser, AX-3.8U1, Multi-Station Navigation
content:
NIAGARA WEB START — REFERENCE (Tridium Niagara Web Start Guide, April 2016)

WHAT WEB START IS:
Java Web Start is an Oracle technology (part of the Java Runtime Environment) that allows Workbench to run OUTSIDE the browser. NiagaraAX-3.8U1 introduced Web Start as an alternative to the Java browser applet. The key difference: it removes the need for the browser Java plugin (NPAPI), which many modern browsers disabled.

HOW IT WORKS:
1. Navigate to the station login page in a browser
2. Click "To connect using Java Web Start click here" link on the login page
3. Browser downloads a JNLP file
4. Java Web Start launches the Niagara Workbench application outside the browser
5. Once downloaded, the JNLP file can launch Workbench on subsequent occasions without needing the browser again
6. The browser can be closed or navigated away — the Workbench application continues running independently

REQUIREMENTS:
- Java 8 JRE minimum (Web Start checks for Java 8 but does NOT check the update release version)
- Works on AX-3.8U1 — Note: this is an AX feature, not native N4. Provided for reference when working with legacy AX systems.

ADVANTAGES OVER BROWSER APPLET:
- Overcomes NPAPI browser compatibility problems (Chrome, Firefox disabled Java NPAPI support)
- Runs as a full desktop application (not constrained by browser sandbox)
- Saves JNLP file locally for repeated use without browser
- Can be branded with OEM icons and shortcut text

CONFIGURATION:
Web Start Config is an added slot in the Web Services Property Sheet (AX-3.8U1).
Properties allow:
- Enabling/disabling application shortcuts (desktop and Start menu)
- Modifying default shortcut text
- Branding: OEM distributors configure brand.properties with their brand defaults; integrators can modify shortcut text for end users

MULTI-STATION NAVIGATION:
Web Start supports connecting to multiple stations without launching separate browser instances. This is useful when an integrator manages several JACE controllers — all can be monitored in a single Workbench session launched via Web Start.

LIMITATIONS:
- Web Start application cannot open certain file types that the browser-based applet can
- Requires Java 8 JRE on client PC
- Some network/firewall configurations may block JNLP file download

RELEVANCE FOR N4:
N4 has moved beyond the Web Start approach — N4 uses HTML5 Hx graphics that run natively in modern browsers without any Java plugin. Web Start knowledge is useful when maintaining legacy AX-3.8 systems or troubleshooting older installations.
